name: Banload
description: Banload is a dangerous Trojan malware family known for distributing other infections, particularly banking trojans. Its presence can lead to privacy issues and financial loss.
additional_info:
Banload (also known as TrojanDownloader:Win32/Banload) is a family of malware-distribution trojans. If installed, trojans from this family inject systems with other computer infections, most of which are categorized as banking trojans that collect user-system information (anti-virus suites typically detect these injected infections as "Win32/Banker"). The presence of Banload (and its payload) on your system can lead to serious privacy issues and significant financial loss.
[]()

name: Basdoor
description: Backdoors allow unauthorized users to bypass security measures and gain high-level access to computer systems. Cybercriminals can then steal data, install malware, and hijack devices.
additional_info:
In the world of cybersecurity, a backdoor refers to any method by which authorized and unauthorized users are able to get around normal security measures and gain high level user access (aka root access) on a computer system, network, or software application. Once they're in, cybercriminals can use a backdoor to steal personal and financial data, install additional malware, and hijack devices.
[Malwarebytes] (https://www.malwarebytes.com/backdoor/)

threat.name: Basdoor - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
Basdoor - MITRE - System Network Configuration Discovery

name: Base64 Encoded Executable
description: Obfuscation conceals code making it hard to read. Base64 encodes data to ASCII, used by attackers to send malware over HTTP. Base64 allows 64 characters for encoding.
additional_info:
Obfuscation is the most commonly used technique to conceal the original code written by the programmer, rendering the executable code difficult to read and hard to understand while maintaining the functionality of the written code.

Base64 is a simple malware obfuscation technique. The very reason why Base64 encoding is used is because using Base64 it is possible to encode binary data to ASCII string format. Thus, attackers encode data in base64 format and send it over HTTP Protocol. Base64 allows only 64 characters for encoding, hence the name.[Infosec Institute](https://resources.infosecinstitute.com/topics/malware-analysis/using-base64-for-malware-obfuscation/)

threat.name: Base64 Encoded Executable - MITRE - Data Encoding - Standard Encoding

name: BatLoader
description: Loaders are malicious tools used by cybercriminals to grab executables from a server, with some having additional features like a user-friendly UI or self-healing capabilities. They are basic remote access Trojans evolving to bypass security.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: BaydenRAT
description: Remote Access Trojans are programs that allow covert surveillance or unauthorized access to a victim PC. They collect keystrokes, passwords, screenshots, and more, providing attackers with remote control of the victim machine.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.

name: BazaLoader
description: New phishing attacks reveal a new malware named BazarBackdoor by TrickBot trojan developers, deploying a network-compromising toolkit for threat actors. Code similarities suggest TrickBot gang's involvement. [Source: Bleepingcomputer]
additional_info:
In new phishing attacks discovered over the past two weeks, a new malware named 'BazarBackdoor', or internally by the malware developers as simply "backdoor", is being installed that deploys a network-compromising toolkit for the threat actors.
The developers of the infamous TrickBot trojan are believed to be behind this new backdoor due to code similarities, executable crypters, and its infrastructure.
[Bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/bazarbackdoor-trickbot-gang-s-new-stealthy-network-hacking-malware/)

name: BeamWinHTTP
description: Trojan.BeamWinHTTP is a malicious dll Trojan that injects code into http traffic, adding systems to a botnet for DDoS attacks. Detected by Malwarebytes.
additional_info:
Trojan.BeamWinHTTP is a Trojan that comes in the form of a dll, which is loaded to inject malicious code into http traffic on the affected system. Trojan.BeamWinHTTP has been found to add affected systems to a botnet that performed DDoS attacks.
[Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/blog/detections/trojan-beamwinhttp)

name: BeeShell
description: A Trojan horse is a type of malware that deceives users about its true intent, often spread through social engineering and can allow attackers to access personal information or control the infected computer.
additional_info:
In computing, a Trojan horse (or simply trojan) is any malware which misleads users of its true intent. The term is derived from the Ancient Greek story of the deceptive Trojan Horse that led to the fall of the city of Troy.

Trojans are generally spread by some form of social engineering, for example where a user is duped into executing an email attachment disguised to appear not suspicious, (e.g., a routine form to be filled in), or by clicking on some fake advertisement on social media or anywhere else. Although their payload can be anything, many modern forms act as a backdoor, contacting a controller which can then have unauthorized access to the affected computer. Trojans may allow an attacker to access users' personal information such as banking information, passwords, or personal identity. It can also delete a user's files or infect other devices connected to the network. Ransomware attacks are often carried out using a trojan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trojan_horse_(computing))

name: BellaCiao
description: BellaCiao is dropper malware that communicates with a DNS server controlled by the attacker to deliver additional malware payloads through hard-coded instructions rather than typical downloads. Group responsible is Charming Kitten.
additional_info:
BellaCiao is classified as dropper malware, which means it is intended to distribute other malware payloads onto a victim's computer system according to instructions from a C2 server. The payload delivered by BellaCiao is not downloaded but rather hardcoded into the executable as malformed base64 strings and then unloaded when needed.

BellaCiao communicates with a DNS server controlled by the attacker, which sends malicious hard-coded instructions via an IP address that imitates the victim's actual IP address. This leads to the deployment of additional malware through hard-coded instructions rather than through a typical download.

The attack chain may result in the installation of a web shell that allows for arbitrary file uploads and downloads, as well as command execution, depending on the resolved IP address. In the security industry, the group responsible for the BellaCiao malware is recognized as Charming Kitten.

[pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/26598-bellaciao-malware)

name: Betabot
description: Betabot is a highly advanced infostealer malware that started as a banking Trojan in 2012 but has evolved to include features allowing its operators to control victims' machines and steal data.
additional_info:
Cybereason concludes that Betabot is a sophisticated infostealer malware that’s evolved significantly since it first appeared in late 2012. The malware began as a banking Trojan and is now packed with features that allow its operators to practically take over a victim’s machine and steal sensitive information.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.betabot)

name: Bifrose
description: Bifrose is a family of Remote Administration Tools (RATs) used by cyber criminals to take control of infected computers, allowing them to perform various actions to generate revenue.
additional_info:
Bifrose is a family of Remote Administration Tools (RATs). Cyber criminals seek to infect computers with programs of this type to take control over them and perform specific actions, which allow them to generate revenue.

Research shows that Bifrose's RATs can be used to manage files, processes, log keystrokes, take screenshots, access webcams, execute commands, edit the registry and steal passwords.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/17246-bifrose-malware)

threat.name: Bifrose - MITRE - Exfiltration Over C2 Channel

name: Bingoml
description: Trojans are malware disguised as real software, used by cyber-criminals to steal data and gain unauthorized access to systems. Users are tricked into loading and executing them through social engineering tactics.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: BisonBooster
description: Iranian state-backed hackers shared a harmful Word document with CERTFA Lab, targeting MEK members or affiliates. The Trojan has been used since June 13, 2023, threatening cybersecurity.
additional_info:

Evidence indicates that, at least since 13 June 2023, this Trojan has been utilized to target members of the Mojahedin-e-Khalq (MEK) or individuals associated with this organization.

name: BlackCat
description: ALPHV, also known as BlackCat, is a ransomware family deployed as part of RaaS operations. It targets Windows, Linux-based OS, and VMWare ESXi, encrypting files with AES or ChaCha20 algorithms. It can self-propagate and delete volume shadow copies.
additional_info:
ALPHV, also known as BlackCat or Noberus, is a ransomware family that is deployed as part of Ransomware as a Service (RaaS) operations. ALPHV is written in the Rust programming language and supports execution on Windows, Linux-based operating systems (Debian, Ubuntu, ReadyNAS, Synology), and VMWare ESXi. ALPHV is marketed as ALPHV on cybercrime forums, but is commonly called BlackCat by security researchers due to an icon of a black cat appearing on its leak site. ALPHV has been observed being deployed in ransomware attacks since November 18, 2021.

ALPHV can be configured to encrypt files using either the AES or ChaCha20 algorithms. In order to maximize the amount of ransomed data, ALPHV can delete volume shadow copies, stop processes and services, and stop virtual machines on ESXi servers. ALPHV can self-propagate by using PsExec to remote execute itself on other hosts on the local network.
(Malpedia)[https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.blackcat]

name: BlackLotus
description: BlackLotus is a stealthy UEFI bootkit that bypasses Secure Boot defenses using CVE-2022-21894, making it a significant threat. Microsoft patched the vulnerability in January 2022.
additional_info:
A stealthy Unified Extensible Firmware Interface (UEFI) bootkit called BlackLotus has become the first publicly known malware capable of bypassing Secure Boot defenses, making it a potent threat in the cyber landscape.

BlackLotus, in a nutshell, exploits a security flaw tracked as CVE-2022-21894 (aka Baton Drop) to get around UEFI Secure Boot protections and set up persistence. The vulnerability was addressed by Microsoft as part of its January 2022 Patch Tuesday update.
[Thehackernews](https://thehackernews.com/2023/03/blacklotus-becomes-first-uefi-bootkit.html)

name: BlackMatter
description: BlackMatter is a new ransomware affiliate program launched in July 2021, claiming to combine the best features of DarkSide, REvil, and LockBit. [RecordedFuture]
additional_info:
BlackMatter is a new ransomware-as-service (RaaS) affiliate program that was founded in July 2021. According to BlackMatter, “The project has incorporated in itself the best features of DarkSide, REvil, and LockBit”.
[RecordedFuture](https://www.recordedfuture.com/blackmatter-ransomware-successor-darkside-revil/)

threat.name: BlackMatter - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
BlackMatter - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
BlackMatter - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: BlackRain
description: A Trojan horse is malware that deceives users about its true intent. It is often spread through social engineering and can result in unauthorized access to personal information or the deletion of files.
additional_info:
In computing, a Trojan horse (or simply trojan) is any malware which misleads users of its true intent. The term is derived from the Ancient Greek story of the deceptive Trojan Horse that led to the fall of the city of Troy.

Trojans are generally spread by some form of social engineering, for example where a user is duped into executing an email attachment disguised to appear not suspicious, (e.g., a routine form to be filled in), or by clicking on some fake advertisement on social media or anywhere else. Although their payload can be anything, many modern forms act as a backdoor, contacting a controller which can then have unauthorized access to the affected computer. Trojans may allow an attacker to access users' personal information such as banking information, passwords, or personal identity. It can also delete a user's files or infect other devices connected to the network. Ransomware attacks are often carried out using a trojan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trojan_horse_(computing))

name: BlackSun
description: BlackSun ransomware encrypts files, renames them with a ".BlackSun" extension, changes the desktop wallpaper, and creates a "BlackSun_README.txt" file until a ransom is paid.
additional_info:
BlackSun encrypts files and keeps them inaccessible until a ransom is paid. It renames encrypted files by appending the ".BlackSun" extension (for example, it renames "1.jpg" to "1.jpg.BlackSun", "2.jpg" to "1.jpg.BlackSun"). Also, BlackSun changes the desktop wallpaper and creates the "BlackSun_README.txt" file.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/21909-blacksun-ransomware)

threat.name: BlackSun - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
BlackSun - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
BlackSun - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: BlackTDS
description: Proofpoint researchers have discovered a new Traffic Distribution System called BlackTDS, which offers a "Cloud TDS" that can distribute malware while avoiding detection by researchers and sandboxes.
additional_info:
Proofpoint researchers have been tracking a new Traffic Distribution System called BlackTDS implicated in the distribution of a variety of malware. BlackTDS is a multitenant TDS tool that has been advertising its services on underground markets since the end of December 2017.

BlackTDS offers a variety of services to its clients that they collectively refer to as a “Cloud TDS.” The operators claim that their Cloud TDS can handle social engineering and  redirection to exploit kits (EKs) while preventing detection by bots -- namely researchers and sandoxes. BlackTDS also includes access to fresh domains with clean reputations over HTTPS if required.
[Proofpoint](https://www.proofpoint.com/us/threat-insight/post/drive-service-blacktds)

threat.name: BlackTDS - MITRE - Exploitation for Client Execution
BlackTDS - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious Link

name: BlackTech
description: BlackTech is a cyber espionage group targeting East Asia, with a focus on Taiwan but also involving Japan and Hong Kong. For more information, visit the MITRE website.
additional_info:
BlackTech is a cyber espionage group operating against targets in East Asia, particularly Taiwan, and occasionally, Japan and Hong Kong.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0098/)

name: Blacklegion
description: BlackLegion ransomware encrypts files, adds ".BlackLegion" extension, and creates a ransom note ("DecryptNote.txt"). Victims must decrypt files to regain access.
additional_info:
BlackLegion is ransomware that restricts access to files by encrypting them. Victims cannot open encrypted files without decrypting them. Additionally, BlackLegion creates a ransom note ("DecryptNote.txt") and renames files by appending a string of random characters, an email address, and the ".BlackLegion" extension.
[PCRisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/28459-blacklegion-ransomware)

name: Blackmagic
description: Ransomware is a type of malware that blocks system access and demands payment to restore it. Early versions demanded payment via snail mail, while modern ones use cryptocurrency or credit card.
additional_info:
Ransom malware, or ransomware, is a type of malware that prevents users from accessing their system or personal files and demands ransom payment in order to regain access. The earliest variants of ransomware were developed in the late 1980s, and payment was to be sent via snail mail. Today, ransomware authors order that payment be sent via cryptocurrency or credit card.
[Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/ransomware/)

threat.name: Blackmagic - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Blackmagic - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Blackmagic - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Blackmoon
description: The Blackmoon banking Trojan, first analyzed in 2014, targets online banking credentials by modifying the Hosts file to redirect domain lookups. It primarily targets South Korean online banking customers via drive-by download.
additional_info:
First analyzed in early 2014 [1] [2], the Blackmoon banking Trojan targets a user’s online banking credentials using a type of pharming that involves modifying or replacing the local Hosts file with one that redirects online banking domain lookups to an IP address controlled by the attacker. Blackmoon has been observed targeting primarily customers of South Korean online banking sites and services, and is usually distributed via drive-by download.
[Proofpoint](https://www.proofpoint.com/us/threat-insight/post/Updated-Blackmoon-Banking-Trojan)

threat.name: Blackmoon - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter

name: Blacknix
description: BlackNix is a high-risk Remote Access Trojan used by cyber criminals to gain control over devices, causing serious issues.
additional_info:
BlackNix is a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) that allows cyber criminals to gain access and control over infected devices. BlackNix is high-risk malware with malicious capabilities, which can cause serious issues.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/17245-blacknix-rat)

name: Blackrota
description: Blackrota backdoor for Linux is in ELF format, supporting x86/x86-64 CPUs. It is based on geacon, a CobaltStrike Beacon in Go language, allowing control of compromised hosts with limited features.
additional_info:
The Blackrota backdoor is currently only available for Linux, in ELF file format, and supports both x86/x86-64 CPU architectures. Blackrota is configured and compiled based on geacon, a CobaltStrike Beacon implemented in the Go language, which can be used as a CobalStrike Beacon that interacts with CobaltStrike to control compromised hosts…However, it only implements some of the key functions in the original CobaltStrike Beacon
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/internet-threat-news/19546-blackrota-dockers-newest-malware)

name: Blister Loader
description: Blister is a loader that carries a malicious payload and has been associated with Evil Corp in the past. Recent developments and past payloads are explored in detail.
additional_info:
Blister is a loader that loads a payload embedded inside it and in the past was observed with activity linked to Evil Corp.
[Foxit](https://blog.fox-it.com/2023/11/01/popping-blisters-for-research-an-overview-of-past-payloads-and-exploring-recent-developments/)

name: Blocker
description: Trojan-Ransom.Win32.Blocker takes control of computer by blocking startup routine, displays window asking user to send SMS for deactivation code to unlock access to computer.
additional_info:
After Trojan-Ransom.Win32.Blocker is installed on a computer, this Trojan adds itself to the computer’s startup routine and blocks the operating system from loading normally. When the operating system is started, Trojan-Ransom.Win32.Blocker takes control of the computer and displays a window asking the user to send an SMS message with special text to the indicated short number. In return, the window states, the user will receive a code for deactivating the malware and unlocking access to the computer.
[Kaspersky](https://threats.kaspersky.com/en/threat/Trojan-Ransom.Win32.Blocker/)

threat.name: Blocker - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Blocker - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Blocker - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Blue Bot
description: Botnets are malicious bots that carry out various harmful activities like downloading malware, receiving and relaying commands, keylogging, DDoS attacks, and encrypting data for ransom.
additional_info:
This botnet is a type of malware bot that may perform many malicious tasks, such as downloading and executing additional malware, receiving commands from a control server and relaying specific information and telemetry back to the control server, updating or deleting itself, stealing login and password information, logging keystrokes, participating in a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack, or locking and encrypting the contents of your computer and demanding payment for its safe return.
[Fortiguard](https://www.fortiguard.com/encyclopedia/botnet/7630327)

name: Blue Eagle
description: Cross platform RAT tool for Windows, Linux, Mac, and Android, built in VB.net. It creates trojans and controls victims on the same port simultaneously. Free on Github, user assumes responsibility.
additional_info:
This is a cross platform RAT tool [Android RAT] [Windows RAT] [Linux RAT] [MAC RAT] which is programmed in VB.net to be a user friendly and easy to use and builds out trojans (.exe , .apk & .jar) and controls the victims running those trojans on same port at same time , and this tool is now registerd to be free , and on the user responsibility
[Github](https://github.com/SaherBlueEagle/XPR-2020-Free)

threat.name: Blue Eagle - MITRE - Command and Control

name: Boldflank
description: Loaders grab malware from attacker server, evolve with features like UI and self-healing. Serve as remote access Trojans for attackers to control compromised computers, maintaining viability for cybercriminals.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: Boogr
description: Malware disguised as games or apps can download malicious files, send premium-rate SMS messages, or link the victim's phone to the attacker's server, posing security risks.
additional_info: Disguised as a game or popular application, malware of this family can download other malicious files, send SMS messages to premium-rate numbers, or connect the victim’s smartphone to the attacker’s command-and-control server.

name: Bookworm Trojan
description: Unit 42 discovered Bookworm, a Trojan initially believed to be a variant of PlugX RAT. However, it has a unique modular architecture, primarily stealing keystrokes and expanding capabilities through C2 server-loaded modules.
additional_info:
Recently, while researching attacks on targets in Thailand, Unit 42 discovered a tool that initially appeared to be a variant of the well-known PlugX RAT based on similar observed behavior such as the usage of DLL side-loading and a shellcode file. After closer inspection, it appears to be a completely distinct Trojan.

Bookworm’s functional code is radically different from PlugX and has a rather unique modular architecture that warranted additional analysis by Unit 42. Bookworm has little malicious functionality built-in, with its only core ability involving stealing keystrokes and clipboard contents. However, Bookworm expands on its capabilities through its ability to load additional modules directly from its command and control (C2) server. 

[Palo Alto Networks](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/bookworm-trojan-a-model-of-modular-architecture/)

name: BoostBeast
description: Trojans are malware disguised as real software used by hackers to steal data and gain access to systems. Users are tricked into downloading and activating them, leading to potential spying and data theft.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: Borat RAT
description: The Borat RAT is a malware that includes a binary builder, supporting modules, and server certificates. It combines RAT, Spyware, and ransomware capabilities, such as keylogging and ransomware attacks.
additional_info:
The Borat RAT comes bundled with its components (e.g. binary builder, supporting modules, server certificates). According to Cyble this malware is an unique combination of RAT, Spyware, and ransomware.
The supporting modules are included; a few of the capabilities: Keylogger, Ransomware, Audio/Webcam Recording, Process Hollowing, Browser Credential/Discord Token Stealing, etc.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.boratrat)

name: Borr
description: Borr is a malicious program sold on hacker forums for $100 per month, then $50/month. Cyber criminals use Borr to steal sensitive information for financial gain.
additional_info:
Borr is the name of a malicious program which can be purchased from hacker forums. The cost is equivalent to $100 for the first month, after which the monthly cost becomes $50. Cyber criminals use Borr to steal various sensitive information, which they can misuse to generate revenue in various ways.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/17081-borr-malware)

name: BotLoader
description: Loaders are basic remote access Trojans allowing attackers to control compromised computers. Evolving loaders may have user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, and act as retail shops for botmaster to sell bots.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: Botnet Generic
description: A botnet is a network of compromised devices controlled by a central server to carry out cyber attacks, steal data, send spam, and grant unauthorized access to devices.
additional_info:
A botnet is a number of Internet-connected devices, each of which is running one or more bots. Botnets can be used to perform Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks, steal data, send spam, and allows the attacker to access the device and its connection. The owner can control the botnet using command and control (C&C) software. The word "botnet" is a portmanteau of the words "robot" and "network". The term is usually used with a negative or malicious connotation.    

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Botnet)

name: BottleEK
description: On December 11, 2019, a new exploit kit called BottleEK targeting Japanese users was discovered during a stroll through ad networks, alongside other known kits like RIG and Fallout.
additional_info:
On December 11, 2019, we were strolling through ad-networks. As before, we observed RIG, Fallout and Underminer Exploit Kit, but observed other interesting Drive-by Download attack. We call it “Bottle Exploit Kit”. BottleEK targets only Japanese users. According to our research, BottleEK has been active at least in September 2019. This time we introduce BottleEK.
[Naosec](https://nao-sec.org/2019/12/say-hello-to-bottle-exploit-kit.html)

threat.name: BottleEK - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious Link
BottleEK - MITRE - Exploitation for Client Execution

name: Brambul
description: Brambul is an SMB worm that decrypts and self-propagates between computers.
additional_info:
Brambul is an SMB protocol computer worm that decrypts and automatically moves from one computer to its second computer.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brambul)

name: Bray
description: This malware steals user names and passwords for online banking services and attempts to collect bank card information such as cardholder name, card number, CVV, and expiration date.
additional_info:
Malware of this family steals user names and passwords used for online banking services, and also attempts to obtain data about the user’s bank card (cardholder name, card number, CVV, and expiration date).
[Kaspersky](https://threats.kaspersky.com/en/threat/Trojan-Banker.AndroidOS.Agent/)

threat.name: Bray - MITRE - Protected User Data: SMS Messages

name: Brosql
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data like online banking credentials or passwords from infected computers. They use various methods like browser hooking, web injections, and keylogging, often as part of botnets.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: BrowserPassview
description: HackTool:Win32/BrowserPassview can extract passwords from browser cache, posing a risk of personal information theft by malware. Microsoft warns of potential security threats.
additional_info:
This hacktool can be used to retrieve the passwords stored in your web browser cache. Malware can also use this tool to steal your personal information.
[Microsoft](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/wdsi/threats/malware-encyclopedia-description?Name=HackTool:Win32/BrowserPassview)

threat.name: BrowserPassview - MITRE - Credentials from Password Stores

name: Brunhilda
description: Brunhilda is a dropper service using the Google Play Store for malware distribution. Cybercrime groups shifting towards DaaS for quick monetization and maintaining a low profile.
additional_info:
Brunhilda is a dropper service that utilizes the Google Play Store to distribute various malware. While cybercrime groups tend to start operating as MaaS businesses, currently there is an upward trend of DaaS (Dropper as a Service) variations.

Applications used to distribute malware are analyzed in detail. Cybercrime groups started developing DaaS platforms to quickly monetize their business, as it is easy to replace the distributed malware while maintaining a low profile.
[Prodaft](https://resources.prodaft.com/brunhilda-daas-malware-report)

name: BrushaLoader
description: BrushaLoader is a downloader used by threat actors to profile infected PCs and load more robust payloads. It enables threat actors to stay under the radar and can deliver disruptive infections.
additional_info:
BrushaLoader is one of a growing group of downloaders frequently employed by threat actors to profile infected PCs and then load more robust payloads on devices of interest. Malware like BrushaLoader contributes to the ongoing trend of “quality over quantity” infections and enables threat actors to better stay under the radar than they can with highly disruptive infections like ransomware or when distributing massive malicious spam campaigns with high-profile malware as their primary payload. At the same time, these loaders can also deliver those same disruptive infections if threat actors choose to load ransomware as secondary payloads, a scenario we have observed on multiple occasions recently.
[Proofpoint](https://www.proofpoint.com/us/threat-insight/post/brushaloader-still-sweeping-victims-one-year-later)

name: Brute Force Attack Outbound
description: Lateral Movement involves using various techniques to navigate through a network and gain access to remote systems. Brute force attacks outbound can indicate compromise or malicious insider behavior.
additional_info:
Lateral Movement consists of techniques that adversaries use to enter and control remote systems on a network. Following through on their primary objective often requires exploring the network to find their target and subsequently gaining access to it. Reaching their objective often involves pivoting through multiple systems and accounts to gain. Adversaries might install their own remote access tools to accomplish Lateral Movement or use legitimate credentials with native network and operating system tools, which may be stealthier. 

[Mitre TA0008](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0008/)

Brute force attack is an exhaustive password guessing technique. Seeing such attack originating from internal host can indicate compromise or malicious insider behavior.

name: Brute Ratel
description: BRc4 is a sophisticated red-teaming tool designed to avoid detection by EDR and AV capabilities. It is newer and less known than Cobalt Strike, but just as dangerous.
additional_info:
Brute Ratel C4 (BRc4), the newest red-teaming and adversarial attack simulation tool to hit the market. While this capability has managed to stay out of the spotlight and remains less commonly known than its Cobalt Strike brethren, it is no less sophisticated. Instead, this tool is uniquely dangerous in that it was specifically designed to avoid detection by endpoint detection and response (EDR) and antivirus (AV) capabilities. 
[Palo Alto](unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/brute-ratel-c4-tool/)

name: Budminer
description: Symantec links Dripion malware to Budminer group and notes shift to new tactics after being exposed in security reports. No recent campaigns using Taidoor malware since 2014.
additional_info:
Based on the evidence we have presented Symantec attributed the activity involving theDripion malware to the Budminer advanced threat group. While we have not seen newcampaigns using Taidoor malware since 2014, we believe the Budminer group has changedtactics to avoid detection after being outed publicly in security white papers and blogs over thepast few years.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/budminer)

name: Buer
description: Buer is a downloader used by threat actors to distribute malware. It has been used in email campaigns and sold as a service since August 2019.
additional_info:
Buer is a downloader sold on underground forums and used by threat actors to deliver payload malware onto target machines. It has been observed in email campaigns and has been sold as a service since August 2019.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.buer)

name: BugSleep
description: BugSleep is a backdoor used to execute commands and transfer files between compromised machines and C&C servers. It is under development, with threat actors enhancing functionality and addressing bugs.
additional_info:
BugSleep is a backdoor designed to execute the threat actors’ commands and transfer files between the compromised machine and the C&C server. The backdoor is currently in development, with the threat actors continuously improving its functionality and addressing bugs.
[Checkpoint](https://research.checkpoint.com/2024/new-bugsleep-backdoor-deployed-in-recent-muddywater-campaigns/)

name: Buhtrap
description: Buhtrap is a hacker group targeting Russian and Ukrainian banks since 2015. They have conducted successful attacks resulting in significant financial losses, using a network worm to infect bank infrastructures.
additional_info:
Buhtrap has been active since 2014, however their first attacks against financial institutions were only detected in August 2015. Earlier, the group had only focused on targeting banking clients. At the moment, the group is known to target Russian and Ukrainian banks. From August 2015 to February 2016 Buhtrap managed to conduct 13 successful attacks against Russian banks for a total amount of 1.8 billion rubles ($25.7 mln). The number of successful attacks against Ukrainian banks has not been identified. Buhtrap is the first hacker group using a network worm to infect the overall bank infrastructure that significantly increases the difficulty of removing all malicious functions from the network. As a result, banks have to shut down the whole infrastructure which provokes delay in servicing customers and additional losses. Malicious programs intentionally scan for machines with an automated Bank-Customer system of the Central Bank of Russia (further referred to as BCS CBR). We have not identified incidents of attacks involving online money transfer systems, ATM machines or payment gates which are known to be of interest for other criminal groups.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/buhtrap)

name: Bumblebee
description: Bumblebee is a C++ loader used by threat actors since March 2022 to download and execute payloads. Linked to ransomware groups like Conti, Quantum, and Mountlocker. Named after user-agent appearance.
additional_info:
Bumblebee is a custom loader written in C++ that has been used by multiple threat actors, including possible initial access brokers, to download and execute additional payloads since at least March 2022. Bumblebee has been linked to ransomware operations including Conti, Quantum, and Mountlocker and derived its name from the appearance of "bumblebee" in the user-agent.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1039/)

name: BunnyLoader
description: BunnyLoader is a malware available for purchase by cybercriminals on online forums for $250. It offers various features like downloading and executing a second-stage payload and harvesting browser credentials.
additional_info:
BunnyLoader is the name of malware available for purchase (for $250) by cybercriminals across multiple online forums. It is presented as a Malware-as-a-Service (MaaS) and provides a range of features, such as downloading and executing a second-stage payload and harvesting browser credentials and system information.
[PCrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/27955-bunnyloader-malware)

name: Bypass Ticket
description: Potentially Unwanted Programs (PUPs) are software that can enter your device without your knowledge, compromising privacy and security by tracking activity, displaying ads, and making unwanted changes that slow down your device.
additional_info: Potentially Unwanted Programs (PUPs) are a type of software that can get onto your device without your knowledge. PUPs are often bundled with free software, downloaded unintentionally, or distributed through deceptive advertising. While PUPs may not necessarily be harmful, they can compromise your privacy and security by tracking your online activity or displaying targeted ads. PUPs can modify your web browser settings, display unwanted ads, install other unwanted software, or make unwanted changes to your system. Some PUPs can also slow down your device and cause it to crash. [Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/cybersecurity/basics/what-is-pup)

name: CASHY200
description: Analysis of xHunt campaign revealed domains linked to pasta58[.]com involved in Sakabota C2 activity. The backdoor CASHY200 uses DNS tunneling to communicate with its C2 server. Random identifiers stored in the registry are key features of CASHY200.
additional_info:
During our continued analysis of the xHunt campaign, we observed several domains with ties to the pasta58[.]com domain associated with known Sakabota command and control (C2) activity. In June 2019, we observed one of these overlapping domains, specifically, windows64x[.]com, being used as the C2 server for a new PowerShell based backdoor that we’ve named CASHY200. This PowerShell backdoor used DNS tunneling to communicate with its C2 server, specifically by issuing DNS A queries to the actor controlled name server at the aforementioned domain. CASHY200 parses data provided by the C2 server within DNS answers to run commands on the system and send the results back to the C2 via DNS queries. In several samples, CASHY200 used randomly generated identifiers that are stored in the registry at HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Cashe\index and used the command value 200 to communicate with the C2 server. These details are the basis for the name CASHY200.
[Unit42](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/more-xhunt-new-powershell-backdoor-blocked-through-dns-tunnel-detection/)

threat.name: CASHY200 - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
CASHY200 - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter

name: CCleaner
description: In September 2017, CCleaner malware was found, with hackers adding malicious code to steal data. This turned the tool meant to clean computers into a threat to user information.
additional_info:
..in September 2017, CCleaner malware was discovered. Hackers took the legitimate program and inserted malicious code that was designed to steal data from users. They turned a tool meant to scrub your computer clean of lurking malware into a serious threat to sensitive and personal information.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/ccleaner-malware)

name: CCoreDoor
description: Sophos discovered new malware like CCoreDoor, PocoProxy, and an updated EAGERBEE variant, used for blackholing AV vendor domains. Other malware variants included NUPAKAGE, Merlin C2 Agent, Cobalt Strike, PhantomNet, RUDEBIRD, and PowHeartBeat.
additional_info:
Sophos identified the use of previously unreported malware we call CCoreDoor (concurrently discovered by BitDefender) and PocoProxy, as well as an updated variant of EAGERBEE malware with new capabilities to blackhole communications to anti-virus (AV) vendor domains in the targeted organization’s network. Other observed malware variants include NUPAKAGE, Merlin C2 Agent, Cobalt Strike, PhantomNet backdoor, RUDEBIRD malware, and the PowHeartBeat backdoor.
[Sophos](https://news.sophos.com/en-us/2024/06/05/operation-crimson-palace-sophos-threat-hunting-unveils-multiple-clusters-of-chinese-state-sponsored-activity-targeting-southeast-asia/)

name: CHAOS RAT
description: CHAOS is a free and open-source Remote Administration Tool available on GitHub. It allows for generating binaries to control remote operating systems.
additional_info:
CHAOS is a free and open-source Remote Administration Tool that allow generate binaries to control remote operating systems.
[Github](https://github.com/tiagorlampert/CHAOS)

name: CHIMNEYSWEEP
description: Identified backdoor CHIMNEYSWEEP uses Telegram or actor-owned infrastructure for command-and-control, capable of key functions including taking screenshots, collecting files, and keylogging. Likely targeting Farsi and Arabic speakers since 2012.
additional_info:
Mandiant further identified CHIMNEYSWEEP, a backdoor that uses either Telegram or actor-owned infrastructure for command-and-control and is capable of taking screenshots, listing and collecting files, spawning a reverse shell, and supports keylogging functionality. CHIMNEYSWEEP shares code with ROADSWEEP and based on observed decoy content has likely been used to target Farsi and Arabic speakers as far back as 2012.
[Mandiant](https://www.mandiant.com/resources/likely-iranian-threat-actor-conducts-politically-motivated-disruptive-activity-against?1)

name: CIA Ransomware
description: Ransomware is malware that blocks access to files or system, demanding payment for access. Early versions required payment via snail mail, now usually demands cryptocurrency or credit card payment.
additional_info:
Ransom malware, or ransomware, is a type of malware that prevents users from accessing their system or personal files and demands ransom payment in order to regain access. The earliest variants of ransomware were developed in the late 1980s, and payment was to be sent via snail mail. Today, ransomware authors order that payment be sent via cryptocurrency or credit card.
[Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/ransomware/)

threat.name: CIA Ransomware - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
CIA Ransomware - MITRE - System Information Discovery
CIA Ransomware - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
CIA Ransomware - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
CIA Ransomware - MITRE - Ingress Tool Transfer

name: CageyChameleon
description: CageyChameleon is a VBS-based backdoor malware that collects and sends user and system information to a C2 server, while also checking for antivirus products and initiating subsequent operations.
additional_info:
CageyChameleon Malware is a VBS-based backdoor which has the capability to enumerate the list of running processes and check for the presence of several antivirus products. CageyChameleon will collect user host information, system current process information, etc. The collected information is sent back to the C2 server, and continue to initiate requests to perform subsequent operations.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/vbs.cageychameleon)

threat.name: CageyChameleon - MITRE - Exfiltration Over C2 Channel

name: Calisto
description: Callisto Group is a threat actor targeting military, government, think tanks, and journalists in Europe and South Caucasus to gather intelligence on foreign and security policy in the regions.
additional_info:
The Callisto Group is an advanced threat actor whose known targets include military personnel, government officials, think tanks, and journalists in Europe and the South Caucasus. Their primary interest appears to be gathering intelligence related to foreign and security policy in the Eastern Europe and South Caucasus regions.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/callisto)

name: Camaro Dragon
description: Check Point Research uncovers targeted attacks on European foreign affairs by Chinese state-sponsored group Camaro Dragon, similar to activities of Mustang Panda.
additional_info:
Check Point Research has closely monitored a series of targeted attacks aimed at European foreign affairs entities. These campaigns have been linked to a Chinese state-sponsored APT group we track as Camaro Dragon, which shares similarities with previously reported activities conducted by state-sponsored Chinese threat actors, namely Mustang Panda.
[Checkpoint](https://research.checkpoint.com/2023/the-dragon-who-sold-his-camaro-analyzing-custom-router-implant/)

name: Campo Loader
description: Loaders are simple Trojans that allow attackers to control a victim's computer remotely. They are evolving to include features like a user-friendly UI and self-healing capabilities, making them more attractive to cybercriminals.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

threat.name: Campo Loader - MITRE - Ingress Tool Transfer

name: Candiru
description: Candiru is an Israeli company selling spyware to governments, capable of infecting and monitoring iPhones, Androids, Macs, PCs, and cloud accounts. [citizenlab]
additional_info:
Candiru is a secretive Israel-based company that sells spyware exclusively to governments. Reportedly, their spyware can infect and monitor iPhones, Androids, Macs, PCs, and cloud accounts.
[citizenlab](https://citizenlab.ca/2021/07/hooking-candiru-another-mercenary-spyware-vendor-comes-into-focus/)

name: Cannibal RAT
description: Remote Access Trojan (RAT) is a malware that enables covert surveillance, remote access, and administrative control of a victim's computer. It is a dangerous tool for hackers.
additional_info: Remote Access Trojan (RAT) is a type of malware that allows covert surveillance, a backdoor for administrative control and unfettered and unauthorized remote access to a victim's machine. The RAT is very dangerous because it enables intruders to get remote control of the compromised computer

name: Carberp
description: Carberp is a malicious software that steals credentials and information, active since 2009. Its source code was leaked in 2013 and used as the basis for Carbanak backdoor.
additional_info:
Carberp is a credential and information stealing malware that has been active since at least 2009. Carberp's source code was leaked online in 2013, and subsequently used as the foundation for the Carbanak backdoor
[Mitre](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0484/)

name: Card Skimmer
description: Web skimming is a type of fraud where malware is injected onto a website's payment page to steal payment information by compromising third-party script services.
additional_info:
Web skimming is a form of internet or carding fraud whereby a payment page on a website is compromised when malware is injected onto the page via compromising a third-party script service in order to steal payment information.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_skimming)

threat.name: Card Skimmer - MITRE - Exfiltration Over Web Service

name: Carderbee APT
description: Attackers used malware with a legitimate Microsoft certificate in a campaign targeting victims mostly in Hong Kong and other parts of Asia. [Symantec]
additional_info:
In the course of this attack, the attackers used malware signed with a legitimate Microsoft certificate. Most of the victims in this campaign are based in Hong Kong, with some victims based in other regions of Asia.
[Symantec](https://symantec-enterprise-blogs.security.com/blogs/threat-intelligence/carderbee-software-supply-chain-certificate-abuse)

name: CargoBay
description: Remote Access Trojans allow covert surveillance or unauthorized access to a victim PC by mimicking keylogger behavior but providing remote access for attackers to monitor user behavior, change settings, and more.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/remote-access-trojan-rat/)

name: Caypnamer
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems. They enable hackers to spy, steal data, and gain backdoor access.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: CeranaKeeper APT
description: ESET researchers found targeted campaigns against Thailand government using file-sharing services. Identified threat actor CeranaKeeper evolves tools quickly to exfiltrate data, uses cloud services for stealth. Presented findings at Virus Bulletin 2024.
additional_info:
ESET researchers have discovered several targeted campaigns against governmental institutions in Thailand, starting in 2023, where massive amounts of data have been exfiltrated. The campaigns misused legitimate file-sharing services such as Dropbox, PixelDrain, GitHub, and OneDrive in the process. Based on the findings, ESET researchers decided to track this activity cluster as the work of a separate threat actor, which ESET named CeranaKeeper. The numerous occurrences of the string “bectrl” in the code of the group’s tools inspired the name: a wordplay between the word beekeeper and the bee species Apis Cerana, or the Asian honeybee. ESET presented its findings about CeranaKeeper and the compromise in Thailand at the 2024 Virus Bulletin conference.

The threat actor behind the attacks on the Thai government, CeranaKeeper, seems particularly relentless, as the plethora of tools and techniques used by the group keeps evolving at a rapid rate. The operators write and rewrite their toolset as needed and react rather quickly to avoid detection. This group’s goal is to harvest as many files as possible and it develops specific components to that end. CeranaKeeper uses cloud and file-sharing services for exfiltration and probably relies on the fact that traffic to these popular services would mostly seem legitimate and harder to block when identified.
[Eset](https://www.eset.com/int/about/newsroom/press-releases/research/eset-research-discovers-new-china-aligned-apt-group-ceranakeeper-which-targeted-the-thai-government/)

name: Cerber
description: Prolific ransomware originally adding ".cerber" extension, now with various extensions. Uses identifiable UDP activity, primarily operating through TOR for payments.详 [Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.cerber)
additional_info:
A prolific ransomware which originally added ".cerber" as a file extension to encrypted files. Has undergone multiple iterations in which the extension has changed. Uses a very readily identifiable set of of UDP activity to checkin and report infections. Primarily uses TOR for payment information.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.cerber)

Cerber - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Cerber - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Cerberus
description: Cerberus is a mobile banking trojan that steals credentials, logs keystrokes, and allows remote control over devices. It is sold on underground forums as a service.
additional_info:
Cerberus is a trojan horse targeting mobile banking credentials. It is capable of logging all keystrokes (including passwords) and stealing 2FA tokens from Google Authenticator and SMS messages. It also allows remote control over the device using TeamViewer. It is sold as Malware as a service on underground forums.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cerberus_(Android))

name: Certishell
description:
Oldest family RAT uses sivpici.php5.sk as C&C server, malware disguised as cracked software, fraud apps, music CD unpackers; downloads simple RAT executable and archive. 

Source: Avast(decoded.avast.io/danielbenes/warez-users-fell-for-certishell/)
additional_info:
The oldest part of the family is a simple RAT with sivpici.php5[.]sk as the C&C server. It places all the needed files in the folder .win inside of the user folder. 

The malware installer comes disguised as one of the following:

Cracked software, such as FixmyPC,
Fraud apps, like SteamCDKeys that share Steam keys,
Music CD unpackers with names like Extractor.exe or Heslo.exe (Heslo means password in Czech/Slovak) that come with a password protected archive with music files.
The malicious executable downloads an executable named UnRAR.exe and a malicious archive that contains a simple RAT written in C++, AutoIT or Go.
(Avast)[https://decoded.avast.io/danielbenes/warez-users-fell-for-certishell/]

threat.name: Certishell - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter: Visual Basic
Certishell - MITRE - System Network Configuration Discovery
Certishell - MITRE - Data from Local System

name: ChChes
description: ChChes is a Trojan linked to menuPass, used in attacks against Japanese organizations in 2016. Its absence of persistence methods indicates it may be a preliminary tool.
additional_info:
ChChes is a Trojan that appears to be used exclusively by menuPass. It was used to target Japanese organizations in 2016. Its lack of persistence methods suggests it may be intended as a first-stage tool.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0144/)

name: ChaChi
description: ChaChi is a new Golang RAT, contributing to the growing number of malicious software written in Go. Analysis tools are still catching up due to the language's relative newness.
additional_info:
ChaChi is another entry in the expanding list of malicious software written in Go, also known as Golang, which is a relatively young programming language. As this is such a new phenomenon, many core tools to the analysis process are still catching up. This can make Go a more challenging language to analyze.
[Blackberry](https://blogs.blackberry.com/en/2021/06/pysa-loves-chachi-a-new-golang-rat)

name: ChamelGang
description: In Q2 2021, PT Expert Security Center investigated an energy company compromised by ChamelGang, disguised under legitimate services of major tech companies. Attackers used supply chain method to breach the network.
additional_info:
In Q2 2021, the PT Expert Security Center incident response team conducted an investigation in an energy company. The investigation revealed that the company's network had been compromised by an unknown group for the purpose of data theft. We gave the group the name ChamelGang (from the word "chameleon"), because the group disguised its malware and network infrastructure under legitimate services of Microsoft, TrendMicro, McAfee, IBM, and Google. The attackers employed two methods. They acquired domains that imitate legitimate ones (newtrendmicro.com, centralgoogle.com, microsoft-support.net, cdn-chrome.com, mcafee-upgrade.com). In addition, the APT group placed SSL certificates that also imitated legitimate ones (github.com, www.ibm.com, jquery.com, update.microsoft-support.net) on its servers. To achieve their goal, the attackers used a trending penetration method—supply chain. The group compromised a subsidiary and penetrated the target company's network through it.
[Ptsecurity](https://www.ptsecurity.com/ww-en/analytics/pt-esc-threat-intelligence/new-apt-group-chamelgang/)

name: Chaos
description: Linux malware called Chaos targets SSH services with brute force attacks, installs reverse shell for controllers to access compromised systems via unsolicited packets. [MITRE]
additional_info:
Chaos is Linux malware that compromises systems by brute force attacks against SSH services. Once installed, it provides a reverse shell to its controllers, triggered by unsolicited packets.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0220/)

name: ChaosBounce
description: Remote Access Trojans allow covert surveillance or unauthorized access to a victim PC. They can collect keystrokes, passwords, screenshots, and more, providing attackers with remote access to the victim machine.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/remote-access-trojan-rat/)

additional_info:
This family includes programs that contain encrypted malicious code. This malicious code is decrypted when the program is launched. After  decryption, the malicious code is injected into the process of the original program.
[Kaspersky](https://threats.kaspersky.com/en/threat/Trojan.Win32.Chapak/)

name: Cheana Stealer
description: CRIL discovered phishing site imitating "WarpVPN" to steal data from Windows, Linux, and macOS users. Separate stealer binaries created for each OS. Targets cryptocurrency-related data and sensitive information. Refer to Cyble for details.
additional_info:
CRIL has identified a phishing website aimed at individuals downloading VPN applications for Windows, Linux, and macOS. The TA has created separate stealer binaries for each operating system, using a deceptive site that mimics the legitimate “WarpVPN” service. This phishing site offers detailed installation instructions specific to each platform. Once installed, the stealer extracts various sensitive data from the victim’s machine. It targets cryptocurrency-related browser extensions, standalone crypto wallets, stored browser passwords, browser login data, cookies, SSH keys, macOS passwords, and Keychain. The below image shows the phishing website. 
[Cyble](https://cyble.com/blog/new-cheana-stealer-targets-vpn-user/)

name: Chinotto
description: Kaspersky researchers discovered new malware named Chinotto targeting North Korean defectors and activists, operated by ScarCruft APT actor. The malware can steal sensitive data and exploit victims' connections.
additional_info:
In a recent investigation, Kaspersky researchers uncovered a previously unknown malware dubbed Chinotto targeting North Korean defectors and human rights activists. The malware operated by an Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actor ScarCruft is implemented in PowerShell, Windows executables, and Android apps. It is capable of controlling and exfiltrating sensitive information from its targets. Furthermore, the attackers attempted to gather information and attack victims' connections using the victim's compromised social network and email.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/about/press-releases/2021_chinotto-kaspersky-uncovered-formerly-unknown-malware-operated-by-scarcruft-threat-actor)

threat.name: Chinotto - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell

name: ChromeBack
description: ChromeBack is identified as a browser hijacker by GoSecure, diverting user traffic and displaying unwanted ads.
additional_info:
GoSecure describes ChromeBack as a browser hijacker, redirecting traffic and serving advertisements to users.
(Malpedia)[https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/js.chromeback]

name: ChromeLoader
description: In January 2022, the ChromeLoader adware campaign was discovered, spreading through malicious ads and browser extensions to potentially leak data from users and organizations, with evolving capabilities.
additional_info:
In January 2022, a new browser hijacker/adware campaign named ChromeLoader (also known as Choziosi Loader and ChromeBack) was discovered. Despite using simple malicious advertisements, the malware became widespread, potentially leaking data from thousands of users and organizations.
Instead of more traditional malware like a Windows executable (.exe) or Dynamic Link Library (.dll), the malware authors used a browser extension as their final payload. The browser extension serves as adware and an infostealer, leaking all of the user’s search engine queries. We discovered significant changes and additions of capabilities throughout this campaign's evolution, and we predict further changes as this campaign continues.
[Unit42](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/chromeloader-malware/)

name: Cinobi
description: Cinobi banking trojan targets users through malvertisements disguised as Japanese porn games, reward point apps, or video streaming apps, exploiting sideloading vulnerabilities.
additional_info:
The malicious application abused sideloading vulnerabilities to load and start the Cinobi banking trojan. ... The campaign's infection routine begins when a user received malvertisements that are disguised as advertisements of either Japanese animated porn games, reward points applications, or video streaming applications.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/21/h/cinobi-banking-trojan-targets-users-of-cryptocurrency-exchanges-.html)
