name: Cinoshi Stealer
description: Cinoshi is a dangerous malware that steals information and has various abilities such as botnet, clipper, and cryptominer functionalities. Its presence can threaten device integrity and user privacy.
additional_info: Cinoshi is the name of an information-stealing malware. There are several variants of this stealer, some of which have additional abilities – including botnet, clipper, and cryptominer functionalities. The presence of Cinoshi malware on the system can endanger both device integrity and user privacy.[pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/26246-cinoshi-stealer)

name: Cisco CVE-2019-1653
description: Vulnerability in Cisco Small Business RV320 and RV325 routers allows remote attacker to access sensitive information through improper URL access controls. Cisco has released firmware updates to address the issue.
additional_info:
A vulnerability in the web-based management interface of Cisco Small Business RV320 and RV325 Dual Gigabit WAN VPN Routers could allow an unauthenticated, remote attacker to retrieve sensitive information. The vulnerability is due to improper access controls for URLs. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability by connecting to an affected device via HTTP or HTTPS and requesting specific URLs. A successful exploit could allow the attacker to download the router configuration or detailed diagnostic information. Cisco has released firmware updates that address this vulnerability.
[MITRE](https://cve.mitre.org/cgi-bin/cvename.cgi?name=CVE-2019-1653)

threat.name: Cisco CVE-2019-1653 - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
Cisco CVE-2019-1653 - MITRE - Exploitation of Remote Services

name: Citadel
description: Citadel is a malware targeting password managers like Keepass and Password Safe, based on Zeus. By 2017, it had infected 11 million computers globally and caused over $500 million in losses.
additional_info:
Citadel is a piece of massively-distributed malware based upon Zeus.[1] It targets credentials stored in password managers such as Keepass, Password Safe and neXus Personal Security Client.
By 2017 (it was first identified in 2011)[3] Citadel had infected about 11 million computers worldwide and had caused over $500 million in losses.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citadel_(malware))

name: ClearFake
description: Malicious fake updates campaign infecting hundreds of websites with ClearFake malware, starting around July 19th, 2023. No tracking of visits by IP or cookies, leading to easier detection by analysts and site owners.
additional_info:
There are several malicious fake updates campaigns being run across thousands of compromised websites. This campaign appears to have started around July 19th, 2023. Based on a search on PublicWWW of the injection base64 there are at least 434 infected sites. The name is a reference to the majority of the Javascript being used without obfuscation. One noticeable difference from SocGholish is that there appears to be no tracking of visits by IP or cookies. As an analyst you can you go back to the compromised site over and over coming from the same IP and not clearing your browser cache. This also means the site owner is more likely to see the infection as well. When a user visits a compromised website with ClearFake, the page initially loads as normal before the whole page is taken over by a call to action to update Chrome.

On the index page of the compromised site there is a Javascript injection. The Javascript is base64 encoded. Presumably this is a dynamic injection and will change over time to reflect the new host for the initial payload. On the index page of the compromised site there is a Javascript injection. The Javascript is base64 encoded. Presumably this is a dynamic injection and will change over time to reflect the new host for the initial payload. The second web call returns a Javascript that creates an iframe to house the fake update UI. The iframe src is set to a Keitaro endpoint. The response from the Keitaro endpoint is the foundation for the HTML to be rendered within the iframe.

[ClearFake Malware Analysis](https://rmceoin.github.io/malware-analysis/clearfake/)

name: Cleartext password
description: Adversaries use network sniffing to capture authentication data and steal credentials. By placing network interfaces into promiscuous mode or using span ports, they can passively access data in transit. Techniques like poisoning and credential dumping are used to obtain credentials for malicious purposes.
additional_info:
Adversaries may sniff network traffic to capture information about an environment, including authentication material passed over the network. Network sniffing refers to using the network interface on a system to monitor or capture information sent over a wired or wireless connection. An adversary may place a network interface into promiscuous mode to passively access data in transit over the network, or use span ports to capture a larger amount of data.

Data captured via this technique may include user credentials, especially those sent over an insecure, unencrypted protocol. Techniques for name service resolution poisoning, such as LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay, can also be used to capture credentials to websites, proxies, and internal systems by redirecting traffic to an adversary. [MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1040/)

Credential Access consists of techniques for stealing credentials like account names and passwords. Techniques used to get credentials include keylogging or credential dumping. Using legitimate credentials can give adversaries access to systems, make them harder to detect, and provide the opportunity to create more accounts to help achieve their goals. [MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0006/)

name: ClickFix
description: In April 2024, researchers found compromised sites leading to an iframe on pley[.]es showing an error message. The payload domain was taken offline, preventing infection. Later, the iframe was replaced with the ClearFake inject.
additional_info:
In mid-April 2024, researchers found compromised sites containing an inject leading to an iframe on pley[.]es. This iframe was shown as an overlay error message claiming that a faulty browser update needed to be fixed. Researchers dubbed this activity cluster ClickFix.

The error message asked the victim to open “Windows PowerShell (Admin)” (which will open an UAC prompt) and then right-click to paste the code. If this was done, PowerShell would run another remote PowerShell script that would download and run an executable, eventually leading to Vidar Stealer. However, just a few days later, after discovery, the payload domain used in the PowerShell was taken offline. Thus, despite the error being displayed on compromised websites, it could not lead to an infection. 

After a few days of this semi-functional state, 15 May 2024, the custom content of the iframe was replaced with the ClearFake inject. It is still serving this inject in early June 2024. As the pley[.]es domain itself seems to be compromised, it’s unclear if these two activity sets – ClearFake and ClickFix – started to work with each other, or if the ClearFake actor re-compromised the iframe, replacing the code with its own content.

[Proofpoint](https://www.proofpoint.com/us/blog/threat-insight/clipboard-compromise-powershell-self-pwn)

name: Clicker
description: Trojan clicker is malware that connects to online resources by manipulating browser commands or replacing system files like the hosts file in Windows to hijack site addresses.
additional_info:
A type of Trojan that attempts to connect to online resources, either by sending commands to the browser or by replacing system files that specify standard site addresses (for example, the hosts file in Windows).
[Kaspersky](https://encyclopedia.kaspersky.com/knowledge/trojan-clicker/)

name: ClipBanker
description: ClipBanker Trojan steals sensitive information like browser history, cookies, and cryptocurrency wallet addresses through PowerShell commands. It manipulates banking actions and spreads through phishing emails or social media posts.
additional_info:
The ClipBanker Trojan is known as an information stealer and spy trojan, it aims to steal and record any type of sensitive information from the infected environment such as browser history, cookies, Outlook data, Skype, Telegram, or cryptocurrency wallet account addresses. The main goal of this threat is to steal confidential information.
The ClipBanker uses PowerShell commands for executing malicious activities. The thing that made the ClipBanker unique is its ability to record various banking actions of the user and manipulate them for its own benefit. The distribution method of the ClipBanker is through phishing emails or through social media posts that lure users to download malicious content.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.clipbanker)

threat.name: ClipBanker - MITRE - Credentials from Password Stores
ClipBanker - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
ClipBanker - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter

name: Cloud Atlas
description: Inception, a cyber espionage group since 2014, targets industries and governments primarily in Russia, but is also active in the US, Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Middle East.
additional_info:
Inception is a cyber espionage group active since at least 2014. The group has targeted multiple industries and governmental entities primarily in Russia, but has also been active in the United States and throughout Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Middle East.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0100/)

name: Cloud9
description: Zimperium zLabs found a harmful browser extension called Cloud9 that steals data, installs malware, and takes control of devices. It spreads through unofficial stores and fake software updates.
additional_info:
The Zimperium zLabs team recently discovered a malicious browser extension, which not only steals the information available during the browser session but can also install malware on a user’s device and subsequently assume control of the entire device. In this blog, we will take a deeper look into the architecture and modus operandi of this malicious browser extension, originally called Cloud9, by the malware author.

Cloud9 was never found on any official browser extension store, instead relying on distribution through threat actor communities, where users of the tool would then hide the malware to deliver to victims. These distribution methods can carry, but the most common we witnessed during the investigation were side-loading through fake executables and malicious websites disguised as Adobe Flash Player updates.
[Zimperium](https://www.zimperium.com/blog/the-case-of-cloud9-chrome-botnet/)

name: CloudWizard
description: CloudWizard and CommonMagic frameworks use RC5Simple library for encryption. Malicious implants change the header value. Victim IDs have a date and two letters. Victims are in Eastern Europe conflict area.
additional_info:
Both frameworks, CloudWizard (version 4) and CommonMagic use the RC5Simple library for encryption. Files encrypted with RC5Simple start with a 7-byte header, which is set to ‘RC5SIMP’ in the library source code. However, this value has been changed in the malicious implants: DUREX43 in CloudWizard and Hwo7X8p in CommonMagic. Additionally, CloudWizard and CommonMagic use the RapidJSON library for parsing JSON objects.

Names of files uploaded to the C2 server in CommonMagic have the format mm.dd _hh.mm.ss.ms.dat (in CloudWizard, the name format is dd.mm.yyyy_hh.mm.ss.ms.dat).

Victim IDs extracted from CloudWizard and CommonMagic samples are similar: they contain a date followed by the two same letters, e.g. 03072020DD, 05082020BB in CloudWizard and WorkObj20220729FF in CommonMagic.

Victims of CommonMagic and CloudWizard are located in the area of conflict in Eastern Europe.
[Securelist](https://securelist.com/cloudwizard-apt/109722/)

name: Cobalt Group
description: Financially motivated Cobalt Group targets financial institutions globally, focusing on banks in Eastern Europe, Central Asia, and Southeast Asia. Known for targeting ATM systems, card processing, payment systems, and SWIFT systems. Possible links to Carbanak malware and group.
additional_info:
Cobalt Group is a financially motivated threat group that has primarily targeted financial institutions. The group has conducted intrusions to steal money via targeting ATM systems, card processing, payment systems and SWIFT systems. Cobalt Group has mainly targeted banks in Eastern Europe, Central Asia, and Southeast Asia. One of the alleged leaders was arrested in Spain in early 2018, but the group still appears to be active. The group has been known to target organizations in order to use their access to then compromise additional victims. Reporting indicates there may be links between Cobalt Group and both the malware Carbanak and the group Carbanak. 
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0080/)

name: Cobalt Strike
description: Cobalt Strike is a commercial penetration testing tool that simulates advanced threat actor attacks, covering the full range of ATT&CK tactics and integrating with tools like Metasploit and Mimikatz.
additional_info:
Cobalt Strike is a commercial, full-featured, penetration testing tool which bills itself as “adversary simulation software designed to execute targeted attacks and emulate the post-exploitation actions of advanced threat actors”. Cobalt Strike’s interactive post-exploit capabilities cover the full range of ATT&CK tactics, all executed within a single, integrated system. 

In addition to its own capabilities, Cobalt Strike leverages the capabilities of other well-known tools such as Metasploit and Mimikatz. 
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0154/)

name: Cobian RAT
description: Cobian RAT is a remote access tool first seen in 2016, used as a backdoor for unauthorized access to systems. More information available on MITRE's website.
additional_info:
Cobian RAT is a backdoor, remote access tool that has been observed since 2016.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0338/)

threat.name: Cobian RAT - MITRE - Command and Control
Cobian RAT - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: CoinMinerESJ
description: Trojan virus can perform various malicious activities like remote access, DoS/DDoS attacks, keylogging, file deletion, and process termination. Fortinet is creating a detailed description of this virus.
additional_info:
Trojan has the capabilities to remote access connection handling, perform Denial of Service (DoS) or Distributed DoS (DDoS), capture keyboard inputs, delete file or object, or terminate process.
The Fortinet Anti-Virus Analyst Team is currently in the process of creating a detailed description for this virus.
[Fortinet](https://fortiguard.fortinet.com/encyclopedia/virus/5972018)

name: Coldstealer
description: ASEC Analysis Team discovered ColdStealer, an information stealer that sends user data to a C2 server. Cybercriminals distribute it using dropper and downloader malware, often through fake software cracking tools.
additional_info:
ASEC Analysis Team has discovered a new information stealer called ColdStealer. It was found that this malware steals various user information and sends it to Command and Control (C2) server. Cybercriminals distribute ColdStealer using a dropper and downloader malware that downloads ColdStealer from the C2 server. The dropper for this stealer is distributed via fake software cracking tools.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/23553-coldstealer-malware)

name: Colibri
description: Loaders grab malicious executables; some have user-friendly UI, self-healing abilities, and a retail-like interface. These basic remote access Trojans let attackers control compromised computers. Evolving loaders are still viable to cybercriminals.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

threat.name: Colibri - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: ComRAT
description: ComRAT is a suspected descendant of Agent.btz, used by Turla. First identified in 2007, it has undergone significant development over the years.
additional_info:
ComRAT is a second stage implant suspected of being a descendant of Agent.btz and used by Turla. The first version of ComRAT was identified in 2007, but the tool has undergone substantial development for many years since.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0126/)

name: Comm100
description: Malware is distributed through a signed Comm100 installer from the company's website, as reported by Crowdstrike.
additional_info:
Malware is delivered via a signed Comm100 installer that was downloadable from the company’s website.
[Crowdstrike](https://www.crowdstrike.com/blog/new-supply-chain-attack-leverages-comm100-chat-installer/)

name: Command and Control
description: Adversaries may use custom command and control protocols to communicate, mimicking or developing protocols on top of standard network stacks, instead of using existing Standard Application Layer Protocols.
additional_info: Adversaries may communicate using a custom command and control protocol instead of encapsulating commands/data in an existing Standard Application Layer Protocol. Implementations include mimicking well-known protocols or developing custom protocols (including raw sockets) on top of fundamental protocols provided by TCP/IP/another standard network stack.

name: Commonly Abused File Sharing
description: File sharing sites may be used by cyber criminals for malware delivery or data theft. Even though the sites are not malicious, they can still facilitate malicious activities, so they are typically banned in corporate settings.
additional_info: File sharing sites are often used by malicious actors for malware delivery or data exfiltration. While the sites themselves are usually not malicious, they nevertheless can function as a platform for malicious actors. Therefore, using those sites in corporate environments is often not permitted.

name: Commonly Abused Online Service
description: Online services can be exploited by malicious actors for malware delivery or data exfiltration. Despite not being inherently malicious, these sites can still be used as a platform for malicious activities, leading to restrictions in corporate environments.
additional_info: Some online services are often used by malicious actors for malware delivery or data exfiltration. While the sites themselves are usually not malicious, they nevertheless can function as a platform for malicious actors. Therefore, using those sites in corporate environments is often not permitted.

name: Compromised Wordpress Plugin
description: Up to 36,000 websites have been affected by a supply-chain attack on WordPress plugins, through which attackers added malicious functions to plugin updates, creating an attacker-controlled administrative account.
additional_info:
WordPress plugins running on as many as 36,000 websites have been backdoored in a supply-chain attack with unknown origins, security researchers said on Monday.

So far, five plugins are known to be affected in the campaign, which was active as recently as Monday morning, researchers from security firm Wordfence reported. Over the past week, unknown threat actors have added malicious functions to updates available for the plugins on WordPress.org, the official site for the open source WordPress CMS software. When installed, the updates automatically create an attacker-controlled administrative account that provides full control over the compromised site. The updates also add content designed to goose search results.

[Ars Technica](https://arstechnica.com/security/2024/06/supply-chain-attack-on-wordpress-plugins-affects-as-many-as-36000-sites/)

name: Comrerop
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems through social engineering. They can spy, steal data, and create backdoor access.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: ConPtyShell
description: ConPtyShell is a reverse shell for Windows that utilizes the Pseudo Console feature to enhance terminal functionality, creating a fully interactive remote powershell. It involves creating a Pseudo Console and attaching pipes.
additional_info:
ConPtyShell is a Fully Interactive Reverse Shell for Windows systems.

The introduction of the Pseudo Console (ConPty) in Windows has improved so much the way Windows handles terminals. ConPtyShell uses this feature to literally transform your bash in a remote powershell.

Briefly, it creates a Pseudo Console and attaches 2 pipes.
Then it creates the shell process (default powershell.exe) attaching the Pseudo Console with redirected input/output.
Then starts 2 Threads for Async I/O:
- one thread for reading from the socket and writing to Pseudo Console input pipe;
- the second thread for reading from the Pseudo Console output pipe and writing to the socket.
[Github](https://github.com/antonioCoco/ConPtyShell)

threat.name: ConPtyShell - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell

name: Confucius
description: Confucius is a cyber espionage group targeting military, high-profile individuals, businesses, and government organizations in South Asia since 2013. Similarities with Patchwork group noted by security researchers.
additional_info:
Confucius is a cyber espionage group that has primarily targeted military personnel, high-profile personalities, business persons, and government organizations in South Asia since at least 2013. Security researchers have noted similarities between Confucius and Patchwork, particularly in their respective custom malware code and targets.
[MITRE] (https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0142/)

name: ConnectWise
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs used by cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems. They can spy on users, steal data, and provide backdoor access to the system.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: Contagious Interview Campaign
description: North Korean threat actors are using OtterCookie malware in the Contagious Interview campaign to target software developers with fake job offers and deliver malicious payloads like BeaverTail and InvisibleFerret.
additional_info:
North Korean threat actors are using new malware called OtterCookie in the Contagious Interview campaign that is targeting software developers.

Contagious Interview has been active since at least December 2022, according to researchers at cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks. The campaign targets software developers with fake job offers to deliver malware such as BeaverTail and InvisibleFerret.

[Bleeping Computer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-ottercookie-malware-used-to-backdoor-devs-in-fake-job-offers/)

name: Copper Stealer
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data on infected computers to send to attackers. They use methods like browser hooking, web injection scripts, form grabbing, keylogging, and stealing saved passwords.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: Covenant
description: Covenant is a framework for controlling and attacking .NET applications, offering offensive tradecraft and collaborative capabilities for red teamers through a web-based interface.
additional_info:
Covenant is a .NET command and control framework that aims to highlight the attack surface of .NET, make the use of offensive .NET tradecraft easier, and serve as a collaborative command and control platform for red teamers.

Covenant is an ASP.NET Core, cross-platform application that includes a web-based interface that allows for multi-user collaboration.
[Github](https://github.com/cobbr/Covenant)

name: Covert channels
description: Covert channels in computer security are unauthorized methods of transferring information between processes, bypassing security measures. Originated in 1973 by Butler Lampson, they are distinguished from legitimate channels subjected to access controls.
additional_info:
In computer security, a covert channel is a type of attack that creates a capability to transfer information objects between processes that are not supposed to be allowed to communicate by the computer security policy. The term, originated in 1973 by Butler Lampson, is defined as channels "not intended for information transfer at all, such as the service program's effect on system load," to distinguish it from legitimate channels that are subjected to access controls by COMPUSEC.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Covert_channel)

threat.name: Covert channels - MITRE - Application Layer Protocol: DNS
Covert channels - MITRE - Application Layer Protocol

name: CrDatLoader
description: Loaders are simple tools used by attackers to collect malicious files, with some having additional features like a user-friendly interface or bot-selling capabilities. They are lightweight to evade security detection.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: Crashedtech
description: The loader code checks in with user information to the C2 server, retrieves payload location, downloads it, and executes it using the .NET API.
additional_info:
The loader code is pretty straightforward its main logic consists of two steps. First, it does a check-in providing user-name, os version, and public IP information to the “/addnew.php” endpoint on the C2, then it parses the server response to extract the location where to download further payloads. After this, it downloads the payload and executes it through the “Process.Start” .NET API.
[Medium]{https://medium.com/@lcam/updates-from-the-maas-new-threats-delivered-through-nullmixer-d45defc260d1}

name: Criminal RAT
description: Remote Access Trojans are malicious programs that allow unauthorized access to a victim's computer, mimicking keyloggers to collect sensitive information and enabling attackers to control the machine remotely.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/remote-access-trojan-rat/)

name: Crimson
description: Crimson malware is used in Operation Transparent Tribe, targeting Indian diplomatic and military victims. For more information, visit [MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0115/).
additional_info:
Crimson is malware used as part of a campaign known as Operation Transparent Tribe that targeted Indian diplomatic and military victims.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0115/)

name: Critroni
description: Ransomware is malware that prevents access to files and demands payment to restore access. It dates back to the 1980s and now demands payment via cryptocurrency or credit card.
additional_info:
Ransomware
Ransom malware, or ransomware, is a type of malware that prevents users from accessing their system or personal files and demands ransom payment in order to regain access. The earliest variants of ransomware were developed in the late 1980s, and payment was to be sent via snail mail. Today, ransomware authors order that payment be sent via cryptocurrency or credit card.
[Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/ransomware/)

threat.name: Critroni - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Critroni - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Critroni - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: CryptBot
description: Infostealer Cryptbot steals credentials for browsers, crypto wallets, credit cards, creates system screenshots. Stolen data uploaded to c2 server in zip file.
additional_info:
A typical infostealer, capable of obtaining credentials for browsers, crypto currency wallets, browser cookies, credit cards, and creates screenshots of the infected system. All stolen data is bundled into a zip-file that is uploaded to the c2.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.cryptbot)

name: CryptXXX
description: CryptXXX is ransomware spread via Angler Exploit Kit. It encrypts files using RSA4096 algorithm, requiring a private key stored on cyber criminals' servers for decryption. Victims must pay ransom for key.
additional_info:
CryptXXX is ransomware-type malware distributed using the Angler Exploit Kit. Following infiltration, CryptXXX encrypts various files stored on local and mounted drives using RSA4096 - an asymmetric encryption algorithm. Thus, public (to encrypt) and private (to decrypt) keys are generated during encryption. To the restore files, victims require the private key, which is stored on Command and Control (C&C) servers belonging to the cyber criminals. To receive the decrypter (with the private key embedded), victims must supposedly pay a ransom. In addition, CryptXXX gathers various private data (browsing details, cookies, etc.)
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/9963-cryptxxx-ransomware)

threat.name: CryptXXX - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
CryptXXX - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
CryptXXX - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Cryptex
description: Trojans are malware that mislead users, often spread through social engineering. They can steal personal information, delete files, and act as a backdoor for unauthorized access to a computer.
additional_info:
In computing, a Trojan horse (or simply trojan) is any malware which misleads users of its true intent. The term is derived from the Ancient Greek story of the deceptive Trojan Horse that led to the fall of the city of Troy.

Trojans are generally spread by some form of social engineering, for example where a user is duped into executing an email attachment disguised to appear not suspicious, (e.g., a routine form to be filled in), or by clicking on some fake advertisement on social media or anywhere else. Although their payload can be anything, many modern forms act as a backdoor, contacting a controller which can then have unauthorized access to the affected computer. Trojans may allow an attacker to access users' personal information such as banking information, passwords, or personal identity. It can also delete a user's files or infect other devices connected to the network. Ransomware attacks are often carried out using a trojan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trojan_horse_(computing))

name: CryptoWall
description: CryptoWall is a ransomware Trojan targeting Windows, first appearing in 2014. It was distributed through a malvertising campaign on the Zedo ad network, using browser plugin exploits and a digital signature.
additional_info:
Another major ransomware Trojan targeting Windows, CryptoWall, first appeared in 2014. One strain of CryptoWall was distributed as part of a malvertising campaign on the Zedo ad network in late-September 2014 that targeted several major websites; the ads redirected to rogue websites that used browser plugin exploits to download the payload. A Barracuda Networks researcher also noted that the payload was signed with a digital signature in an effort to appear trustworthy to security software.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ransomware#CryptoWall)

threat.name: CryptoWall - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
CryptoWall - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
CryptoWall - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Cryxos
description: Cryxos trojans trick users by displaying a fake notification claiming their computer has been blocked due to a virus, prompting them to call a phone number for help in a scam.
additional_info:
Cryxos trojans display an alarming notification message saying that the user's computer or web browser has been 'blocked' due to a virus infection, and that their personal details are 'being stolen'. The user is then directed to call a phone number for assistance in the 'removal process'. This is a version of a 'call support' scam.
[F-secure](https://www.f-secure.com/v-descs/trojan_js_cryxos.shtml)

name: Cuba Ransomware
description: Cuba ransomware family observed since 2019 using double extortion method, threatens to release victim's data if ransom not paid. Ransom note deposited with unique ID for contact via Tox or email. Files encrypted with ".cuba" extension.
additional_info:
The Cuba ransomware family has been observed since 2019. They use the now ubiquitous "double extortion" method of threatening to release a victim's data on the Internet if they do not pay the requested ransom.

Once the ransomware has executed, a ransom note will be deposited in any directory where files have been encrypted. The ransom note will be named "!! READ ME !!.txt" and contain a unique ID to contact the ransomware controllers to pay. The primary contact channel is Tox (a peer-to-peer instant messaging protocol) with a backup e-mail address if a victim cannot make contact. Files encrypted by Cuba will have a ".cuba" file extension appended.

[Fortinet](https://www.fortinet.com/blog/threat-research/ransomware-roundup-gwisin-kriptor-cuba-and-more)

additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.

name: CustomerLoader
description: CustomerLoader is a malicious program causing chain infections by loading additional malware onto compromised devices, primarily through the DotRunpeX injector trojan. It has been active since at least May 2023.
additional_info:
CustomerLoader is a malicious program designed to cause chain infections. In other words, it loads additional malicious components and programs onto compromised devices. All known CustomerLoader infections relied on the DotRunpeX injector trojan to infiltrate the final payload. Over forty malware families were proliferated in this manner.

The cybersecurity community has first become aware of CustomerLoader's existence in June of 2023; however, there is some evidence suggesting that this malware has been active since at least May of the same year.

Due to the variety of distribution methods implemented for CustomerLoader, it is likely that the program's developers are offering it as a service – thus, the malware is used by multiple threat actors.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/27260-customerloader-malware)

name: Cutwail
description: Cutwail botnet is responsible for sending spam emails since 2007, spread by a Trojan called Pushdo. Targets machines running Windows.
additional_info:
The Cutwail botnet, founded around 2007, is a botnet mostly involved in sending spam e-mails. The bot is typically installed on infected machines by a Trojan component called Pushdo. It affects computers running Microsoft Windows.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cutwail_botnet)

name: CyberGate
description: CyberGate is a malicious remote access tool used by cyber criminals to control computers and steal sensitive information. Infected users should uninstall it immediately.
additional_info:
CyberGate is one of many remote access tools (RATs) that allow users to control other connected computers remotely. Cyber criminals often use these programs for malicious purposes such as to steal personal, sensitive information and misuse it to generate revenue. People who have computers infected with programs such as CyberGate should uninstall them immediately.

[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/15607-cybergate-rat)

threat.name: CyberGate - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
CyberGate - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
CyberGate - MITRE - Data from Local System

name: CyberHaven Compromised Extension
description: Cyberhaven's Chrome extension was breached, allowing attackers to inject malicious code, compromising sensitive data of 400,000 users. A threat campaign targeting 2.6 million users on the Chrome Web Store was exposed.
additional_info:
Cyberhaven, a cybersecurity company, experienced a significant breach involving its Chrome browser extension, which was exploited by attackers to inject malicious code into users’ browsers. This code enabled the theft of sensitive data, including passwords and cookies, compromising the security of around 400,000 users. Following the breach a threat campaign targeting 2,600,000 users on the Chrome Web Store was exposed.
[Extensiontotal](https://www.extensiontotal.com/cyberhaven-incident-live)

name: Cylance Ransomware
description: Cylance is ransomware that encrypts files on Windows and Linux systems, appending the ".Cylance" extension to filenames and creating a "CYLANCE_README.txt" ransom note, unrelated to Cylance cybersecurity services.
additional_info:
Cylance is the name of malicious software that operates as ransomware. Its purpose is to encrypt files (to prevent victims from accessing their data). Additionally, Cylance appends the ".Cylance" extension to filenames and creates the "CYLANCE_README.txt" file (a ransom note).

Cylance targets Windows and Linux operating systems. An example of how Cylance modifies filenames: it renames "1.jpg" to "1.jpg.Cylance", "2.png" to "2.png.Cylance", and so forth. This ransomware shares the same name as Cylance enterprise cybersecurity, but the latter offers customers endpoint security services that are completely unrelated to the ransomware.[pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/26415-cylance-ransomware)

name: Cytrox Predator APT
description: Citizen Lab discovered REPLYSPY threat actor posting Cytrox Predator infection links in replies to officials, aligning with Security Lab's conclusions about Cytrox infrastructure targeting US and international individuals.
additional_info:
The Citizen Lab independently received and collected a set of since-deleted posts by this threat actor, which we call REPLYSPY. Our findings align with the Security Lab’s conclusions concerning Cytrox infrastructure, and we assess with high confidence that REPLYSPY included Cytrox Predator infection links in replies to numerous U.S. and international officials and others.
[Citizenslab](https://citizenlab.ca/2023/10/predator-spyware-targets-us-eu-lawmakers-journalists/)

name: DBatLoader

additional_info:
This Delphi loader misuses Cloud storage services, such as Google Drive to download the Delphi stager component. The Delphi stager has the actual payload embedded as a resource and starts it.

additional_info:
DarkCrystal, also known as dcRAT, is a Remote Access Trojan (RAT). Malware of this type is designed to enable remote access and control over an infected device. RATs can manipulate machines in various ways and can have likewise varied functionalities. DarkCrystal is a dangerous piece of software, which poses a significant threat to device and user safety.
[DcRat](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/18388-darkcrystal-rat)

name: DDG
description: DDG is a botnet discovered in October 2017 with P2P capability. It targets crypto currency mining, specifically Monero.
additional_info:
First activity observed in October 2017. DDG is a botnet with P2P capability that is targeting crypto currency mining (Monero).
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/elf.ddg)

name: DDoSia
description: DDoSia is a pro Russia hacktivist group's DDoS attack toolkit targeting countries critical of the Russian invasion of Ukraine. It was launched on Telegram in 2022, gaining thousands of subscribers.
additional_info:
DDoSia is a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack toolkit, developed and used by the pro Russia hacktivist nationalist group NoName057(16) against countries critical of the Russian invasion of Ukraine.

The DDoSia project was launched on Telegram in early 2022. The NoName057(16) main group main Telegram channel reached more than 45,000 subscribers as of June 2023, while the DDoSia project channels reached over 10,000 users. Administrators posted instructions for potential volunteers who want to participate in projects, and they added the possibility to pay in cryptocurrency for users who declare a valid TON wallet based on their contribution to the DDoS attacks.

[Sekoia](https://blog.sekoia.io/following-noname05716-ddosia-projects-targets/)

name: DFKRAT
description: Solar 4RAYS team discovered espionage malware DFKRAT on executive authorities in 2023. Same attackers targeted Russian-speaking victims with different malware samples in 2021-2023, designated as NGC2180.
additional_info:
Translated: 
At the end of 2023, the Solar 4RAYS team, as part of the Compromise Assessment, discovered an attack on one of the executive authorities. We have identified samples of multi-stage malware, which at the final stage deploys an implant on the target system, which we called DFKRAT. The malware, developed for espionage purposes, allows attackers to, among other things, steal data from the victim’s file system. We conducted extensive research and found other samples that differ technically, but were used by the same group of attackers throughout 2021–2023 against Russian-speaking targets. At the moment, we cannot attribute the cluster of this activity to any known grouping, so we designated it NGC2180.
[Rt-solar](https://rt-solar.ru/solar-4rays/blog/4124/)

name: DNS Tunneling
description: Domain Name Resolution (DNS) is a protocol that resolves human readable domains to IP addresses. Attackers can use DNS tunneling to establish C2 callbacks, exfiltrate data, and evade detection.
additional_info:
Domain Name Resolution (DNS) is a fundamental protocol that enables internet to work. It resolves human readable domains to IP addresses. It is a delegated system where anyone can register a domain under a list of top level domains. Owner of the subdomain is under full control of the owner and the owner can make any subdomain under it. Subdomain owner can even point the subdomain to another DNS server that is under their control, in order to manage records there.

DNS tunneling is a technique where C2 callbacks are established or data is exfiltrated using this delegation. Attacker would register a domain and delegate it toward their resource. Attacker can then make additional subdomains and register them toward C2 servers for periodic callbacks. If server IP gets blocked, then the same domain would simply be pointed toward another C2 IP.

Attacker can also exfiltrate data by embedding it into DNS queries themselves. Recursive and delegated nature of the queries would mean they would reach the server belonging to attacker, regardless if the queries themselves actually resolve to anything.

name: DNSBin
description: Simplistic dropper delivers malware for detonation and includes code for checkin traffic, identifies filename and uses PipeDream API. Source: [Sysopfb](sysopfb.github.io/malware/2019/09/26/Golang-Dropper-With-A-Rat.html)
additional_info:
This dropper is pretty simplistic as most dropper variants are, it’s job is to deliver an onboard piece of malware for detonation. Before getting to the delivery code though the malware has some interesting code for checkin traffic. The first thing it does is get what filename it’s running as and then performs an API request using the service PipeDream.
[Sysopfb](sysopfb.github.io/malware/2019/09/26/Golang-Dropper-With-A-Rat.html)

name: DNSChanger
description: DNSChanger is a malicious Trojan that hijacks DNS settings, redirecting users to fraudulent sites. It poses a threat to online security and privacy.
additional_info:
DNSChanger is a DNS hijacking Trojan.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNSChanger)

name: DOUBLEBACK
description: In cybersecurity, a backdoor allows users to bypass normal security measures and gain high-level access to systems or networks, enabling cybercriminals to steal data, install malware, and hijack devices.
additional_info:
In the world of cybersecurity, a backdoor refers to any method by which authorized and unauthorized users are able to get around normal security measures and gain high level user access (aka root access) on a computer system, network, or software application. Once they're in, cybercriminals can use a backdoor to steal personal and financial data, install additional malware, and hijack devices.
[Malwarebytes] (https://www.malwarebytes.com/backdoor/)

name: DTLoader
description: Loaders are simple tools used by cybercriminals to grab malicious software from servers. Some loaders have additional features like a user-friendly interface or self-healing capabilities, making them more sophisticated.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: Dalbit
description: Dalbit (Moonlight) hacking group has been targeting Korean companies since 2022. Researchers have identified and named the group, known for its activities on the Open Threat Exchange platform.
additional_info: Researchers identified the Dalbit (m00nlight) hacking group that has been targeting Korean companies since 2022 and is known as the “Dalbit” (Moonlight). [Open Threat Exchange](https://otx.alienvault.com/pulse/63ed0da62aae6e8426eeb9ce)

name: DanaBot
description: DanaBot is a persistent and information-stealing malware that focuses on monetizing stolen data rather than demanding instant ransom. It utilizes social engineering in low-volume campaigns and is modular for increased flexibility and robust capabilities.
additional_info:
Proofpoints describes DanaBot as the latest example of malware focused on persistence and stealing useful information that can later be monetized rather than demanding an immediate ransom from victims. The social engineering in the low-volume DanaBot campaigns we have observed so far has been well-crafted, again pointing to a renewed focus on “quality over quantity” in email-based threats. DanaBot’s modular nature enables it to download additional components, increasing the flexibility and robust stealing and remote monitoring capabilities of this banker.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.danabot)

name: DarkCloud
description: Info stealers are malware that collect data on an infected computer and send it to attackers. They steal information like credentials through various methods like keylogging or form grabbing.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

additional_info:
DarkComet is a Windows remote administration tool and backdoor.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0334/)

name: DarkGate
description: DarkGate is a sophisticated commodity loader first seen in 2018, featuring various capabilities such as downloading and executing files, keylogging, and privilege escalation. New versions have been advertised on a Russian eCrime forum since May 2023.
additional_info:
First documented in 2018, DarkGate is a commodity loader with features that include the ability to download and execute files to memory, a Hidden Virtual Network Computing (HVNC) module, keylogging, information-stealing capabilities, and privilege escalation. DarkGate makes use of legitimate AutoIt files and typically runs multiple AutoIt scripts. New versions of DarkGate have been advertised on a Russian language eCrime forum since May 2023.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.darkgate)

additional_info:
DarkHydrus is a threat group that has targeted government agencies and educational institutions in the Middle East since at least 2016. The group heavily leverages open-source tools and custom payloads for carrying out attacks.

name: DarkMe
description: Fuying Lab analyzed activities linking them to the APT group Evilnum. Attackers demonstrated advanced capabilities with new trojan programs DarkMe and PikoloRAT. Multiple attackers participated in the operation.
additional_info:
Through in-depth analysis, Fuying Lab determined that this series of activities is a continuation of the recent attack activities of the APT organization Evilnum (http://blog.nsfocus.net/agentvxapt-evilnum/). Compared with previous activities, the Evilnum attackers inherited their representative attack methods in this operation, but used more diverse attack processes and complex attack components, and enabled two new Trojan programs, DarkMe and PikoloRAT, Demonstrated its high tool development ability, process design ability and rich experience in offensive and defensive confrontation. At the same time, due to the obvious differences in the design ideas and specific implementations of different attack processes, Fuying Lab believes that multiple attackers participated in the operation at the same time.
[Nsfocus](http://blog.nsfocus.net/darkcasino-apt-evilnum/)

name: DarkRadiation
description: A new Bash ransomware named DarkRadiation targets Red Hat and Debian-based Linux distributions, using Telegram API for C&C communication. Scripts are still under development with low detection rates in Virus Total.
additional_info:
A recently discovered Bash ransomware piqued our interest in multiple ways. Upon investigating, we found that the attack chain is fully implemented as a bash script, but it also seems that the scripts are still under development. Most components of this attack mainly target Red Hat and CentOS Linux distributions; however, in some scripts Debian-based Linux distributions are included as well. The worm and ransomware scripts also use the API of the messaging application Telegram for command-and-control (C&C) communication. We also found that most components of this attack have very low detection numbers in Virus Total.
[TrendMicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/21/f/bash-ransomware-darkradiation-targets-red-hat--and-debian-based-linux-distributions.html)

name: DarkSide
description: DarkSide ransomware encrypts files, renames them by adding victim's ID as extension, and drops a ransom note in folders. Designed to make files inaccessible for victims until ransom is paid.
additional_info:
DarkSide was discovered by MalwareHunterTeam. Most of the times malware of this type is designed to make files inaccessible for victims by encrypting them, modify their filenames and generate some ransom note. DarkSide renames encrypted files by appending victim's ID as an extension. For example, it renames "1.jpg" to "1.jpg.d0ac7d95", "2.jpg" to "2.jpg.d0ac7d95", and so forth. It drops the "README.[victim's_ID].TXT" ransom note in every folder that contains encrypted data.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/18504-darkside-ransomware)

The level of control these programs have varies, however, some can allow user-level manipulation of the affected machine. The functionalities of RATs likewise varies and so does the scope of potential misuse. DarkTrack has a broad range of functions/capabilities, which make this Trojan a highly-dangerous piece of software.

The RAT allows attackers to control the infected computer remotely, giving them access to sensitive data and the ability to perform a range of malicious actions.

name: DarkWatchman
description: Prevailion's PACT identified a new RAT called DarkWatchman that uses fileless techniques, a DGA for C2 infrastructure, and operates without writing to disk, posing challenges for security detection tools.
additional_info:
In late November, Prevailion’s Adversarial Counterintelligence Team (PACT) identified what appeared to be a malicious javascript-based Remote Access Trojan (RAT) that uses a robust Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA) to identify its Command and Control (C2) infrastructure and that utilizes novel methods for fileless persistence, on-system activity, and dynamic run-time capabilities like self-updating and recompilation.  This RAT, which PACT refers to by its internal codename “DarkWatchman”, has been observed being distributed by email and represents an evolution in fileless malware techniques, as it uses the registry for nearly all temporary and permanent storage and therefore never writes anything to disk, allowing it to operate beneath or around the detection threshold of most security tools.  PACT has reverse engineered the DGA, dynamically analyzed the malware, investigated the Threat Actor’s (TA) web-based infrastructure, and consolidated the results of our analysis into the following report.
[Prevailion](https://www.prevailion.com/darkwatchman-new-fileless-techniques/)

name: Darkhotel
description: Darkhotel is a South Korean threat group that has targeted victims mainly in East Asia since 2004. They conduct cyber espionage operations through hotel Internet networks, spearphishing, and peer-to-peer networks.
additional_info:
Darkhotel is a suspected South Korean threat group that has targeted victims primarily in East Asia since at least 2004. The group's name is based on cyber espionage operations conducted via hotel Internet networks against traveling executives and other select guests. Darkhotel has also conducted spearphishing campaigns and infected victims through peer-to-peer and file sharing networks.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0012/)

name: Data Exfiltration Toolkit (DET)
description: DET is a proof of concept toolkit for Data Exfiltration using multiple channels simultaneously. It tests network monitoring and DLP solutions against various exfiltration techniques.
additional_info:
DET (is provided AS IS), is a proof of concept to perform Data Exfiltration using either single or multiple channel(s) at the same time.

The idea was to create a generic toolkit to plug any kind of protocol/service to test implmented Network Monitoring and Data Leakage Prevention (DLP) solutions configuration, against different data exfiltration techniques.
[Github](https://github.com/PaulSec/DET)

additional_info:
DazzleSpy is a backdoor-type malware, which our researchers sampled from ESET's WeLiveSecurity community website. After analyzing this piece of malicious software, we concluded that it is capable of receiving/executing commands and extracting files from the infected device. At the time of writing, DazzleSpy had been observed being used for geopolitically-motivated attacks.

name: DealPly
description: DealPly is a browser extension that displays coupon deals on shopping websites to help users save time and money. However, it is considered unwanted due to its deceptive distribution strategy.
additional_info:
The DealPly browser extension displays coupon deals when computer users visit online shopping websites such as eBay, Amazon, etc. Developers of this browser extension claim that this app helps Internet users to save time and money whilst shopping online.

Note that DealPly is not a virus or malware, it is a legitimate browser add-on, however, its deceptive distribution strategy categorizes it as an unwanted browser extension. 

[pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/6982-remove-dealply-adware)

name: Deathhm
description: Trojans are malware disguised as harmless files or links that deceive users into giving unauthorized access to their systems, often leading to theft of personal information or files.
additional_info:
In computing, a Trojan horse (or simply trojan) is any malware which misleads users of its true intent. The term is derived from the Ancient Greek story of the deceptive Trojan Horse that led to the fall of the city of Troy.

Trojans are generally spread by some form of social engineering, for example where a user is duped into executing an email attachment disguised to appear not suspicious, (e.g., a routine form to be filled in), or by clicking on some fake advertisement on social media or anywhere else. Although their payload can be anything, many modern forms act as a backdoor, contacting a controller which can then have unauthorized access to the affected computer. Trojans may allow an attacker to access users' personal information such as banking information, passwords, or personal identity. It can also delete a user's files or infect other devices connected to the network. Ransomware attacks are often carried out using a trojan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trojan_horse_(computing))

name: DecryptmyFiles
description: Ransomware blocks access to files/system and demands payment for access. Evolved from 80s mail to cryptocurrencies. More here: [Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/ransomware/)
additional_info:
Ransom malware, or ransomware, is a type of malware that prevents users from accessing their system or personal files and demands ransom payment in order to regain access. The earliest variants of ransomware were developed in the late 1980s, and payment was to be sent via snail mail. Today, ransomware authors order that payment be sent via cryptocurrency or credit card.
[Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/ransomware/)

Deep Panda is a suspected Chinese threat group known to target many industries, including government, defense, financial, and telecommunications. The intrusion into healthcare company Anthem has been attributed to Deep Panda. This group is also known as Shell Crew, WebMasters, KungFu Kittens, and PinkPanther. Deep Panda also appears to be known as Black Vine based on the attribution of both group names to the Anthem intrusion. [4] Some analysts track Deep Panda and APT19 as the same group, but it is unclear from open source information if the groups are the same.

DeimosC2 server and agents works on, and has been tested on, Windows, Darwin, and Linux. It is entirely written in Golang with a front end written in Vue.js.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user

name: DiamondFox
description: DiamondFox is a modular malware-as-a-service available for purchase on hacker forums. It enables cyber criminals to conduct various attacks without technical knowledge, such as keylogging and DDoS attacks.
additional_info:
DiamondFox is highly modular malware offered as malware-as-a-service, and is for sale on various hacker forums. Therefore, cyber criminals who are willing to use DiamondFox do not necessarily require any technical knowledge to perform their attacks.

Once purchased, this malware can be used to log keystrokes, steal credentials (e.g., usernames, email addresses, passwords), hijack cryptocurrency wallets, perform distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks, and to carry out other malicious tasks.

DiamondFox allows cyber criminals to choose which plug-ins to keep activated and see infection statistics in real-time.
