The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: Dmechant
description: Info stealers are malware that collect data from infected computers to send to attackers. They use various methods including keylogging, browser hooking, and form grabbing to target online banking, social media, and email credentials.
additional_info:
Stealer:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: Doina Loader
description: Loaders are remote access Trojans allowing attackers to control compromised computers. These often lightweight to avoid detection by security technology, some now include advanced features like UI and self-healing capabilities.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.
[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: Dojos
description: Trojan horse malware is disguised as legitimate software to gain unauthorized access to users' systems. It can spy on you, steal data, and allow backdoor access to cyber-criminals.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

threat.name: Dojos - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
Dojos - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Dojos - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter

name: Domino
description: Domino malware is used by cybercriminals to spread Project Nemesis information stealer or Cobalt Strike. It includes a backdoor, loader, and info stealer component, propagated through phishing.
additional_info:
Domino is a type of malware that has been utilized by cybercriminals as early as late February 2023 to disseminate either the Project Nemesis information stealer or Cobalt Strike. The perpetrators achieve their objectives through the use of a Domino backdoor and loader. The Domino campaign is propagated through various methods such as phishing.

The Domino backdoor functions by gathering basic system data, which is then sent to the C2. The C2 returns an AES-encrypted payload, which is usually the Domino loader. The loader, in turn, has an encrypted payload in its resources, which is decrypted using AES. The decrypted payload is a .NET info stealer that identifies itself as the Nemesis Project.

[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/26503-domino-malware)

name: Dora RAT
description: Dora RAT is a malware with reverse shell and file functions. There are two types: standalone executable and injected into explorer.exe process.
additional_info:
Dora RAT is a relatively simple type of malware that supports reverse shell and file download/upload functions. There are two types of Dora RAT: a type that operates as a standalone executable file, and a type that operates by being injected into the explorer, that is, the explorer.exe process.
[Asec](https://asec-ahnlab-com.translate.goog/ko/65495/?_x_tr_sl=auto&_x_tr_tl=en&_x_tr_hl=en&_x_tr_pto=wapp)

name: Dorkbot
description: DorkBot is an old malware distributed through social media and messaging apps. It is a downloader for conducting DDoS attacks and stealing passwords. Sri Lanka, India, and Russia have been most affected.
additional_info:
DorkBot is a known malware that dates back to 2012. It is thought to be distributed via links on social media, instant messaging applications or infected removable media. Although it is a veteran among the notorious malware families, we believe that more networks have been infected with Dorkbot than previously expected, with the most affected countries being Sri Lanka, India and Russia.

The malware essentially serves as a general purpose downloader and launcher of other binary components, mostly modules for conducting DDoS attacks or stealing passwords. The analysis in this case was based on the sample that was observed in multiple infections in the wild in the past month.

[Check Point Research](https://research.checkpoint.com/2018/dorkbot-an-investigation/)

name: DownLoadAdmin
description: This application can lead to unwanted behaviors on PCs, such as adding files at startup, modifying file associations, and installing software that may not have been intended.
additional_info:
This application can affect the quality of your computing experience. We have seen this leading to the following potentially unwanted behaviors on PCs:

Adds files that run at startup
Modifies file associations
Injects into other processes on your system
Injects into browsers
Changes browser settings
Changes browser shortcuts
Installs browser extensions
Adds a local proxy
These applications are most commonly software bundlers or installers for applications such as toolbars, adware, or system optimizers. We have observed this application installing software that you might not have intended on your PC.
[Microsoft](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/wdsi/threats/malware-encyclopedia-description?Name=PUA:Win32/DownloadAdmin&threatId=213945)

name: Downeks
description: A Trojan downloader is a malicious program disguised as legitimate software that downloads and installs harmful files onto a device without the user's consent, intended to cause damage or steal information.
additional_info:
A Trojan downloader is a type of Trojan horse that downloads and installs files, often malicious programs. A Trojan horse is a type of software that looks legitimate but can be malicious in nature. Sometimes these programs can be downloaded onto a device without the user’s knowledge or consent. A Trojan’s purpose is to damage, disrupt, steal, or generally inflict some other harm on your computer and devices.
[Norton](https://us.norton.com/internetsecurity-malware-what-is-a-trojan-downloader.html)

name: Downware
description: Trojan.Win32.Downware is detected by antivirus programs as a Potentially Unwanted Program that may contain adware, install toolbars, or have unclear objectives.
additional_info:
Trojan.Win32.Downware is a specific detection used by Emsisoft Anti-Malware, AVG Antivirus and other antivirus products to indicate and detect a Potentially Unwanted Program.
A potentially unwanted application is a program that contains adware, installs toolbars or has other unclear objectives.
[Malwaretips](https://malwaretips.com/blogs/trojan-win32-downware-removal/)

name: Dreambot
description: In 2014, a variant of Gozi ISFB called Dreambot was created with additional anti-vm checks and new functionality like TOR support. It often uses the Fluxxy fast-flux network.
additional_info:
2010 Gozi v2.0, Gozi ISFB, ISFB, Pandemyia(*)
2014 Dreambot (Gozi ISFB variant)

In 2014, a variant of Gozi ISFB was developed. Mainly, the dropper performs additional anti-vm checks (vmware, vbox, qemu), while the actual bot-dll remains unchanged in most parts. New functionality, such as TOR support, was added though and often, the Fluxxy fast-flux network is used.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.dreambot)

name: Dridex
description: Dridex is a banking Trojan derived from the Bugat/Cridex source code, used for financial gain by attackers.
additional_info: [Dridex](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0384/) is a banking Trojan that has been used for financial gain. Dridex was created from the source code of the Bugat banking trojan (also known as Cridex)

name: Drinik
description: CERT-In warned Indian taxpayers of Drinik malware targeting customers of 27 banks. Drinik, initially an SMS stealer in 2016, evolved into an Android banking trojan by August 2021, posing a significant threat.
additional_info:
In September 2021, the Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) issued a warning about a new malware strain targeting Indian taxpayers and mentioned that customers of around 27 banks were at risk of this attack.
The Threat Actors (TA) behind this campaign were suspected of using Drinik malware. An early variant of Drinik malware was first spotted in 2016 as an SMS stealer. Around August 2021, the malware was observed to be active again, this time evolving into an Android banking trojan.
[Cyble](blog.cyble.com/2022/10/27/drinik-malware-returns-with-advanced-capabilities-targeting-indian-taxpayers/?hss_channel=tw-1141929006603866117)

name: Drokbk
description: Drokbk malware uses .NET, consists of dropper and payload, with limited built-in functionality. It primarily executes commands from C2 server. Detected in 2022 U.S. local government network intrusion.
additional_info:
Drokbk is written in .NET and is made up of a dropper and a payload. The malware has limited built-in functionality and primarily executes additional commands or code from the command and control (C2) server. Early signs of its use in the wild appeared in a February 2022 intrusion at a U.S. local government network.
[Secureworks](https://www.secureworks.com/blog/drokbk-malware-uses-github-as-dead-drop-resolver)

name: DuckLogs
description: DuckLogs is a malicious software with information theft, remote control, keylogging capabilities. It is sold on the dark web for $19.99/month or $69.99 one-time fee.
additional_info:
DuckLogs is malware that includes information-stealing, remote control, keylogging, and other features. It allows cybercriminals to steal sensitive information, inject additional malware, and perform other malicious activities.
DuckLogs is Malware-as-a-Service (MaaS). Cybercriminals behind DuckLogs sell it to other crooks for $19.99 per month, $39.99 per three months, or a $69.99 one-time fee.
[PCrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/25456-ducklogs-malware)

name: Ducktail Stealer
description: Samples associated with the DUCKTAIL operation since late 2021 were written in .NET Core and compiled using its single file feature, bundling all dependencies into a single executable. This use of .NET Core in malware is uncommon.
additional_info: Since late 2021, samples associated with the DUCKTAIL operation were exclusively written in .NET Core and were compiled using its single file feature. This feature bundles all dependent libraries and files into a single executable, including the main assembly2. The usage of .NET Core and its single-file feature is not commonly seen in malware. [WithSecure](https://labs.withsecure.com/content/dam/labs/docs/WithSecure_Research_DUCKTAIL.pdf)

name: DynamicRAT
description: DynamicRAT is a malware spread through email attachments, giving attackers remote control of systems. It has features like data theft, hardware control, remote actions, DDoS attacks, and evasion tactics to avoid detection.
additional_info:
DynamicRAT is a malware that is spread via email attachments and compromises the security of computer systems. Once running on a device, DynamicRAT establishes a persistent presence and gives attackers complete remote control. Its features include sensitive data exfiltration, hardware control, remote action, and the ability to perform DDoS attacks. In addition, DynamicRAT uses evasion and persistence techniques to evade detection and analysis by security solutions.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/jar.dynamicrat)

name: Dyre
description: Dyre is a banking Trojan used for financial gain. It can steal credentials and perform man-in-the-middle attacks on online banking services.
additional_info:
Dyre is a banking Trojan that has been used for financial gain.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0024/)

name: ELF
description: Malware actors alter legitimate binaries to create trojans for system exploitation. The ELF file format is widely used for executable files, object code, shared libraries, and core dumps in Unix systems.
additional_info:
Malware actors often try to infect/add specific code to legitimate binaries in an effort to trojanize (generate segment-padded trojans) those binaries and take advantage of allowed executable on the system.

In computing, the Executable and Linkable Format[citation needed] (ELF, formerly named Extensible Linking Format), is a common standard file format for executable files, object code, shared libraries, and core dumps. First published in the specification for the application binary interface (ABI) of the Unix operating system version named System V Release 4 (SVR4), and later in the Tool Interface Standard, it was quickly accepted among different vendors of Unix systems. In 1999, it was chosen as the standard binary file format for Unix and Unix-like systems on x86 processors by the 86open project.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Executable_and_Linkable_Format)

name: ERMAC
description: Version 2.0 of ERMAC Android banking trojan targets 467 apps, stealing login credentials and crypto wallets for financial fraud. Sold on darknet for $5,000/month due to increased features and popularity.
additional_info:
The ERMAC Android banking trojan has released version 2.0, increasing the number of applications targeted from 378 to 467, covering a much wider range of apps to steal account credentials and crypto wallets.

The goal of the trojan is to send stolen login credentials to threat actors, who then use them to take control of other people’s banking and cryptocurrency accounts and conduct financial or other forms of fraud.

ERMAC is currently sold to members of darknet sites at a subscription rate of $5,000 per month, which is $2k over the price tag of the first version, reflecting the upgrade in features and its popularity.
[Bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-ermac-20-android-malware-steals-accounts-wallets-from-467-apps/)

name: Earth Berberoka
description: New APT group Earth Berberoka (aka GamblingPuppet) targets gambling websites, operating on Windows, Linux, and macOS platforms using malware historically linked to Chinese-speaking individuals. Discovery by TrendMicro.
additional_info:
Recently discovered a new advanced persistent threat (APT) group that we have dubbed Earth Berberoka (aka GamblingPuppet). Based on our analysis, this group targets gambling websites. Our investigation has also uncovered that Earth Berberoka targets the Windows, Linux, and macOS platforms, and uses malware families that have been historically attributed to Chinese-speaking individuals.
[TrendMicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/22/d/new-apt-group-earth-berberoka-targets-gambling-websites-with-old.html)

threat.name: Earth Berberoka - MITRE - Ingress Tool Transfer
Earth Berberoka - MITRE - System Information Discovery
Earth Berberoka - MITRE - Command and Control
Earth Berberoka - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: Earth Hundun Group
description: Earth Hundun is a cyberespionage group targeting Asia-Pacific technology and government sectors using the complex Waterbear malware. The latest version, Deuterbear, includes anti-detection features.
additional_info:
Earth Hundun is a cyberespionage-motivated threat actor that has been active for several years in the Asia-Pacific region, targeting the technology and government sectors.
The group has been known for employing several tools and techniques, including Waterbear, a malware entity that has had over 10 versions since 2009.
Waterbear is known for its complexity, as it uses a number of evasion mechanisms to minimize the chance of detection and analysis. Succeeding versions have added enhancements that make it even more troublesome to deal with.
In 2022, Earth Hundun began using the latest version of Waterbear — also known as Deuterbear — which has several changes, including anti-memory scanning and decryption routines, that make us consider it a different malware entity from the original Waterbear.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_no/research/24/d/earth-hundun-waterbear-deuterbear.html)

name: Earth Kasha
description: Earth Kasha utilizes various TTPs to exploit vulnerabilities in enterprise products such as Array AG, Proself, and FortiOS/FortiProxy, deploying backdoors like Cobalt Strike and NOOPDOOR for network persistence.
additional_info:
Earth Kasha has also employed different Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) in the Initial Access phase, which now exploits public-facing applications such as SSL-VPN and file storage services. We observed that vulnerabilities of enterprise products, such as Array AG (CVE-2023-28461), Proself (CVE-2023-45727) and FortiOS/FortiProxy (CVE-2023-27997), were abused in the wild. Earth Kasha was changing these vulnerabilities to abuse from time to time. After gaining access, they deployed several backdoors in the victim's network to achieve persistence. These include Cobalt Strike, LODEINFO, and the newly discovered NOOPDOOR, which we will describe later.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/24/k/lodeinfo-campaign-of-earth-kasha.html)

name: Earth Krahang APT
description: Chinese espionage group Earth Krahang breaches 70 organizations including 48 government entities across 23 countries using standard tactics, possibly a wing of iSoon contracted by the CCP, employing open source tools and social engineering.
additional_info:
A previously unidentified Chinese espionage group has managed to breach at least 70 organizations across 23 countries, including 48 in the government space, despite using rather standard-fare tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs).

"Earth Krahang" doesn't seem to be a high-level military APT. In a new report, researchers from Trend Micro suggested that it may be one wing of iSoon, a private hack-for-hire operation contracted by the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). And fitting such a cybercrime operation, rather than employing ultra-sophisticated malware and stealth tactics, it uses an arsenal of largely open source and well-documented tools, plus one-day vulnerabilities and standard social engineering, to defeat its targets.

Despite this, its list of victims rivals that of the likes of Volt Typhoon, BlackTech, and Mustang Panda.

[Darkreading](https://www.darkreading.com/threat-intelligence/chinese-apt-earth-krahang-compromised-48-gov-orgs-5-continents)

name: Earth Minotaur
description: Earth Minotaur uses instant messaging apps to trick victims into clicking malicious links, disguising themselves as various characters to increase success. The links lead to MOONSHINE exploit kit servers installing backdoors on devices.
additional_info:
Earth Minotaur sends carefully crafted messages via instant messaging apps to entice victims to click an embedded malicious link. They disguise themselves as different characters on chats to increase the success of their social engineering attacks. The malicious links led victims to MOONSHINE exploit kit servers, which install backdoors on the victims' devices.
[TrendMicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/24/l/earth-minotaur.html)

name: Easy Stealer
description: First advertisement for "Easy Stealer" appeared on XSS.is, claiming to be one of the best products on the market with a user-friendly panel, custom File Grabber, and Dynamic Loader.
additional_info:
On July 23rd, the first advertisement for “Easy Stealer” appeared on the Russian criminal forum, XSS.is. The post was created by the alias “EasyStealer”, claiming to be “one of the best products on the market, supported by an experienced team”. The advertisement details a “User-friendly panel”, custom File Grabber, and Dynamic Loader. 
[Bridewell](https://www.bridewell.com/insights/blogs/detail/uncovering-the-easy-stealer-infostealer)

name: Ekipa RAT
description: Ekipa RAT is a malicious program sold for $4500 on hacker forums, used by threat actors to remotely control infected computers, gather data, and perform malicious activities.
additional_info:
Ekipa is the name of a Remote Administration Trojan (RAT) sold on a hacker forum for $4500. Threat actors use RATs to perform remote malicious activities on infected computers. Ekipa RAT can gather system information, manage files, and perform other tasks. It should be removed from the infected computers immediately.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/25619-ekipa-rat)

name: ElectroRAT
description: New RAT malware named ElectroRAT discovered targeting Windows, Linux, and macOS users to empty cryptocurrency wallets. Written in Golang, it has been part of a campaign since the start of 2020.
additional_info:
Security researchers have discovered a new remote access trojan (RAT) used to empty the cryptocurrency wallets of thousands of Windows, Linux, and macOS users.

Named ElectroRAT after being discovered in December, the cross-platform RAT malware is written in Golang and it was used as part of a campaign that has been targeting cryptocurrency users since the start of 2020.
[Bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/cross-platform-electrorat-malware-drains-cryptocurrency-wallets/)

name: ElectronBot
description: Electron Bot is a malware discovered by Check Point Research targeting Windows devices to gain remote access and execute commands, distributed through applications on the Microsoft Store.
additional_info:
Electron Bot is the name of the malware that has been discovered by Check Point Research. We have learned that Electron Bot is used to gain remote access to computers and execute various commands. It is distributed via various applications on the Microsoft Store (Electron Bot targets Windows devices).
[PCrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/23162-electron-bot-malware)

name: ElegyRAT
description: ElegyRAT is a Remote Access Tool based on AsyncRAT, allowing remote monitoring and control of computers. It bypasses antivirus software for research purposes only, not for illegal use.
additional_info: ElegyRAT is a Remote Access Tool (Be Based On AsyncRAT) designed to remotely monitor and control other computers through a secure encrypted connection.It mainly rewrites the task startup items and individual plug-ins to facilitate bypass AV Static killing and behavior interception.(As of 20201231 Bypass 360sd HuoRong Kaspersky Microsoft defender a series of antivirus software,for learning research only,do not use for illegal purposes.)

name: Elusive Stealer
description: Elusive is an information stealer malware that targets sensitive data like log-in credentials, credit card details, and cryptocurrency wallets by extracting and exfiltrating them from systems and applications.
additional_info:
Elusive is the name of an information stealer. This malware operates by extracting and exfiltrating vulnerable data from systems and installed applications. Elusive targets log-in credentials, credit card details, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sensitive information.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/29163-elusive-stealer)

name: Elysium Stealer
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data from infected computers to send to attackers. They acquire data through browsers, web injection scripts, form grabbing, keylogging, and stealing saved passwords.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: Emmenhtal Loader
description: A shortcut file triggers a chain of events, leading to the execution of malicious PowerShell scripts, including the downloading and running of Infostealers like CryptBot or Lumma stealer.
additional_info:
The shortcut file launches an embedded PowerShell script which spawns an execution of the LOLBIN mshta.exe to read an HTA concatenated to a legitimate PE file downloaded from an attacker-controlled C2. The PE file is a legitimate Windows binary except that it is padded with HTA data that embeds a malicious JavaScript code. Once interpreted and executed by mshta.exe, the JavaScript decodes and runs a PowerShell decrypter script. The latter decrypts an obfuscated PowerShell loader which finally downloads and runs either CryptBot or Lumma stealer.
[Ornagecyberdefense](https://www.orangecyberdefense.com/global/blog/cert-news/emmenhtal-a-little-known-loader-distributing-commodity-infostealers-worldwide)

name: Emotet
description: Emotet is a versatile malware used for downloading other malicious software, especially targeting the banking sector. It first appeared in 2014 and is often linked to TrickBot and IcedID.
additional_info:
Emotet is a modular malware variant which is primarily used as a downloader for other malware variants such as TrickBot and IcedID. Emotet first emerged in June 2014 and has been primarily used to target the banking sector.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0367/)

name: Emptiness
description: Emptiness, written in Golang, evolves from basic DDoS attacks to include ssh scanning for self-propagation. Constantly changing C2 communication protocols make it difficult to track for security researchers.
additional_info:
Emptiness is written by Golang and supports both Windows and Linux. Our further analysis reveal its iterative evolution: the early version Emptiness was only armed with DDoS function and was propagated through Mirai.shiina variant botnet that had been run by the same author for a long time. The latter version Emptiness was added with ssh scan to enable self-propagation. Besides, C2 communication protocols of both Emptiness and Mirai.shiina kept changing constantly, which increases the difficulty of being tracked by security researchers.
[Netlab360](https://blog.netlab.360.com/emptiness-a-new-evolving-botnet/)

threat.name: Emptiness - MITRE - Network Denial of Service
Emptiness - MITRE - Endpoint Denial of Service

name: Enigma
description: Downloader known as Enigma Loader is used to stage the execution of the Enigma Stealer malware. Identified by Trend Micro as a threat, it is designed to deliver malicious payloads.
additional_info:
According to Trend Micro, this is a downloader, dedicated to stage execution of a second stage malware called Enigma Stealer.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.enigma_loader)

name: Epsilon Stealer
description: Info stealers are malware that collect data from infected computers and send it to attackers. They use various methods like browser hooking, web injection scripts, keylogging, and form grabbing to steal credentials and passwords.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: Erbium
description: Erbium is a type of malicious software called a stealer, designed to extract sensitive data from infected devices. It was discovered by researchers while inspecting malware-selling hotspots.
additional_info:
Erbium is a piece of malicious software classified as a stealer. Malware within this category is designed to extract vulnerable data from infected devices. Our researchers discovered Erbium while inspecting malware-selling hotspots.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/24546-erbium-stealer)

name: EternalBlue
description: The SMBv1 server in several Windows versions allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via crafted packets. EternalBlue is used for lateral movement.
additional_info:
The SMBv1 server in Microsoft Windows Vista SP2; Windows Server 2008 SP2 and R2 SP1; Windows 7 SP1; Windows 8.1; Windows Server 2012 Gold and R2; Windows RT 8.1; and Windows 10 Gold, 1511, and 1607; and Windows Server 2016 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via crafted packets, aka "Windows SMB Remote Code Execution Vulnerability".

This vulnerability is different from those described in CVE-2017-0143, CVE-2017-0145, CVE-2017-0146, and CVE-2017-0148.

EternalBlue is heavily used for lateral movement.
Source: [MITRE](https://cve.mitre.org/cgi-bin/cvename.cgi?name=CVE-2017-0144)

threat.name: EternalBlue - MITRE - Exploitation of Remote Services

name: Eternity
description: Eternity is a malicious malware family discovered by Cyble Research Labs, actively sold on the Web. Its developers use Telegram for sales, support, and customization, also employing it as a C&C server and proliferation tool.
additional_info:
Discovered by Cyble Research Labs, Eternity is the name of a malware family. Actively sold on the Web, Eternity's developers use the Telegram IM (Instant Messaging) service to sell their malicious wares, as well as provide support and customization to buyers. Telegram can also be employed by the attackers using Eternity programs as their C&C (Command and Control) server and proliferation tool.

Currently, this malware family consists of a stealer, worm, miner, clipper, ransomware, and DDoS bot.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/23853-eternity-malware)

threat.name: Eternity - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Eternity - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
Eternity - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
Eternity - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Eternity - MITRE - Exfiltration Over C2 Channel

name: EtterSilent
description: Intel 471 discovered a popular malicious document builder called "EtterSilent" used by cybercrime groups. Its updates help avoid detection, showcasing how ease of use drives the cybercrime economy.
additional_info:
The cybercrime underground often mimics behaviors that we see in everyday facets of life. Intel 471’s latest discovery is an example of one of these patterns: when a product takes off in the marketplace, users will rush to obtain it and find unique ways to use it in order to fit their needs.

The latest “product” is a malicious document builder, known in the underground as “EtterSilent,” that Intel 471 has seen leveraged by various cybercrime groups. As it has grown in popularity, it has constantly been updated in order to avoid detection. Used in conjunction with other forms of malware, it’s a prime example of how ease of use and a concentration of skill sets leads to a commoditization of the cybercrime economy.
[Intel471](https://intel471.com/blog/ettersilent-maldoc-builder-macro-trickbot-qbot)

threat.name: EtterSilent - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
EtterSilent - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious Link

name: EvasivePanda
description: ESET researchers discovered cyberespionage campaign targeting Tibetans using a watering hole and supply-chain compromise to deliver malicious software named Nightdoor for Windows and macOS.
additional_info:
ESET researchers discovered a cyberespionage campaign that, since at least September 2023, has been victimizing Tibetans through a targeted watering hole (also known as a strategic web compromise), and a supply-chain compromise to deliver trojanized installers of Tibetan language translation software. The attackers aimed to deploy malicious downloaders for Windows and macOS to compromise website visitors with MgBot and a backdoor that, to the best of our knowledge, has not been publicly documented yet; we have named it Nightdoor.
[Eset](https://www.welivesecurity.com/en/eset-research/evasive-panda-leverages-monlam-festival-target-tibetans/)

name: EvilNominatus
description: A malicious BAT file associated with the EvilNominatus ransomware, originating from Iran, was uploaded to VirusTotal and linked to a young Iranian developer who boasted about its creation on Twitter.
additional_info:
As part of our monitoring of malicious files in current use, we detected a malicious BAT file that was
uploaded to VirusTotal from Iran. This file executes a ransomware that we associated with the
EvilNominatus ransomware, initially exposed at the end of 2021. It seems that the ransomware's developer is a young Iranian, who bragged about its development on Twitter.
[Clearskysec](https://www.clearskysec.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/EvilNominatus_Ransomware_7.4.22.pdf)

threat.name: EvilNominatus - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
EvilNominatus - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
EvilNominatus - MITRE - Create or Modify System Process
EvilNominatus - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: EvilProxy
description: Resecurity discovered a new Phishing-as-a-Service named EvilProxy on the Dark Web, also known as Moloch. It is connected to a phishing kit developed by underground actors targeting financial institutions and e-commerce.
additional_info:
Resecurity has recently identified a new Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) called EvilProxy advertised in the Dark Web. On some sources the alternative name is Moloch, which has some connection to a phishing-kit developed by several notable underground actors who targeted the financial institutions and e-commerce sector before.
[Resecurity]()

name: Evilnum
description: ESET analyzed operations of Evilnum, APT group behind Evilnum malware targeting financial technology companies. Group's toolset includes custom malware and tools from Malware-as-a-Service provider Golden Chickens.
additional_info:
ESET has analyzed the operations of Evilnum, the APT group behind the Evilnum malware previously seen in attacks against financial technology companies. While said malware has been seen in the wild since at least 2018 and documented previously, little has been published about the group behind it and how it operates. The group’s targets remain fintech companies, but its toolset and infrastructure have evolved and now consist of a mix of custom, homemade malware combined with tools purchased from Golden Chickens, a Malware-as-a-Service (MaaS) provider whose infamous customers include FIN6 and Cobalt Group.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/evilnum)

name: Expiro
description: Expiro is a polymorphic file infector that inserts unique viral code into each infected file while maintaining the same malicious function. It can inject code into web pages and steal login credentials.
additional_info:
Expiro is a family of polymorphic file infectors, meaning that the viral code inserted into each infected file is unique, while still maintaining the same malicious functionality.
The viral payload includes functionality to inject malicious code into web pages visited as well as steal login credentials.
[Sophos](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/threat-center/threat-analyses/viruses-and-spyware/W32~Expiro-H/detailed-analysis)

threat.name: Expiro - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: Exploited Web Application
description: Public websites are frequently targeted by automated attacks aiming to exploit known vulnerabilities. Once successful, attackers take over the site to use as a botnet node or distribute malware.
additional_info: Public facing websites are a constant target for attackers. These attacks are mostly automated and attempt to exploit known vulnerabilities for popular software. Once successful, the site is taken over by malicious actor and is usually used as botnet node to use the server resources for attacking third party, or as malware distribution platform.

name: FFDroider
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data from infected computers to send to attackers. They steal credentials from online services using methods such as browser hooking, web injection, and keylogging.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

threat.name: FFDroider - MITRE - Compromise Accounts: Social Media Accounts

name: FIN12
description: UNC1878 is a financially motivated threat actor that deploys RYUK ransomware. After a decrease in activity over the summer, they have returned in the fall to resume their operations.
additional_info:
UNC1878 is a financially motivated threat actor that monetizes network access via the deployment of RYUK ransomware. Earlier this year, Mandiant published a blog on a fast-moving adversary deploying RYUK ransomware, UNC1878. Shortly after its release, there was a significant decrease in observed UNC1878 intrusions and RYUK activity overall almost completely vanishing over the summer. But beginning in early fall, Mandiant has seen a resurgence of RYUK along with TTP overlaps indicating that UNC1878 has returned from the grave and resumed their operations.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/unc1878)

threat.name: FIN12 - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact

name: FIN7
description: FIN7 is a financially-motivated threat group targeting US retail, restaurant, and hospitality sectors since mid-2015. They use point-of-sale malware and at times operate through a front company called Combi Security.
additional_info:
FIN7 is a financially-motivated threat group that has primarily targeted the U.S. retail, restaurant, and hospitality sectors since mid-2015. They often use point-of-sale malware. A portion of FIN7 was run out of a front company called Combi Security. FIN7 is sometimes referred to as Carbanak Group, but these appear to be two groups using the same Carbanak malware and are therefore tracked separately. 
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0046/)

name: FIN8
description: FIN8 is a financially motivated threat group that carries out spearphishing attacks targeting retail, restaurant, and hospitality industries. More information available on MITRE's website.
additional_info:
FIN8 is a financially motivated threat group known to launch tailored spearphishing campaigns targeting the retail, restaurant, and hospitality industries. 
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0061/)

name: FROZENBARENTS APT
description: Russian cyber group FROZENBARENTS stays active with GRU Unit 74455, targeting Ukraine through intelligence, information operations, and data leaks via Telegram. Ukraine remains a top focus for them.
additional_info: FROZENBARENTS (aka Sandworm), a group attributed to Russian Armed Forces’ Main Directorate of the General Staff (GRU) Unit 74455, continues to focus heavily on the war in Ukraine with campaigns spanning intelligence collection, IO, and leaking hacked data through Telegram. [Google](https://blog.google/threat-analysis-group/ukraine-remains-russias-biggest-cyber-focus-in-2023/)

name: FRat
description: A RAT using Node.js, Sails, and Socket.IO to gather data on a victim. Visit Malpedia for more details on win.frat.
additional_info:
A RAT employing Node.js, Sails, and Socket.IO to collect information on a target.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.frat)

threat.name: FRat - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell
FRat - MITRE - Exfiltration Over C2 Channel

name: FTCode
description: Ransomware ftcode encrypts files, changes extension to .FTCODE, demands ransom for decryption key. Targets Italy, impersonates telecom provider for payments. Info from Malpedia.
additional_info:
The malware ftcode is a ransomware which encrypts files and changes their extension into .FTCODE. It later asks for a ransom in order to release the decryption key, mandatory to recover your files. It is infamous for attacking Italy pretending to be a notorious telecom provider asking for due payments.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/ps1.ftcode)

threat.name: FTCode - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
FTCode - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
FTCode - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact

name: Fabookie
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs used by hackers to access systems. They can spy on users, steal data, and provide backdoor access to cybercriminals through social engineering.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

threat.name: Fabookie - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
Fabookie - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: Facefish
description: Facefish is a backdoor for Linux platform consisting of Dropper and Rootkit modules. It steals user credentials, uploads device information, bounces shell, and executes arbitrary commands.
additional_info:
Facefish consists of 2 parts, Dropper and Rootkit, and its main function is determined by the Rootkit module, which works at the Ring3 layer and is loaded using the LD_PRELOAD feature to steal user login credentials by hooking ssh/sshd program related functions, and it also supports some backdoor functions. Therefore, Facefish can be characterized as a backdoor for Linux platform.

The main functions of Facefish are

Upload device information
Stealing user credentials
Bounce Shell
Execute arbitrary commands
[netlab360](https://blog.netlab.360.com/ssh_stealer_facefish_en/)

threat.name: Facefish - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter

name: Fadok
description: Worm.Win32.Fadok.AA infiltrates systems through malicious downloads or dropped files, spreading through networks to cause disruption, steal data, or install additional malware.
additional_info: This Worm arrives on a system as a file dropped by other malware or as a file downloaded unknowingly by users when visiting malicious sites. [Trend Micro](https://www.trendmicro.com/vinfo/us/threat-encyclopedia/malware/worm.win32.fadok.aa)

name: Fake Browser
description: Attackers infect enterprise networks with malware by promoting fake browser updates on hacked websites, leading to the installation of banking trojans and later ransomware encryption on compromised networks.
additional_info:
Attackers are utilizing hacked web sites that promote fake browser updates to infect targets with banking trojans. In some cases, post exploitation toolkits are later executed to encrypt the compromised network with ransomware.

Between May and September 2019, FireEye has conducted multiple incident response cases where enterprise customers were infected with malware through fake browser updates.

Hacked sites would display these "fakeupdates" through JavaScript alerts that state the user is using an old version of a web browser and that they should download an offered "update" to keep the browser running "smoothly and securely".

[bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/fake-browser-updates-infect-enterprises-with-ransomware-bankers/)

name: Fake LogMeInRescue
description: Trojans are malware that deceive users and can be spread through social engineering. They allow unauthorized access to computers and can steal personal information or carry out ransomware attacks.
additional_info:
In computing, a Trojan horse (or simply trojan) is any malware which misleads users of its true intent. The term is derived from the Ancient Greek story of the deceptive Trojan Horse that led to the fall of the city of Troy.

Trojans are generally spread by some form of social engineering, for example where a user is duped into executing an email attachment disguised to appear not suspicious, (e.g., a routine form to be filled in), or by clicking on some fake advertisement on social media or anywhere else. Although their payload can be anything, many modern forms act as a backdoor, contacting a controller which can then have unauthorized access to the affected computer. Trojans may allow an attacker to access users' personal information such as banking information, passwords, or personal identity. It can also delete a user's files or infect other devices connected to the network. Ransomware attacks are often carried out using a trojan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trojan_horse_(computing))

name: Fake Service
description: Phishing involves scammers posing as familiar companies to obtain personal information. Consumers should avoid responding to suspicious emails requesting personal or financial details to prevent falling victim to these scams.
additional_info:
Phishing is a type of online scam that targets consumers by sending them an e-mail that appears to be from a well-known source – an internet service provider, a bank, or a mortgage company, for example. It asks the consumer to provide personal identifying information. Then a scammer uses the information to open new accounts, or invade the consumer’s existing accounts. There are several tips that consumers can follow to avoid phishing scams, such as not responding to e-mails or pop-up messages that ask for personal or financial information.

[FTC](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/topics/identity-theft/phishing-scams)

name: FakeAV
description: FAKEAV variants are spread through compromised websites, malicious links, search results, social media posts, and ads. They can also be downloaded by other malware.
additional_info:
FAKEAV variants arrive on systems via compromised websites, spammed malicious links; poisoned search results that lead to FAKEAV download pages, malicious posts on social networking sites, and malicious advertisements. They may also be downloaded by other malware.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/vinfo/us/threat-encyclopedia/malware/fakeav)

threat.name: FakeAV - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: FakeAdsBlock
description: Malwarebytes discovered Android/Trojan.FakeAdsBlock, a deceptive adware installer posing as an ad blocker for Android devices. It is highly stealthy and must be removed immediately.
additional_info:
Android/Trojan.FakeAdsBlock is Malwarebytes’ detection name for an extremely stealthy adware installer pretending to be an ad blocker for Android devices.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/detections/android-trojan-fakeadsblock/)

name: FakeCalls
description: Voice phishing in South Korea led to $600 million in losses in 2020, with 170,000 victims from 2016 to 2020. Check Point found 2500 FakeCalls malware samples with unique evasions.
additional_info:
Voice phishing attacks have a long history in the South Korean market. According to the report published on the South Korean government website, financial losses due to voice phishing constituted approximately 600 million USD in 2020, with the number of victims reaching as many as 170,000 people in the period from 2016 to 2020.

Check Point Research discovered more than 2500 samples of the FakeCalls malware that used a variety of combinations of mimicked financial organizations and implemented anti-analysis (also called evasions) techniques. The malware developers paid special attention to the protection of their malware, using several unique evasions that we had not previously seen in the wild.
[Check Point Research](https://research.checkpoint.com/2023/south-korean-android-banking-menace-fakecalls/)

name: FakeInst
description: Malware imitates legitimate app installation process, sends SMS messages to premium-rate numbers while showing fake window to users.
additional_info:
Malware in this family shows a fake window to the user, imitating the installation process of a legitimate app. During the fake installation, the malware secretly sends SMS messages to premium-rate short numbers.
[Kaspersky](https://threats.kaspersky.com/en/threat/Trojan-SMS.AndroidOS.FakeInst/)

threat.name: FakeInst - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: FakeM
description: Some versions of FAKEM RAT imitate Windows Messenger and Yahoo Messenger traffic, while others disguise themselves as regular web traffic. The malware has been used in attacks since September 2009.
additional_info:
Some variants attempt to disguise network traffic to look like Windows® Messenger and Yahoo!® Messenger traffic. Another variant tries to make the content of its traffic look like ordinary web traffic. The FAKEM RAT appears to have been actively used in attacks since September 2009. 
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/13/a/hiding-in-plain-sight-the-fakem-remote-access-trojan.html?utm_source=twitterfeed&utm_medium=twitter&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+Anti-MalwareBlog+%28Trendlabs+Security+Intelligence+Blog%29)

name: FakeWallet
description: Malicious apps are targeting Chinese users to steal cryptocurrency funds. The release of source code may lead to more copycat attackers spreading to other markets, but it is unclear if it was intentional.
additional_info:
The main goal of these malicious apps is to steal users’ funds and until now we have seen this scheme mainly targeting Chinese users. As cryptocurrencies are gaining popularity, we expect these techniques to spread into other markets. This is further supported by the public sharing, in November 2021, of the source code of the front-end and back-end distribution website, including the recompiled APK and IPA files. We found this code on at least five websites, where it was shared for free, and thus expect to see more copycat attackers. From the posts we found, it is difficult to determine whether it was shared intentionally or if it leaked.
[Welivesecurity](https://www.welivesecurity.com/2022/03/24/crypto-malware-patched-wallets-targeting-android-ios-devices/)

threat.name: FakeWallet - MITRE - Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
FakeWallet - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious Link
FakeWallet - MITRE - Exploitation for Client Execution

name: Fallout
description: New exploit kit discovered in August 2018 targets Adobe Flash Player and Windows vulnerabilities. Successful infection allows attacker to download more malware onto victim's computer.
additional_info:
The exploit kit was discovered in August 2018 and takes advantage of flaws in Adobe Flash Player and Microsoft Windows. A successful infection will allow the attacker to download additional malware onto the victims computer.
[McAfee](https://www.mcafee.com/enterprise/en-us/threat-center/threat-landscape-dashboard/exploit-kits-details.fallout-exploit-kit.html)

name: Fenix Botnet
description: Metabase Q has discovered the Fenix botnet targeting tax-paying individuals in Mexico and Chile during tax season. Attackers redirect victims to fake government portals, prompting them to download malware.
additional_info:
The Threat Intelligence team at Metabase Q has recently uncovered a local group that created a new botnet self-proclaimed as “Fenix,” which specifically targets users accessing government services, particularly tax-paying individuals in Mexico and Chile. This botnet takes advantage of the tax season in both countries, which occurred last April. In their malicious campaign, the attackers redirect victims to fraudulent websites that mimic the official portals of the Servicio de Administración Tributaria (SAT) in Mexico and the Servicio de Impuestos Internos (SII) in Chile. These fake websites prompt users to download a supposed security tool, claiming it will enhance their portal navigation safety. However, unbeknownst to the victims, this download actually installs the initial stage of malware, ultimately enabling the theft of sensitive information such as credentials.
[Metabaseq](https://www.metabaseq.com/fenix-botnet/)

name: Ficker Stealer
description: FickerStealer is an advanced information stealer sold on underground forums as MaaS. Prices range from $90 to $900 depending on subscription time. It steals sensitive data and can act as a File Grabber and Downloader.
additional_info: FickerStealer is an exceptional information stealer, first seen in the wild in August 2020. This info-stealer was sold on underground forums as a MaaS (Malware-as-a-service) by a seller named @ficker (@ficker_sup on Telegram). The average price of an information stealer is $200, and it varies depending on their additional capabilities and the subscription time for the service. The price for FickerStealer cost ranges between $90 up to $900, and it only depends on the subscription time to the service. The malware is used to steal sensitive information, including login credentials, credit card information, cryptocurrency wallets and browser information from applications such as WinSCP, Discord, Google Chrome, Electrum, etc. It does all that by implementing a different approach than other stealers (we’ll cover it later). Additionally, FickerStealer can function as a File Grabber and collect additional files from the compromised machine, and it can act as a Downloader to download and execute several second-stage malware. [CyberArk](https://www.cyberark.com/resources/threat-research-blog/fickerstealer-a-new-rust-player-in-the-market)

name: Fickle Stealer
description: New malware called Fickle Stealer, written in Rust, aims to steal sensitive information from compromised hosts. It is distributed through various attack chains, some using PowerShell to bypass UAC.
additional_info:
A new Rust-based information stealer malware called Fickle Stealer has been observed being delivered via multiple attack chains with the goal of harvesting sensitive information from compromised hosts.

Fortinet FortiGuard Labs said it's aware of four different distribution methods -- namely VBA dropper, VBA downloader, link downloader, and executable downloader -- with some of them using a PowerShell script to bypass User Account Control (UAC) and execute Fickle Stealer.
[Thehackernews](https://thehackernews.com/2024/06/new-rust-based-fickle-malware-uses.html)

name: FileTour
description: Adware.FileTour is a family of Russian-origin adware bundlers that target Windows systems, identified by Malwarebytes as generic detection name for this type of malware.
additional_info:
Adware.FileTour is Malwarebytes’ generic detection name for a family that consists mostly of adware bundlers of Russian origin that target Windows systems.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/detections/adware-filetour/)

name: Filecoder
description: Filecoders/Ransomware infections encrypt personal and data files on workstations and mapped shared drives. It may appear to spread through a network, but it is contained to individual machines.
additional_info:
Filecoders/Ransomware are infections that encrypt personal and data files. Typically a workstation is infected, and then the filecoder/ransomware will attempt to encrypt any mapped shared drives. This can make this infection seem as though it is spreading through your network when it is not.
[Eset](https://support.eset.com/en/kb3433-best-practices-to-protect-against-filecoder-ransomware-malware#)

threat.name: Filecoder - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Filecoder - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Filecoder - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Filez
description: Loaders are evolving remote access Trojans that allow attackers to control compromised computers. They can have user-friendly features and serve as a marketplace for selling bots to potential clients.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.

[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: FinderBot
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by cyber-thieves to steal data and gain access to systems. Users are tricked into downloading them through social engineering tactics.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

threat.name: FinderBot - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
FinderBot - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious Link
FinderBot - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: FireEye
description: FireEye/Mandiant Red Team disclosed a security breach on Dec 8, 2020, where state-sponsored attackers stole their tools, leading to exposure of various Red Team tools.
additional_info:
Security breach at FireEye/Mandiant Red Team  - Dec 8 2020

FireEye/Mandiant Red Team announced on Dec 8th 2020 that highly sophisticated state-sponsored adversary stole FireEye Red Team tools. Consequence is involving exposure of numerous Red Team tools.
[Zdnet](https://www.zdnet.com/article/fireeye-one-of-the-worlds-largest-security-firms-discloses-security-breach/)

name: Firebird
description: Remote Access Trojans provide covert surveillance or unauthorized access to a victim PC. They collect keystrokes, passwords, screenshots, and allow an attacker to gain remote access for various malicious activities.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/remote-access-trojan-rat/)

name: FlawedAmmyy RAT
description: FlawedAmmyy is a RAT discovered in 2016, based on leaked source code from Ammyy Admin. It allows for remote access, posing a potential cybersecurity threat.
additional_info:
FlawedAmmyy is a remote access tool (RAT) that was first seen in early 2016. The code for FlawedAmmyy was based on leaked source code for a version of Ammyy Admin, a remote access software.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0381/)

name: FlawedGrace
description: FlawedGrace is a C++ RAT first seen in 2017, available on MITRE's threat website.
additional_info:
FlawedGrace is a fully featured remote access tool (RAT) written in C++ that was first observed in late 2017.[
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0383/)

name: FlyStudio
description: Trojan named PUA.Win32.Flystudio.BG arrives on system as file dropped by other malware or downloaded unknowingly from malicious sites, identified by Trendmicro.
additional_info:
This Trojan arrives on a system as a file dropped by other malware or as a file downloaded unknowingly by users when visiting malicious sites.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/vinfo/hk/threat-encyclopedia/malware/pua.win32.flystudio.bg)

name: Fodcha
description: CNCERT and 360netlab identified a large DDoS botnet called Fodcha, with over 10,000 daily active bots in China and 100 victims daily. It uses the chacha algorithm for encryption.
additional_info:
Recently, CNCERT and 360netlab worked together and discovered a rapidly spreading DDoS botnet on the Internet. The global infection looks fairly big as just in China there are more than 10,000 daily active bots (IPs) and alsomore than 100 DDoS victims beingtargeted on a daily basis. We named the botnet Fodcha because of its initial use of the C2 domain name folded.in and its use of the chacha algorithm to encrypt network traffic.
[Netlab360](https://blog.netlab.360.com/fodcha-a-new-ddos-botnet/)

threat.name: Fodcha - MITRE - Exploitation for Client Execution
Fodcha - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: Foreign
description: Trojan-Ransom.Win32.Foreign is a malware that blocks computer access, demands ransom for restoration, and hides system controls including Task Manager, taskbar, and Start menu.
additional_info:
This family of Trojans blocks the user’s computer and requests a ransom for restoring its functionality.

The program adds itself to the list of programs launched automatically after the operating system loads, limits access to the desktop, and hides the windows of the Windows Task Manager and system controls, such as the taskbar and Start menu.
[Kaspersky](https://threats.kaspersky.com/en/threat/Trojan-Ransom.Win32.Foreign/)

threat.name: Foreign - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Foreign - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Foreign - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: FormBook
description: FormBook is a malware virus that steals personal data from victims' computers through malicious attachments in spam emails. It is distributed as a 'service' for aspiring cyber criminals to use.
additional_info:
FormBook is a virus designed to steal personal data from victims' computers. Research shows that this malware is distributed using spam emails that contain malicious attachments. In addition, developers provide this virus as a 'service' - any aspiring cyber criminal can pay a subscription and gain access to the FormBook tool. The list of victims is large, however, most FormBook infections have been detected in USA and South Korea.
[FormBook](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/12493-formbook-virus)

name: Foudre
description: In February 2017, new "Foudre" malware was observed evolving from "Infy", with actors implementing anti-takeover techniques to avoid sinkholing of their C2 domains after a previous takedown.
additional_info:
In February 2017, we observed an evolution of the “Infy” malware that we’re calling “Foudre” (“lightning”, in French). The actors appear to have learned from our previous takedown and sinkholing of their Command and Control (C2) infrastructure – Foudre incorporates new anti-takeover techniques in an attempt to avoid their C2 domains being sinkholed as we did in 2016.
[PaloAlto](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/unit42-prince-persia-ride-lightning-infy-returns-foudre/)

name: Frebniis
description: Malware silently steals data and runs harmful code in Windows via exploit of Microsoft IIS. Uses Failed Request Event Buffering module to monitor HTTP requests.
additional_info:
Researchers have discovered a clever piece of malware that stealthily exfiltrates data and executes malicious code from Windows systems by abusing a feature in Microsoft Internet Information Services (IIS). [Arstechinca](https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2023/02/new-backdoor-targeting-windows-servers-is-ultra-stealthy/)

Malware injects malicious code into Failed Request Event Buffering module in order to monitor HTTP requests from attacker.[Symantec](https://symantec-enterprise-blogs.security.com/blogs/threat-intelligence/frebniis-malware-iis)

name: Fsysna
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems. They can spy, steal data, and provide backdoor access to systems.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: Fullz House
description: Credit card skimming attacks are evolving, with a new hacking group targeting e-commerce websites with enhanced variants that include keylogging and phishing capabilities.
additional_info:
Credit card skimming attacks have been continuously evolving for the past few years. Recently, a new hacking group has been observed targeting e-commerce websites using some enhanced variants of skimming attacks, which includes additional capabilities such as keylogging and phishing.
[Cyware](https://cyware.com/news/fullz-house-introduces-enhancements-into-card-skimming-attacks-ed159026)

name: FunnyDream
description: Bitdefender reports malware infections linked to FunnyDream group in a cyber-espionage campaign, impacting over 200 systems. [Zdnet]
additional_info:
The malware infections are part of a widespread cyber-espionage campaign carried out by a group named FunnyDream, according to a new report published today by security firm Bitdefender.
[Zdnet](https://www.zdnet.com/article/more-than-200-systems-infected-by-new-chinese-apt-funnydream/)

name: GALLIUM
description: GALLIUM is an active threat group since 2012, targeting telecommunications networks. Suspected to be Chinese state-sponsored due to tools and tactics used.
additional_info:
GALLIUM is a group that has been active since at least 2012, primarily targeting high-profile telecommunications networks. GALLIUM has been identified in some reporting as likely a Chinese state-sponsored group, based in part on tools used and TTPs commonly associated with Chinese threat actors.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0093/)

name: GCleaner
description: G-Cleaner is advertised as a legitimate app to speed up Windows computers, but it actually installs a malicious program called AZORult. Deceptive promotion on website leads to malware installation.
additional_info:
G-Cleaner is promoted as an app that supposedly speeds up and optimizes Windows computers. It is promoted as a legitimate application (and its appearance may suggest this) and has a website from which it can be downloaded. In fact, its installation setup also contains a malicious program. G-Cleaner is installed together with AZORult, a trojan-type malicious program.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/14937-g-cleaner-unwanted-application)

threat.name: GCleaner - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Gafygt
description: BASHLITE is Linux malware that infects systems to launch DDoS attacks. It was originally known as Bashdoor and has been used to launch massive attacks up to 400 Gbps.
additional_info:
BASHLITE (also known as Gafgyt, Lizkebab, Qbot, Torlus and LizardStresser) is malware which infects Linux systems in order to launch distributed denial-of-service attacks (DDoS).Originally it was also known under the name Bashdoor, but this term now refers to the exploit method used by the malware. It has been used to launch attacks of up to 400 Gbps.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BASHLITE)

name: Gamaredon
description: Gamaredon Group, active since 2013, targets individuals linked to the Ukrainian government. Named after a misspelled "Armageddon", the group's activities have been documented in early campaigns.
additional_info:
Gamaredon Group is a threat group that has been active since at least 2013 and has targeted individuals likely involved in the Ukrainian government. The name Gamaredon Group comes from a misspelling of the word "Armageddon", which was detected in the adversary's early campaigns.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0047/)

name: Gamut
description: Gamut spambot is downloaded via Trojan Downloader from spam email attachment. Installation is simple - launches from Temp folder, installs as Windows service.
additional_info:
Gamut was found to be downloaded by a Trojan Downloader that arrives as an attachment from a spam email message. The bot installation is quite simple. After the malware binary has been downloaded, it launches itself from its current directory, usually the Windows %Temp% folder and installs itself as a Windows service. 
[Trustwave](https://www.trustwave.com/en-us/resources/blogs/spiderlabs-blog/gamut-spambot-analysis/)

name: GandCrab
description: GandCrab ransomware, discovered in January 2018, encrypts files on Microsoft Windows PCs and demands ransom for data access. It targets both consumers and businesses.
additional_info:
First observed in January of 2018, GandCrab ransomware is a type of malware that encrypts victims’ files and demands ransom payment in order to regain access to their data. GandCrab targets consumers and businesses with PCs running Microsoft Windows.
[MalwareBytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/gandcrab/)

threat.name: GandCrab - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
GandCrab - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
GandCrab - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact

name: Gasket
description: Mespinoza ransomware gang used a backdoor to keep access to hacked networks. The backdoor was identified as Win.Gasket by Malpedia.
additional_info:
A backdoor used by Mespinoza ransomware gang to maintain access to a compromised network.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.gasket)

threat.name: Gasket - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: Gasti
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by hackers to gain unauthorized access to users' systems. They can steal data and spy on users once activated.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

threat.name: Gasti - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
Gasti - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter
Gasti - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: Gelsemium
description: ESET researchers connect Gelsemium group to NoxPlayer supply-chain attack on gamers, traced back to 2014 with malicious tools found in Operation TooHash cyber-espionage campaign by G DATA.
additional_info:
ESET researchers have linked a stealthy cyberespionage group known as Gelsemium to the NoxPlayer Android emulator supply-chain attack that targeted gamers earlier this year.

The hacking group's activity goes back to 2014 when some of their malicious tools were discovered by G DATA’s SecurityLabs while investigating a targeted cyber-espionage campaign (dubbed Operation TooHash) powered by spear-phishing.
[Bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/stealthy-gelsemium-cyberspies-linked-to-noxplayer-supply-chain-attack/)

name: Generic Adware
description: Adware is unwanted software that displays ads on your screen, often through a web browser. It can disguise itself as legitimate or piggyback on another program to trick users into installing it.
additional_info: Adware is unwanted software designed to throw advertisements up on your screen, most often within a web browser. Some security professionals view it as the forerunner of the modern-day PUP (potentially unwanted program). Typically, it uses an underhanded method to either disguise itself as legitimate, or piggyback on another program to trick you into installing it on your PC, tablet, or mobile device. [Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/adware)

name: Generic Coinminer
description: Coinminer is malicious software that uses victim's CPU and RAM to mine for cryptocurrency without their knowledge. Sophisticated versions limit CPU usage to avoid detection.
additional_info:
Coinminer is an unwanted malicious software which uses the victim's computational power (CPU and RAM mostly) to mine for coins (for example Monero or Zcash). The malware achieves persistence by adding one of the opensource miners on startup without the victim's consensus. Most sophisticated coin miners use timer settings or cap the CPU usage in order to remain stealthy.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.coinminer)

name: Generic Loader
description: Loaders are evolving remote access Trojans, offering user-friendly interfaces, self-healing capabilities, and a platform for botmasters to sell bots. They are typically lightweight to evade security measures.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.

[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: Generic Phishing
description: Phishing involves pretending to be a trusted entity to steal personal information through fake websites or messages, often targeting users of social media, banks, and online platforms.
additional_info:
Phishing is the fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information such as usernames, passwords and credit card details by disguising oneself as a trustworthy entity in an electronic communication.Typically carried out by email spoofing or instant messaging, it often directs users to enter personal information at a fake website which matches the look and feel of the legitimate site.

Phishing is an example of social engineering techniques being used to deceive users. Users are often lured by communications purporting to be from trusted parties such as social web sites, auction sites, banks, online payment processors or IT administrators.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phishing#Phishing_types)

name: Generic TDS
