description: Traffic Direction Systems (TDS) are tools used by threat actors to control internet traffic, redirecting it to malicious sites. TDS is often used with malware for phishing, distributing payloads, and ransomware attacks.
additional_info:
Traffic Direction Systems (TDS) are tools used by threat actors to control and manipulate the flow of internet traffic. These systems allow attackers to redirect traffic from infected devices to malicious websites or servers under their control. By doing so, threat actors can carry out a variety of malicious activities including delivering malware, stealing sensitive information, or launching denial of service attacks.

TDS can be used in conjunction with other malware to amplify their impact and effectiveness. For example, threat actors may use TDS to redirect users from legitimate websites to fake login pages designed to steal their credentials. This tactic, known as phishing, is commonly used in combination with banking trojans or other types of password-stealing malware to maximize the chances of success.

Additionally, TDS can be used to distribute malware payloads more efficiently by redirecting traffic to malicious servers hosting exploit kits. These kits are designed to automatically identify and exploit vulnerabilities in a user's system to deliver malware without their knowledge. By using TDS to drive traffic to exploit kits, threat actors can infect a large number of devices quickly and easily.

Furthermore, TDS can also be used in conjunction with ransomware attacks. Threat actors can use TDS to redirect users to websites hosting ransomware payloads, which encrypt the victim's files and demand a ransom for their decryption. By controlling the flow of traffic using TDS, attackers can ensure that their ransomware payloads reach as many victims as possible.

name: Generic Worm
description: A computer worm replicates and spreads through computer networks, exploiting security vulnerabilities. It can control and infect multiple computers quickly, causing harm to the network and consuming bandwidth.
additional_info:
A computer worm is a standalone malware computer program that replicates itself in order to spread to other computers. It often uses a computer network to spread itself, relying on security failures on the target computer to access it. It will use this machine as a host to scan and infect other computers. When these new worm-invaded computers are controlled, the worm will continue to scan and infect other computers using these computers as hosts, and this behaviour will continue. Computer worms use recursive methods to copy themselves without host programs and distribute themselves based on exploiting the advantages of exponential growth, thus controlling and infecting more and more computers in a short time. Worms almost always cause at least some harm to the network, even if only by consuming bandwidth, whereas viruses almost always corrupt or modify files on a targeted computer.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_worm)

name: Generik
description: Trojans are disguised malware used by cyber-thieves to steal data and gain access to systems. Users are tricked into loading Trojans, allowing criminals to spy, steal data, and gain backdoor access.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: Gh0st
description: gh0st RAT is a widely used remote access tool with public source code. It has been utilized by multiple threat groups for malicious activities.
additional_info:
gh0st RAT is a remote access tool (RAT). The source code is public and it has been used by multiple groups.

Source: [MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0032/)

name: Gh0stBins
description: Gh0stBins is a sophisticated Remote Access Trojan (RAT) written in C++ that enables remote access and control over compromised machines. It is linked to a Chinese threat actor.
additional_info:
Gh0stBins is the name of a sophisticated Remote Access Trojan (RAT) written in the C++ programming language. Malware classed as such is designed to enable remote access and control over compromised machines. RATs are highly versatile malicious tools capable of performing a variety of actions on infected systems.

There is some evidence linking Gh0stBins to an unspecified Chinese threat actor.

[pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/27113-gh0stbins-rat)

name: Ghostlocker
description: GhostSec introduced GhostLocker, a Ransomware-as-a-Service, offering support to customers via Telegram. They have shifted focus to target Israel, diverging from their previous agenda.
additional_info:
The hacker collective called GhostSec has unveiled an innovative Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) framework called GhostLocker. They provide comprehensive assistance to customers interested in acquiring this service through a dedicated Telegram channel. Presently, GhostSec is focusing its attacks on Israel. This move represents a surprising departure from their past activities and stated agenda.
[Uptycs](https://www.uptycs.com/blog/ghostlocker-ransomware-ghostsec)

name: Gibon Ransomware
description: GIBON ransomware, discovered by Matthew Mesa, is spread through malicious MS Office documents attached to spam emails. It contains macro commands that download and install malware.
additional_info:
GIBON is a ransomware-type virus discovered by security researcher, Matthew Mesa. This malware is distributed via a malicious MS Office document attached to spam emails. The document contains a number of macro commands designed to download and install malware.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/11858-gibon-ransomware)

name: Gift Cardshark
description: RiskIQ's report "Gift Cardsharks" reveals a widespread campaign targeting various victims since 2016, showing hallmarks of state-sponsored activity with specific infrastructure and likely financial motives.
additional_info:
In our latest Intelligence report named “Gift Cardsharks” RiskIQ shows how the campaign is, in reality, a far-ranging assault that exceeds the compromised infrastructure of Wipro and involves a long list of targets dating back to 2016. Although attribution cannot be confirmed, the group’s numerous concurrent attacks display hallmarks of some state-sponsored activity including specific infrastructure, impressive organization, and, likely, a financial motive.
[Malwarenews](https://malware.news/t/meet-the-gift-cardsharks-behind-the-massive-campaign-targeting-victims-with-commercially-available-tools/30821)

name: Gigabud
description: Gigabud is an Android RAT that can steal banking credentials, record screens, and disguise as legitimate apps. It is distributed through deceptive websites by threat actors.
additional_info:
Gigabud is the name of an Android Remote Access Trojan (RAT) Android that can record the victim's screen and steal banking credentials by abusing the Accessibility Service. Gigabud masquerades as banking, shopping, and other applications. Threat actors have been observed using deceptive websites to distribute Gigabud RAT.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/25784-gigabud-rat-android)

name: Ginzo
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data from infected computers, targeting credentials for online services. They use methods like browser hooking, web injection scripts, form grabbing, keylogging, and stealing saved passwords.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

threat.name: Ginzo - MITRE - Data from Local System

name: Girostat
description: Info stealer malware collects data such as online banking credentials and social media passwords, and can use methods like keylogging or form grabbing. Modern info stealers can be controlled remotely.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: Globalshadow
description: Malware uses fake company VPN portal to infiltrate networks in the Middle East, leveraging Interactsh project for beaconing. Threat actor tracks infection stages, similar to tactics used by APT28.
additional_info:
One of the more notable aspects of this malware is its use of a command-and-control (C&C) infrastructure that pivots to a newly registered URL ("sharjahconnect"; note that Sharjah is one of the emirates of the UAE) designed to resemble a company VPN portal. This disguise not only aids in the initial infiltration but also helps maintain persistent access to compromised networks.

Furthermore, the malware uses the Interactsh project (a tool primarily designed for penetration testers to verify the success of their exploits) for beaconing purposes. Using this tool, threat actors can trigger connections to hostnames within Interactsh’s oast[.]fun domain. Note that other threat actors such as APT28 have been observed exploiting this resource. In this case, the threat actor behind the malware used these connections as a beaconing mechanism to track which targets progress through various stages of the infection chain.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/24/h/threat-actors-target-middle-east-using-fake-tool.html)

name: Globe Imposter
description: GlobeImposter is a ransomware virus that pretends to be Purge ransomware. It encrypts files and demands ransom for decryption. Remove it immediately to prevent data loss.
additional_info: GlobeImposter is a ransomware-type virus that mimics Purge (Globe) ransomware.[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/10861-globeimposter-ransomware)

threat.name: Globe Imposter - MITRE - Ingress Tool Transfer
Globe Imposter - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Globe Imposter - MITRE - System Information Discovery
Globe Imposter - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Globe Imposter - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Glove Stealer
description: Glove Stealer is a .NET information stealer targeting sensitive data from browser extensions and software, including cryptocurrency wallets and password managers. It bypasses encryption using a supporting module. Spread through phishing emails and disguised as a fixing tool.
additional_info:
Glove Stealer is an information stealer written in .NET, stealing sensitive data from wide range of browser extensions as well as locally installed software 
It focuses on browser data (cookies, autofill, …), cryptocurrency wallets, 2FA authenticators, password managers, email clients, and more Glove Stealer uses a dedicated supporting module to bypass App-Bound encryption by using IElevator service. 

While observed being spread via phishing emails resembling ClickFix, it itself also tries to mimic a fixing tool which users might use during troubleshooting problems they have encountered. [Gendigital](https://www.gendigital.com/blog/insights/research/glove-stealer)

name: Glupteba
description: Glupteba is a trojan virus distributed by cyber criminals through malicious ads on legitimate websites. It can install other malicious programs and should be removed immediately.
additional_info:
Glupteba is a trojan-type program, malicious software that installs other programs of this type. Cyber criminals distribute Glupteba through malicious advertisements that can be injected into legitimate websites or advertising networks. Research shows that Glubteba can be used to distribute a browser stealer or router exploiter. In any case, this malware should be uninstalled immediately.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/15782-glupteba-trojan)

name: Gmobi
description: Trojan SDK found in Android apps like com.rock.gota, Trend Micro Dr.Safety, Dr.Booster, and Asus WebStorage, displays ads, downloads software, and steals data. Detected by DrWeb.
additional_info:
A Trojan SDK (Software Development Kit) incorporated into Android applications. It is designed to display advertisements, download and install software, and collect confidential information. The malware was detected in such applications as com.rock.gota (system software for Micromax AQ5001 firmware update), Trend Micro Dr.Safety, Dr.Booster, and Asus WebStorage.
[DrWeb](https://vms.drweb.com/virus/?i=7999629)

name: GoBotKR
description: GoBotKR is a high-risk trojan that emerged in May, 2018 and targets South Korean users. It opens a 'backdoor' for cyber criminals to control infected machines remotely and should be removed immediately.
additional_info:
GoBotKR is high-risk trojan-type infection that entered the scene in May, 2018. It is essentially an updated version of another trojan called GoBot2, which is written in the Go programming language. GoBotKR opens a 'backdoor' for cyber criminals to access and remotely control the infected machine. Research shows that this malware is distributed using torrent websites and targets mainly South Korean users. The presence of this infection on your system can lead to various issues and, therefore, it should be eliminated immediately.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/15390-gobotkr-malware)

threat.name: GoBotKR - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter

name: GoBruteForcer
description: GoBruteforcer is a new Golang-based malware targeting web servers to create a botnet, specifically aimed at Unix-like platforms using brute-force attacks with hard-coded credentials.
additional_info:
A new Golang-based malware dubbed GoBruteforcer has been found targeting web servers running phpMyAdmin, MySQL, FTP, and Postgres to corral the devices into a botnet.

GoBruteforcer is mainly designed to single out Unix-like platforms running x86, x64 and ARM architectures, with the malware attempting to obtain access via a brute-force attack using a list of credentials hard-coded into the binary.
[https://thehackernews.com/2023/03/gobruteforcer-new-golang-based-malware.html](The Hacker News)

name: GobRAT
description: GobRAT is a Go-based RAT that communicates with a C2 server via TLS, executes commands, and is packed with UPX version 4. Samples have been confirmed for ARM, MIPS, x86, and x86-64 architectures.
additional_info:
GobRAT is a RAT written in Go language and communicates with C2 server via TLS and executes various commands. It is packed with UPX version 4 series, and samples for various architectures such as ARM, MIPS, x86, and x86-64 have been confirmed.
[Jpcert](https://blogs.jpcert.or.jp/en/2023/05/gobrat.html)

name: Godlua
description: Godlua Backdoor has unique communication mechanism using hardcoded DNS name, Pastebin.com, GitHub.com, DNS TXT, HTTPS for Lua byte-code files download, and DNS over HTTPS for secure communication.
additional_info:
Godlua Backdoor has a redundant communication mechanism for C2 connection, a combination of hardcoded dns name, Pastebin.com, GitHub.com as well as DNS TXT are used to store the C2 address, which is not something we see often. At the same time, it uses HTTPS to download Lua byte-code files, and uses DNS over HTTPS to get the C2 name to ensure secure communication between the bots, the Web Server and the C2.
[Netlab360](https://blog.netlab.360.com/an-analysis-of-godlua-backdoor-en/)

name: Godzilla Webshell
description: A web shell is a malicious file on a web server that allows attackers to execute commands remotely, gain persistence, and move laterally through a network to compromise additional hosts and data.
additional_info:
A web shell is an internet-accessible malicious file implanted in a victim web server’s file system that enables an attacker to execute commands by visiting a web page. Once placed on a compromised web server, it allows an attacker to perform remote command execution to the operating system running on the host machine. The web shell provides the attacker with a form of persistence in the compromised system and the potential to further pivot through the network to compromise hosts and data that may not otherwise be externally accessible.
[Gigamon](https://blog.gigamon.com/2022/09/28/investigating-web-shells/)

name: GoldDigger
description: First iOS Trojan, GoldFactory, discovered by Group-IB, steals facial recognition data to gain unauthorized bank account access. Details at group-ib.com/blog/goldfactory-ios-trojan/.
additional_info: Group-IB uncovers the first iOS Trojan harvesting facial recognition data used for unauthorized access to bank accounts. [GroupIB](https://group-ib.com/blog/goldfactory-ios-trojan/)

name: Goldbackdoor
description: Goldbackdoor is a multi-stage malware that allows threat actors to stop deployment after infecting initial targets, separating the first stage tooling and final payload. Targeting journalists.
additional_info:
Goldbackdoor is a multi-stage malware that separates the first stage tooling and the final payload, which allows the threat actor to halt deployment after initial targets are infected.
[thrreatpost](https://threatpost.com/hackers-target-journalists-goldbackdoor/179389/)

threat.name: Goldbackdoor - MITRE - System Network Configuration Discovery
Goldbackdoor - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
Goldbackdoor - MITRE - Create or Modify System Process

name: Goldoon Botnet
description: FortiGuard Labs discovered a new botnet exploiting a decade-old D-Link vulnerability (CVE-2015-2051) allowing remote execution of arbitrary commands through crafted HTTP requests on the HNAP interface.
additional_info:
FortiGuard Labs observed a new botnet targeting a D-Link vulnerability from nearly a decade ago, CVE-2015-2051. This vulnerability allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands via a GetDeviceSettings action on the HNAP interface. As a result, an attacker can create a crafted HTTP request with a malicious command embedded in the header.
[Fortinet](https://www.fortinet.com/blog/threat-research/new-goldoon-botnet-targeting-d-link-devices)

name: GoodMorning
description: GoodMorning ransomware encrypts files, demands payment for decryption. Files are renamed with victim ID and cyber criminals' email address, followed by ".GoodMorning" extension. Payment is required to regain access to data.
additional_info:
GoodMorning is a piece of malicious software, which is categorized as ransomware. It operates by encrypting data to demand payment for the decryption. In other words, the affected files are rendered inaccessible and unusable, and victims are asked to pay - to restore their data. During the encryption process, files are renamed according to this pattern: original filename, "Id" followed by the ID assigned to the victim in brackets, the words "Send Email", cyber criminals' email address in brackets, and the ".GoodMorning" extension.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/21150-goodmorning-ransomware)

threat.name: GoodMorning - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
GoodMorning - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
GoodMorning - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Goofy
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems. Once activated, they can spy on users, steal data, or access systems.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: GootLoader
description: REvil ransomware and Gootkit banking Trojan delivering malware payloads globally, focusing on South Korea, Germany, France, and North America. Gootkit's delivery method has been significantly improved over the years.
additional_info:
The malware delivery method pioneered by the threat actors behind the REvil ransomware and the Gootkit banking Trojan has been enjoying a renaissance of late, as telemetry indicates that criminals are using the method to deploy an array of malware payloads in South Korea, Germany, France, and across North America.

The Gootkit malware family has been around more than half a decade – a mature Trojan with functionality centered around banking credential theft. In recent years, almost as much effort has gone into improvement of its delivery method as has gone into the NodeJS-based malware itself.
[Sophos](https://news.sophos.com/en-us/2021/03/01/gootloader-expands-its-payload-delivery-options/)

name: Gootkit
description: GootKit is a trojan-type malware that steals personal information and acts as a 'backdoor' for cyber criminals to remotely control infected computers. It is often distributed by the Emotet trojan.
additional_info:
GootKit (also known as talalpek, Trojan.GootKit or Xswkit) is a trojan-type malicious computer program. Like many other trojans, GootKit steals various personal, confidential information. Once installed, it also acts as a 'backdoor' allowing cyber criminals to access and control a computer remotely (e.g. to download additional files to an infected computer). GootKit is often distributed using another trojan-type program called Emotet.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/14310-gootkit-trojan)

name: Graftor
description: Hupigon (Graftor) is a backdoor Trojan first detected in 2008. It is known by multiple names (Backdoor.Win32.Hupigon, Trojan.Win32.Hupigon, Backdoor.Win32.Graftor, Trojan.Win32.Graftor).
additional_info:
Hupigon (also Graftor) detected as (Backdoor.Win32.Hupigon, Trojan.Win32.Hupigon, Backdoor.Win32.Graftor, and Trojan.Win32.Graftor) is a backdoor Trojan. Its first known detection goes back to November, 2008, according to Securelist from Kaspersky Labs.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hupigon)

threat.name: Graftor - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Grandoreiro
description: Grandoreiro is a banking trojan first seen in 2016, written in Delphi, and operates on a Malware-as-a-Service model. Victims are confirmed in Brazil, Mexico, Portugal, and Spain.
additional_info:
Grandoreiro is a banking trojan written in Delphi that was first observed in 2016 and uses a Malware-as-a-Service (MaaS) business model. Grandoreiro has confirmed victims in Brazil, Mexico, Portugal, and Spain.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0531/)

name: GravityRAT
description: GravityRAT is an ongoing remote access tool developed since 2016. The actor is unknown, but usernames "TheMartian" and "The Invincible" have been linked to them. Identified in attacks in India.
additional_info:
GravityRAT is a remote access tool (RAT) and has been in ongoing development since 2016. The actor behind the tool remains unknown, but two usernames have been recovered that link to the author, which are "TheMartian" and "The Invincible." According to the National Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT) of India, the malware has been identified in attacks against organization and entities in India.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0237/)

name: GreetingGhoul
description: GreetingGhoul is a cryptocurrency-stealing malware active in Europe, South America, and the US, with possible ties to Russian-speaking cyber criminals. Targets digital wallets for theft.
additional_info:
GreetingGhoul is a piece of malicious software that targets cryptocurrency wallets. This stealer-type malware is focused exclusively on digital currencies. GreetingGhoul is currently active in Europe, South America, and the United States. There is tenuous evidence pointing to the cyber criminals behind this stealer being from a Russian-speaking region.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/27014-greetingghoul-malware)

name: GuLoader
description: GuLoader is a file downloader that has been distributing various RAT malware such as NETWIRE, Agent Tesla, NanoCore, FormBook, and Parallax RAT since December 2019. [MITRE]
additional_info:
GuLoader is a file downloader that has been used since at least December 2019 to distribute a variety of remote administration tool (RAT) malware, including NETWIRE, Agent Tesla, NanoCore, FormBook, and Parallax RAT.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0561/)

name: H0lyGh0st
description: DEV-0530 threat actor group, believed to be of North Korean origin, employs ransomware according to MSTIC. More information can be found on Malpedia.
additional_info:
Ransomware used by threat actor group DEV-0530, attributed by MSTIC to North Korean origin.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.sienna_blue)

threat.name: H0lyGh0st - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
H0lyGh0st - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
H0lyGh0st - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: HCRootkit
description: A rootkit is malicious software that allows unauthorized access to a computer system, often hiding its presence. Its name comes from "root" (privileged account) and "kit" (software components).
additional_info:
A rootkit is a collection of computer software, typically malicious, designed to enable access to a computer or an area of its software that is not otherwise allowed (for example, to an unauthorized user) and often masks its existence or the existence of other software.[1] The term rootkit is a compound of "root" (the traditional name of the privileged account on Unix-like operating systems) and the word "kit" (which refers to the software components that implement the tool).[2] The term "rootkit" has negative connotations through its association with malware.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rootkit)

name: HMR RAT
description: A Remote Access Trojan (RAT) is a malicious software enabling unauthorized access to a victim's computer, allowing covert surveillance and administrative control, making it very dangerous for compromised machines.
additional_info: Remote Access Trojan (RAT) is a type of malware that allows covert surveillance, a backdoor for administrative control and unfettered and unauthorized remote access to a victim's machine. The RAT is very dangerous because it enables intruders to get remote control of the compromised computer

name: HackTool
description: Hacking tools are software designed to bypass security measures on computers and networks. Some administrators use them to test security weaknesses. Trendmicro provides information on different types of hacking tools.
additional_info:
Hacking tools are applications that crack or break computer and network security measures. Hacking tools have different capabilities that have been designed to penetrate systems. Some system administrators have been known to use similar programs to test security and identify possible avenues for intrusion.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/vinfo/us/threat-encyclopedia/malware/hacktool)

threat.name: HackTool - MITRE - Exploitation for Client Execution
HackTool - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: HackingTeam
description: HackingTeam was an IT company in Milan that sold surveillance tools to governments, law enforcement, and corporations. Their software allowed monitoring of internet users, decryption of files, recording of communications, and remote activation of devices.
additional_info:
HackingTeam was a Milan-based information technology company that sold offensive intrusion and surveillance capabilities to governments, law enforcement agencies and corporations. Its "Remote Control Systems" enable governments and corporations to monitor the communications of internet users, decipher their encrypted files and emails, record Skype and other Voice over IP communications, and remotely activate microphones and camera on target computers.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hacking_Team)

name: Hades
description: Ransomware encrypts victim's files, demanding payment for decryption. Advanced malware uses cryptoviral extortion, making it difficult to recover files without decryption key. Perpetrators often use digital currencies for ransom.
additional_info:
Ransomware is a type of malware from cryptovirology that threatens to publish the victim's data or perpetually block access to it unless a ransom is paid. While some simple ransomware may lock the system so that it is not difficult for a knowledgeable person to reverse, more advanced malware uses a technique called cryptoviral extortion. It encrypts the victim's files, making them inaccessible, and demands a ransom payment to decrypt them. In a properly implemented cryptoviral extortion attack, recovering the files without the decryption key is an intractable problem – and difficult to trace digital currencies such as paysafecard or Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies are used for the ransoms, making tracing and prosecuting the perpetrators difficult.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ransomware)

threat.name: Hades - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
Hades - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Hades - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: Hakistan
description: Android keylogger captures touches and notifications, reports via email. Find on Github.
additional_info:
A Full featured Android keylogger that stores Touches, Notifications and reports its handler via email.
[Github](https://github.com/hakistan/HakistanKeylogger)

name: Hamas Application Infrastructure
description: Insikt Group discovered an application linked to Hamas terrorist organization that communicates with their website alqassam[.]ps, potentially connected to TAG-63 cyber group serving Hamas. Website activity peaked during Hamas incursion into Israeli territory. Source: Recorded Future.
additional_info:
Hamas Application Infrastructure Reveals Possible Overlap with TAG-63 and Iranian Threat Activity.

Insikt Group identified an application disseminated on a Telegram Channel used by members or supporters of the Hamas terrorist organization. The application is configured to communicate with Hamas’s Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades website — alqassam[.]ps. The website has worked intermittently since the start of Hamas’s ground incursion into Israeli territory on October 7, 2023.

Infrastructure analysis associated with alqassam[.]ps led to the identification of a cluster of domains that mimic the domain registration tradecraft of TAG-63 (AridViper, APT-C-23, Desert Falcon), a cyber group that we believe operates at the behest of the Hamas terrorist organization.

[Recorded Future](https://go.recordedfuture.com/hubfs/reports/cta-2023-1019.pdf)

name: Hancitor
description: Hancitor is a downloader malware used by Pony and other info-stealers to deliver malicious payloads and steal sensitive information.
additional_info:
Hancitor is a downloader that has been used by Pony and other information stealing malware.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0499/)

name: Hangover
description: Patchwork and Operation Hangover are likely conducted by the same actors, according to MITRE.
additional_info:
It is believed that the actors behind Patchwork are the same actors behind Operation Hangover.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0040/)

name: HavanaCrypt
description: New HAVANAcrypt ransomware poses as Google Software Update, uses Microsoft IP as C&C server, and exploits QueueUserWorkItem function along with KeePass Password Safe modules during encryption routine.
additional_info:
..a brand-new ransomware family that employs a similar scheme: It disguises itself as a Google Software Update application and uses a Microsoft web hosting service IP address as its command-and-control (C&C) server to circumvent detection. Our investigation also shows that this ransomware uses the QueueUserWorkItem function, a .NET System.Threading namespace method that queues a method for execution, and the modules of KeePass Password Safe, an open-source password manager, during its file encryption routine.
[Trendmicro](https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/22/g/brand-new-havanacrypt-ransomware-poses-as-google-software-update.html)

threat.name: HavanaCrypt - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
HavanaCrypt - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
HavanaCrypt - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Havoc
description: Havoc is a flexible post-exploitation command and control framework developed by @C5pider, available on Github for use.
additional_info:
Havoc is a modern and malleable post-exploitation command and control framework, created by @C5pider
[Github](https://github.com/HavocFramework/Havoc)

name: HawkEye
description: HawkEye is a dangerous virus that records keystrokes and account information, spread through spam emails. Users are tricked into opening attachments, allowing HawkEye to infiltrate their systems, and sometimes criminals use RATs.
additional_info:
HawkEye is high-risk virus designed to record keystrokes, account credentials, and other similar information. In most cases, HawkEye is distributed using spam email campaigns. Developers send thousands of deceptive emails that encourage users to open attached files, however, opening them results in infiltration of HawkEye. Cyber criminals sometimes infiltrate HawkEye manually, using remote access tools (RATs).
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/14041-hawkeye-keylogger-virus)

name: Hawkish Eyes
description: DarkVision is a Remote Administration Trojan that allows attackers to remotely control an infected computer, access sensitive data, and execute malicious actions without the victim's authorization.
additional_info:
DarkVision is the name of a Remote Administration Trojan (RAT). Malware of this type is designed to provide unauthorized access to a victim's computer. The RAT allows attackers to control the infected computer remotely, giving them access to sensitive data and the ability to perform a range of malicious actions.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/26678-darkvision-rat)

name: Helminth
description: Helminth is a backdoor with two variants, one using VBScript and PowerShell delivered via Excel macros, and another as a standalone Windows executable.
additional_info:
Helminth is a backdoor that has at least two variants - one written in VBScript and PowerShell that is delivered via a macros in Excel spreadsheets, and one that is a standalone Windows executable. 
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0170/)

name: Heracles
description: A Trojan downloader is a malicious program that installs files onto a device without the user's consent, often causing harm by disrupting, stealing, or damaging computer systems and devices.
additional_info:
A Trojan downloader is a type of Trojan horse that downloads and installs files, often malicious programs. A Trojan horse is a type of software that looks legitimate but can be malicious in nature. Sometimes these programs can be downloaded onto a device without the user’s knowledge or consent. A Trojan’s purpose is to damage, disrupt, steal, or generally inflict some other harm on your computer and devices.
[Norton](https://us.norton.com/internetsecurity-malware-what-is-a-trojan-downloader.html)

name: HermeticWizard
description: HermeticWizard is a worm spreading HermeticWiper through local networks using WMI and SMB services, first seen on Feb. 23. Protection available by Paloalto.
additional_info:
HermeticWizard is a worm first observed on Feb. 23 that spreads HermeticWiper across hosts in a local network using Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) and Server Message Block (SMB) services.
[Paloalto](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/blog/security-operations/cortex-xdr-protections-against-malware-associated-with-ukraine-and-russia-cyber-activity/)

threat.name: HermeticWizard - MITRE - Exploitation of Remote Services

name: Hermit
description: Spyware named "Hermit" likely developed by Italian companies RCS Lab S.p.A and Tykelab Srl, which may be a front company. [Lookout]
additional_info:
Based on our analysis, the spyware, which we named “Hermit,” is likely developed by Italian spyware vendor RCS Lab S.p.A and Tykelab Srl, a telecommunications solutions company we suspect to be operating as a front company.
[Lookout](https://www.lookout.com/blog/hermit-spyware-discovery)

name: HiatusRAT
description: HiatusRAT infects systems to allow remote interaction, covertly converting them into proxies for threat actors. It includes unusual prebuilt functionality and a packet-capture binary for monitoring router traffic.
additional_info:
Once a targeted system is infected, HiatusRAT allows the threat actor to remotely interact with the system, and it utilizes prebuilt functionality – some of which is highly unusual – to convert the compromised machine into a covert proxy for the threat actor. The packet-capture binary enables the actor to monitor router traffic on ports associated with email and file-transfer communications.
[BlackLotusLabs](https://blog.lumen.com/new-hiatusrat-router-malware-covertly-spies-on-victims/)

name: Hiddad
description: Trojans are sneaky malware that can do various harmful activities without user knowledge, like remote access, data theft, and denial-of-service attacks. Stay updated using FortiGuard Encyclopedia.
additional_info:
A trojan is a type of malware that performs activites without the user’s knowledge. These activities commonly include establishing remote access connections, capturing keyboard input, collecting system information, downloading/uploading files, dropping other malware into the infected system, performing denial-of-service (DoS) attacks, and running/terminating processes.
The Fortinet Antivirus Analyst Team is constantly updating our descriptions. Please check the FortiGuard Encyclopedia regularly for updates.
[FortiGuardLabs](https://www.fortiguard.com/encyclopedia/virus/8211482)

name: Hiddapp
description: HiddenApp is a Trojan that secretly installs unwanted apps or libraries on devices. These additional apps may contain malware or adware, compromising the user's device and privacy.
additional_info:
HiddenApp is a type of Trojan that will install additional apps or libraries without user’s knowledge and consent. Often the payloads will contain additional malware or adware.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/detections/android-trojan-hiddenapp/)

threat.name: Hiddapp - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: HiddenTear
description: Hidden Tear is an open-source ransomware trojan that attacks Windows computers. It was uploaded to GitHub in August 2015, marking a significant development in cyber security threats.
additional_info:
Hidden Tear is the first open-source ransomware trojan that targets computers running Microsoft Windows.The original sample was posted in August 2015 to GitHub.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hidden_Tear)

threat.name: HiddenTear - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
HiddenTear - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
HiddenTear - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact

name: Higaisa APT
description: Higaisa is a threat group with suspected South Korean origins that has targeted organizations in North Korea and other countries since at least 2009.
additional_info:
Higaisa is a threat group suspected to have South Korean origins. Higaisa has targeted government, public, and trade organizations in North Korea; however, they have also carried out attacks in China, Japan, Russia, Poland, and other nations. Higaisa was first disclosed in early 2019 but is assessed to have operated as early as 2009.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0126/)

name: HilalRAT
description: Group developed malware disguised as Quran and chat app to compromise accounts/devices by extracting contact info, messages, files, location, camera & microphone access. Named HilalRAT. [Facebook]
additional_info:
To compromise people’s accounts and devices, this group copied and modified
a legitimate Android application — a birthday calendar app — so it could extract contact
information and send it to the attacker’s remote server. They also developed remote
access-capable malware for Android that disguised as a Quran, a chat app to retrieve
people’s contacts list, text messages, files, location information, and activate camera and
microphone. We named this previously unreported malware strain HilalRAT (remote
access trojan), after seeing “hilal'' in several of the malware samples we analyzed. 
(Facebook)[https://about.fb.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Meta-Quarterly-Adversarial-Threat-Report_Q1-2022.pdf]

threat.name: HilalRAT - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
HilalRAT - MITRE - System Network Configuration Discovery

name: Hitik
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by cyber-thieves to gain unauthorized access to systems. Users are tricked into installing them, leading to potential data theft and system compromise.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: HiveRAT
description: Remote Access Trojans allow covert surveillance and unauthorized access to victim PCs by mimicking keyloggers. They provide backdoor access for attackers to control victim machines and potentially engage in criminal activities.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/remote-access-trojan-rat/)

name: Homoglyph
description: IDN homograph attack deceives users by using similar looking characters in domain names to trick users into visiting malicious websites. Users should be cautious of subtle character changes in URLs.
additional_info: The internationalized domain name (IDN) homograph attack is a way a malicious party may deceive computer users about what remote system they are communicating with, by exploiting the fact that many different characters look alike (i.e., they are homographs, hence the term for the attack, although technically homoglyph is the more accurate term for different characters that look alike). For example, a regular user of example.com may be lured to click a link where the Latin character "a" is replaced with the Cyrillic character "а".

name: Horabot
description: Horabot is a campaign targeting Spanish-speaking users in the Americas, spreading a banking Trojan and spam tool to manipulate Outlook mailboxes, steal email addresses, distribute phishing emails, and obtain login credentials.
additional_info:
Horabot is part of the campaign where threat actors infect machines with a banking Trojan and spam tool. This campaign primarily focuses on Spanish-speaking users in the Americas. Horabot enables attackers to manipulate Outlook mailboxes, extract email addresses, distribute phishing emails with harmful attachments, and acquire login credentials and security codes.
[PCrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/26920-horabot-malware)

name: Hqwar
description: Trojan-Banker programs steal bank account information and other data, then transfer it to malicious users. Stolen data can be transmitted through email, FTP, the web, or other methods.
additional_info:
Trojan-Banker programs are designed to steal user account data relating to online banking systems, e-payment systems and plastic card systems. The data is then transmitted to the malicious user controlling the Trojan. Email, FTP, the web (including data in a request), or other methods may be used to transit the stolen data.
[Kaspersky](https://encyclopedia.kaspersky.com/knowledge/trojan-banker/)

name: Huelar
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs, used by cyber-thieves to gain unauthorized access to users' systems. They can spy, steal data, and provide backdoor access to hackers.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

threat.name: Huelar - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: HydraRAT
description: Adversaries use Command and Control techniques to communicate with compromised systems stealthily within a victim network, mimicking normal traffic to avoid detection.
additional_info:
The adversary is trying to communicate with compromised systems to control them.

Command and Control consists of techniques that adversaries may use to communicate with systems under their control within a victim network. Adversaries commonly attempt to mimic normal, expected traffic to avoid detection. There are many ways an adversary can establish command and control with various levels of stealth depending on the victim’s network structure and defenses.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0011/)

name: Hydraq
description: Hydraq is a data-theft trojan used in the 2009 Google intrusion. Variations of it have been used in recent campaigns by Chinese actors, possibly including APT17.
additional_info:
Hydraq is a data-theft trojan first used by Elderwood in the 2009 Google intrusion known as Operation Aurora, though variations of this trojan have been used in more recent campaigns by other Chinese actors, possibly including APT17.
[Mitre](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0203/)

name: Hydrochasma APT
description: A likely intelligence-gathering campaign targeting shipping companies and medical laboratories in Asia using publicly available tools by threat actor Hydrochasma, potentially interested in COVID-19 industries since October 2022.
additional_info:
Shipping companies and medical laboratories in Asia are being targeted in a likely intelligence-gathering campaign that relies exclusively on publicly available and living-off-the-land tools.

Hydrochasma, the threat actor behind this campaign, has not been linked to any previously identified group, but appears to have a possible interest in industries that may be involved in COVID-19-related treatments or vaccines.

This activity has been ongoing since at least October 2022. While Symantec, by Broadcom Software, did not see any data being exfiltrated in this campaign, the targets, as well as some of the tools used, indicate that the most likely motivation in this campaign is intelligence gathering.
[Symantec](https://symantec-enterprise-blogs.security.com/blogs/threat-intelligence/hydrochasma-asia-medical-shipping-intelligence-gathering)

name: ICMP Tunneling
description: ICMP is a critical network service used for connectivity verification, but attackers can inject malicious traffic into ICMP packets, potentially compromising network security.
additional_info: ICMP is the standard protocol has been used historically to verify connectivity between hosts. Nowadays, IPv6 even uses a advanced version called ICMPv6 that replaces supporting protocols in IPv4, like ARP for host discovery. ICMP is therefor a critical network service that is usually allowed in firewalls. But attackers can inject malicious traffic into normal looking ICMP echo and reply packets. ICMP Echo, used for ping, contains random alphabetic chars as dummy payload. But attackers can inject malicious TCP traffic into those packets to establish C2 channels or to exfiltrate data.

name: IFFM
description: Info stealers collect data from infected computers to send to attackers. They use various methods such as browser hooking, web injection, form grabbing, keylogging, and stealing saved passwords.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: IIS-Raid
description: IIS Raid is a malicious tool that exploits the flexibility of IIS to create backdoors and execute attacker-defined actions on the web server. Available on Github.
additional_info:
IIS Raid is a native IIS module that abuses the extendibility of IIS to backdoor the web server and carry out custom actions defined by an attacker.
[Github](https://github.com/0x09AL/IIS-Raid)

name: IIStealer
description: IIStealer is a malicious extension for IIS that can access and steal payment information from e-commerce transactions by monitoring network communication on the server.
additional_info:
IIStealer is implemented as a malicious extension for Internet Information Services (IIS), Microsoft web server software. Being a part of the server, IIStealer is able to access all the network communication flowing through the server and steal data of interest to the attackers – in this case, payment information from e-commerce transactions.
[Welivesecurity](https://www.welivesecurity.com/2021/08/06/iistealer-server-side-threat-ecommerce-transactions/)

name: IRATA
description: Trojans are disguised malware designed to trick users into loading them onto their systems. Once activated, they can allow cyber-criminals to spy, steal data, and gain access to the system.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: Ice Breaker
description: APT refers to stealthy threat actors, often nation states, gaining unauthorized network access and remaining undetected. It can also apply to non-state groups conducting targeted intrusions for specific objectives.
additional_info:
An advanced persistent threat (APT) is a stealthy threat actor, typically a nation state or state-sponsored group, which gains unauthorized access to a computer network and remains undetected for an extended period.[1][2] In recent times, the term may also refer to non-state-sponsored groups conducting large-scale targeted intrusions for specific goals.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_persistent_threat)

name: IceApple
description: IceApple is an in-memory-only framework that focuses on keeping a low forensic footprint on infected hosts. This aligns with state-sponsored missions of intelligence collection and has a targeted approach.
additional_info:
IceApple uses an in-memory-only framework that highlights the adversary’s priority of
maintaining a low forensic footprint on the infected host. This is typical of long-running
objectives aimed at intelligence collection and aligns with a targeted, state-sponsored mission.
[CrowdStrike](https://www.crowdstrike.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/crowdstrike-iceapple-a-novel-internet-information-services-post-exploitation-framework-1.pdf)

name: IceRAT
description: IceRAT is a dangerous malware that operates as a backdoor rather than a Remote Access Trojan. It does not have full control over infected systems but is still highly dangerous.
additional_info:
IceRAT is a piece of malicious software. While its name contains the abbreviation "RAT", this malware operates less like a Remote Access Trojan and more as a backdoor. RATs are designed to enable remote access and control over an infected machine, whilst backdoor trojans cause chain infections (i.e. download/install additional malware). The former often have backdoor capabilities, however the distinction is made through RATs' ability to stealthily control (i.e. issue and execute commands on) a compromised device. However, despite IceRAT's inability to exercise this level of control over infected systems, it is nonetheless deemed to be a highly dangerous piece of software.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/19542-icerat-malware)

name: IceXLoader
description: IceXLoader is commercial malware that downloads and deploys additional malware. Its latest version is written in Nim, a new language used by threat actors, including the NimzaLoader variant of BazarLoader.
additional_info:
IceXLoader is a commercial malware used to download and deploy additional malware on infected machines. The latest version is written in Nim, a relatively new language utilized by threat actors the past two years, most notably by the NimzaLoader variant of BazarLoader used by the TrickBot group.
[Fortinet](https://www.fortinet.com/blog/threat-research/new-icexloader-3-0-developers-warm-up-to-nim)

name: IcedID
description: Discovered by IBM in 2017, IcedID banking Trojan originally targeted financial institutions in the U.S. Recent major version release in 2020 introduced significant changes. Evolving threat.
additional_info:
The IcedID banking Trojan was discovered by IBM X-Force researchers in 2017. At that time, it targeted banks, payment card providers, mobile services providers, payroll, webmail and e-commerce sites, mainly in the U.S. IcedID has since continued to evolve, and while one of its more recent versions became active in late-2019, X-Force researchers have identified a new major version release that emerged in 2020 with some substantial changes. 
[securityintelligence.com](https://securityintelligence.com/posts/breaking-the-ice-a-deep-dive-into-the-icedid-banking-trojans-new-major-version-release/)

Sometimes these programs can be downloaded onto a device without the user’s knowledge or consent. A Trojan’s purpose is to damage, disrupt, steal, or generally inflict some other harm on your computer and devices.

Both share similar code with SonicGlyde; a discord stealer, which is a variant of the Epsilon Stealer, which captures browser cookies, credentials, and credit card information saved in Discord.

Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.

name: Indexsinas
description: Indexsinas SMB worm targets vulnerable industries like healthcare, hospitality, education, and telecommunications to reduce crypto miners on hacked PCs. It has been lurking since 2019, utilizing old weapons like EternalBlue.
additional_info:
The  Indexsinas SMB worm is aiming for susceptible situations in which scientists cautioned – focusing on healthcare, hospitality, education, and the telecommunications industries. Its ultimate objective is to reduce crypto miners on hacked PCs. 

Since 2019, Indexsinas, aka NSABuffMiner, has been lurked. It uses the old weapon arsenal Equation Group, along with EternalBlue and EternalRomance, to invade Windows SMB shares and DoublePulsar backdoor. Indexsinas is using lateral mobility to assimilate specific environments aggressively. 
[ehackingnews](https://www.ehackingnews.com/2021/07/indexsinas-smb-worm-attacks-vulnerable.html)

A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. name: Inido!rts
description: Trojans are malware that deceive users, often spreading through social engineering. They allow unauthorized access to computers, can steal personal information, delete files, and spread ransomware.
additional_info:
In computing, a Trojan horse (or simply trojan) is any malware which misleads users of its true intent. The term is derived from the Ancient Greek story of the deceptive Trojan Horse that led to the fall of the city of Troy.

Trojans are generally spread by some form of social engineering, for example where a user is duped into executing an email attachment disguised to appear not suspicious, (e.g., a routine form to be filled in), or by clicking on some fake advertisement on social media or anywhere else. Although their payload can be anything, many modern forms act as a backdoor, contacting a controller which can then have unauthorized access to the affected computer. Trojans may allow an attacker to access users' personal information such as banking information, passwords, or personal identity. It can also delete a user's files or infect other devices connected to the network. Ransomware attacks are often carried out using a trojan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trojan_horse_(computing))

name: Injector
description: Injector Trojans insert malicious code into processes on a computer to download malware, disrupt web browsing, or monitor user actions.
additional_info:
Injector trojans insert malicious code into processes running on a computer in order to perform various actions, such as downloading additional malware, interfering with web browsing activities or monitoring the user's actions.
[f-secure](https://www.f-secure.com/v-descs/trojan_w32_injector.shtml)

name: InnoStealer
description: Threat actors using new malware named "Inno Stealer" in Windows 11 setup scam. Malware not uploaded to Virus Total. Loader file creates temporary files and establishes persistence. [Bleepingcomputer]
additional_info:
According to CloudSEK, the threat actors behind this campaign are using a new malware that researchers named “Inno Stealer” due to its use of the Inno Setup Windows installer.

The researchers say that Inno Stealer doesn’t have any code similarities to commodity other info-stealers currently in circulation and they have not found evidence of the malware being uploaded to the Virus Total scanning platform.

The loader file (Delphi-based) is the “Windows 11 setup” executable contained in the ISO, which, when launched, dumps a temporary file named is-PN131.tmp and creates another .TMP file where the loader writes 3,078KB of data.

CloudSEK explains that the loader spawns a new process using the CreateProcess Windows API the helps spawn new processes, establish persistence, and plant four files.
[Bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/unofficial-windows-11-upgrade-installs-info-stealing-malware/)

name: Insecure legacy protocol - FTP
description: FTP is an old data transfer protocol lacking security, making it vulnerable to MITM attacks. More secure alternatives like SFTP exist, leading to decreased FTP usage, especially among regular users.
additional_info:
File Transfer Protocol (FTP) is one of oldest data transfer protocols still in use. However, it is not designed with security in mind. All data, including authentication password exchange, is visible in cleartext. Thus being vulnerable to Man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks.

More secure alternatives such as SSH File Transfer Protocol (SFTP) exist for use in infrastructure. Moreover, advance of cloud based filesharing platforms have made FTP usage among regular users rare.

In fact, FTP is often used by malicious actors for tool delivery and data exfiltration thanks to its simplicity.

name: Insecure legacy protocol - LLMNR
description: LLMNR is a protocol for local name resolution, used in Windows and Linux. Microsoft is phasing it out for mDNS due to security vulnerabilities like potential interception of broadcast messages.
additional_info:
The Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution (LLMNR) is a protocol based on the Domain Name System (DNS) packet format that allows both IPv4 and IPv6 hosts to perform name resolution for hosts on the same local link. It is included in Windows Vista, Windows Server 2008, Windows 7, Windows 8, Windows 10. It is also implemented by systemd-resolved on Linux. LLMNR is defined in RFC 4795 but was not adopted as an IETF standard.

As of April 2022, Microsoft has begun the process of phasing out both LLMNR and NetBIOS name resolution in favour of mDNS. [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Link-Local_Multicast_Name_Resolution)

It broadcasts over local networks, meaning an attacker could intercept the broadcast messages. The attacker can then respond with spoofed message claiming to be internal resource. Victim system would then attempt to authenticate with spoofed system and in process transmit credential hashes.

name: Interactsh
description: Unit 42 has observed active exploits using Interactsh, an open-source service for testing exploits. It can validate vulnerabilities and monitor domain paths in real-time, used by researchers and attackers.
additional_info:
Unit 42 has observed active exploits related to an open-source service called Interactsh. This tool can generate specific domain names to help its users test whether an exploit is successful. It can be used by researchers – but also by attackers – to validate vulnerabilities via real-time monitoring on the trace path for the domain. Researchers creating a proof of concept (PoC) for an exploit can insert Interactsh to check whether the PoC is working, but the service could also be used by attackers who want to be sure an exploit is working.
[Unit42](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/exploits-interactsh/)

The Invicta Stealer can collect system information, system hardware details, wallet data, and browser data and extract information from applications like Steam and Discord.
[Cyble](https://blog.cyble.com/2023/05/25/invicta-stealer-spreading-through-phony-godaddy-refund-invoices/)

Once they're in, cybercriminals can use a backdoor to steal personal and financial data, install additional malware, and hijack devices.

name: JLORAT
description: JLORAT is a backdoor malware that copies itself under %AppData%, sets up persistence via a registry RUN key, and gathers victim machine information to send to a C2 server on a non-standard port.
additional_info:
JLORAT copies itself under %AppData% and sets up persistence via a registry RUN key. It also creates a mutex to ensure atomic execution (“whatever”, as in the default usage example for the “single-instance” Rust library that is embedded). The backdoor starts by gathering information on the victim machine, such as the system information, current user and public IP address. The information is sent via an HTTP POST request to the C2 on a non-standard port (i.e., 9942). Sample data sent by the C2 could be:
[Securelist](https://securelist.com/tomiris-called-they-want-their-turla-malware-back/109552/)

It uses the .NET (de)serialization feature to interact with a Javascript file which is the core module running on a victim machine. Once the malware is run on the victim, the framework can load several plugins performing additional malicious activities on the target.

name: JokerSpy
description: New macOS malware "JokerSpy" discovered, creating backdoors targeting crypto exchange. Rare for Mac threats, but growing. Bitdefender and Elastic Labs researching. Uses binary "xcc" for Intel and ARM Macs.
additional_info:
A new and strange macOS malware called "JokerSpy" has been identified, with its first known backdoor creation hitting a crypto exchange.
While Mac threats are relatively rare compared to Windows, the number of instances where macOS is the target has continued to grow. In a new discovery, it seems there's one more backdoor-creating malware to add to the list of potential threats.

Initially reported by researchers by Bitdefender with independent research also carried out by Elastic Security Labs, the malware known as JokerSpy is still relatively unknown, in part due to a lack of samples. So far, BitDefender is working on four samples in total, while Eastic focused on the breach of a "prominent Japanese cryptocurrency exchange."

As part of the malware's construction, it uses a binary called "xcc" that contains Mach-O files for x86 Intel and ARM M1 architectures, theoretically allowing it to work on Intel and Apple Silicon Macs. The file checks for permissions managed by Apple's Transparency, Consent, and Control system.

[Apple Insider](https://appleinsider.com/articles/23/06/27/new-mysterious-macos-malware-infiltrates-crypto-exchange)
