name: KHRAT
description: Remote Access Trojans provide covert surveillance and unauthorized access to victim PCs, mimicking keyloggers but allowing attackers control over the victim machine, potentially leading to criminal activities and more.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/remote-access-trojan-rat/)

threat.name: KHRAT - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: KLBanker
description: Info stealers are malware that collect data from infected computers and send it to attackers. They can steal credentials through various methods like keylogging, form grabbing, and hooking browsers.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: KONNI
description: KONNI is a Windows remote administration tool used since 2014, evolving in capabilities through 2017. Linked to North Korean themed campaigns, it shares code with NOKKI malware and potentially APT37.
additional_info:
KONNI is a Windows remote administration too that has been seen in use since 2014 and evolved in its capabilities through at least 2017. KONNI has been linked to several campaigns involving North Korean themes.[1] KONNI has significant code overlap with the NOKKI malware family. There is some evidence potentially linking KONNI to APT37.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0356/)

name: KPOT
description: KPOT Stealer malware exfiltrates account data from a variety of platforms including web browsers, email, and gaming software. It features zero persistence and memory capabilities, making it difficult to detect.
additional_info:
KPOT Stealer is a “stealer” malware that focuses on exfiltrating account information and other data from web browsers, instant messengers, email, VPN, RDP, FTP, cryptocurrency, and gaming software.
[Proofpoint](https://www.proofpoint.com/us/threat-insight/post/new-kpot-v20-stealer-brings-zero-persistence-and-memory-features-silently-steal)

name: Kaiji
description: Kaiji is a DDoS malware written in Go, spreading through SSH brute force attacks. Its recovered function names hint at Chinese origins and it was named by MalwareMustDie. [Malpedia link](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/elf.kaiji)
additional_info: Surfaced in late April 2020, Intezer describes Kaiji as a DDoS malware written in Go that spreads through SSH brute force attacks. Recovered function names are an English representation of Chinese words, hinting about the origin. The name Kaiji was given by MalwareMustDie based on strings found in samples.[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/elf.kaiji)

threat.name: Kaiji - MITRE - Event Triggered Execution: Unix Shell Configuration Modification

name: Kaiten
description: Kaiten is a Trojan horse that creates a backdoor on compromised computers, allowing for additional malicious activities. It does not replicate itself and typically arrives through malware or from visiting malicious sites.
additional_info:
According to netenrich, Kaiten is a Trojan horse that opens a back door on the compromised computer that allows it to perform other malicious activities. The trojan does not create any copies of itself. This Backdoor arrives on a system as a file dropped by other malware or as a file downloaded unknowingly by users when visiting malicious sites.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/elf.kaiten)

name: Kamran
description: Kamran spyware targets Urdu-speaking users in Gilgit-Baltistan region through a fake app that displays legitimate content from Hunza News website while incorporating custom spyware code.
additional_info:
The Kamran spyware, discovered through a possible watering-hole attack on the Hunza News website, specifically targets Urdu-speaking users in the Gilgit-Baltistan region. Operating as a malicious Android app, Kamran displays legitimate content from the news website while incorporating custom spyware code.
[PCrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/28259-kamran-spyware-android)

name: Karagany
description: Trojan.Karagany is a modular remote access tool connected to Dragonfly and Dragonfly 2.0. Its source code came from leaked Dream Loader malware from 2010, sold on underground forums.
additional_info:
Trojan.Karagany is a modular remote access tool used for recon and linked to Dragonfly and Dragonfly 2.0. The source code for Trojan.Karagany originated from Dream Loader malware which was leaked in 2010 and sold on underground forums.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0094/)

name: Karen
description: Ransomware like Karen encrypts files and appends a ".karen" extension to their filenames, along with creating a "README.txt" file with contact information.
additional_info:
Typically, ransomware encrypts files and provides contact information. It is also common that malware of this type renames encrypted files (for example, it appends its extension to their filenames). Karen ransomware creates the "README.txt" text file and appends the ".karen" extension (e.g., it renames "1.jpg" to "1.jpg.karen", "2.jpg" to "2.jpg.karen").
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/21575-karen-ransomware)

threat.name: Karen - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Karen - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Karen - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: Kaseya CVE-2021-30116
description: Kaseya's VSA software is used to monitor and manage software and network infrastructure either through a hosted cloud service or on-premises servers. Recent attacks have impacted around 60 customers.
additional_info:
The VSA software is used by Kaseya customers to remotely monitor and manage software and network infrastructure. It’s supplied either as a hosted cloud service by Kaseya, or via on-premises VSA servers.

The attacks on the VSA .... are now estimated to have led to the encryption of files for around 60 Kaseya customers using the on-premises version of the platform – many of which are managed service providers (MSPs) who use VSA to manage the networks of other businesses.
[Threatpost](https://threatpost.com/kaseya-patches-zero-day-exploits/167548/)

threat.name: Kaseya CVE-2021-30116 - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact

name: Kazy
description: Trojans are malicious software disguised as legitimate programs that allow cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems to spy, steal data, and obtain backdoor access.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

threat.name: Kazy - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
Kazy - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: Kbot
description: KBOT is malicious software that steals passwords, logins, cryptocurrency wallet data, and personal information. Kaspersky detects it as Kpot. Remove it immediately if installed on your system.
additional_info:
KBOT is malicious software that can steal various credentials such as passwords, logins, cryptocurrency wallet data, lists of files and installed programs, banking-related and other personal information. It can cause serious problems for victims. Research shows that Kaspersky solutions detects this malware and associated components as Kpot. If you believe that KBOT (also known as KBOT stealer) is installed on the operating system, you should remove it immediately.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/17033-kbot-trojan)

name: Keitaro
description: Cyber criminals exploited the TDS platform Keitaro to redirect users to malware exploit kits RIG and Fallout, infecting them with malicious software. Legitimate TDS platforms are used for targeted advertising.
additional_info:
Cyber ​​criminals violated the law TDS (Traffic Direction System) platform Keitaro and used it to redirect them users in exploit kits RIG and Fallout in order to infect them with malicious software.

TDS platforms are designed for redirection of users in particular sites. Legitimate TDS platforms, such as Keitaro, are mainly used by individuals and companies that want to advertise services or their products. Platforms drive users to the pages that companies want, targeting specific customers and promoting an ad campaign.
[techbizweb](https://techbizweb.com/abuse-of-legal-tds-platform-to-distribute-malware/)

threat.name: Keitaro - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious Link
Keitaro - MITRE - Exploitation for Client Execution

name: KeyBoy
description: KeyBoy is targeted malware documented on MITRE's website. It has been used in specific campaigns to gather sensitive information and has various functionality that can be detrimental to victims.
additional_info:
KeyBoy is malware that has been used in targeted campaigns again
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0387/)

name: Khaosz
description: A Trojan horse is a type of malware disguised as legitimate software, used by cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems. Users are tricked into loading Trojans which can spy, steal data, and allow backdoor access.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

name: Khepri
description: Khepri is a free and open-source agent and post-exploitation tool written in Golang and C++. It is cross-platform and available on Github.
additional_info:
Free,Open-Source,Cross-platform agent and Post-exploiton tool written in Golang and C++.
[Github](https://github.com/geemion/Khepri)

name: Khonsari
description: Ransomware is a type of malware that blocks access to a system or files until a ransom is paid. Originally, payment was sent by mail, but now it is done with cryptocurrency or credit card.
additional_info:
Ransom malware, or ransomware, is a type of malware that prevents users from accessing their system or personal files and demands ransom payment in order to regain access. The earliest variants of ransomware were developed in the late 1980s, and payment was to be sent via snail mail. Today, ransomware authors order that payment be sent via cryptocurrency or credit card.
[Malwarebytes](https://www.malwarebytes.com/ransomware/)

threat.name: Khonsari - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Khonsari - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Khonsari - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: KillAV
description: Trojan.KillAV is a type of Trojan that can disable antivirus programs, detected by Malwarebytes.
additional_info:
Trojan.KillAV is Malwarebytes’ generic detection name for Trojans that are capable of disabling antivirus (AV) programs.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/detections/trojan-killav/)

name: KillProc
description: Troj/KillProc-D is a Trojan program that terminates anti-virus and security software processes. It drops WINU.EXE in the System folder, creates a service called AMA, and runs on system startup.
additional_info:
Troj/KillProc-D is a Trojan program used to terminate processes related to various anti-virus and security software programs.

Troj/KillProc-D first drops a file to the System folder with the filename WINU.EXE, also detected as Troj/KillProc-D. It then creates a service for this file called AMA and sets it to run on system startup. 
[Sophos](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/threat-center/threat-analyses/viruses-and-spyware/Troj~KillProc-D/detailed-analysis.aspx)

name: Kimsuky
description: Kimsuky, a North Korean threat group since 2013, targets think tanks and nuclear-related entities. Recently expanded to US, Russia, Europe; known for Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power Co. compromise.
additional_info:
Kimsuky is a North Korean-based threat group that has been active since at least September 2013. The group initially focused on targeting Korean think tanks and DPRK/nuclear-related targets, expanding recently to the United States, Russia, and Europe. The group was attributed as the actor behind the Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power Co. compromise.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0094/)

name: Kinsing
description: Kinsing is a Golang-based malware that mines cryptocurrency and spreads to other hosts within the victim's environment.
additional_info:
Kinsing is Golang-based malware that runs a cryptocurrency miner and attempts to spread itself to other hosts in the victim environment.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0599/)

name: Knotweed
description: Microsoft discovered a private-sector actor named KNOTWEED using Windows and Adobe 0-day exploits like CVE-2022-22047 in limited attacks on European and Central American customers, deploying malware called Subzero.
additional_info:
The Microsoft Threat Intelligence Center (MSTIC) and the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) found a private-sector offensive actor (PSOA) using multiple Windows and Adobe 0-day exploits, including one for the recently patched CVE-2022-22047, in limited and targeted attacks against European and Central American customers. The PSOA, which MSTIC tracks as KNOTWEED, developed malware called Subzero which was used in these attacks.
[Microsoft](https://www.microsoft.com/security/blog/2022/07/27/untangling-knotweed-european-private-sector-offensive-actor-using-0-day-exploits/)

name: Koadic
description: Koadic is a publicly available Windows post-exploitation framework for penetration testing, performed through the command-line with options for staging payloads and creating implants using Windows Script Host.
additional_info:
Koadic is a Windows post-exploitation framework and penetration testing tool. Koadic is publicly available on GitHub and the tool is executed via the command-line. Koadic has several options for staging payloads and creating implants. Koadic performs most of its operations using Windows Script Host.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0250/)

name: Kolobko
description: Cisco created ClamAV detections for Win.Exploit.Kolobko-9950675-0 and Win.Backdoor.Kolobko-9950676-0. The exploit was for CVE-2022-24521, a Windows vulnerability patched by Microsoft in April 2022. Cisco did not provide details on the exploit executable.
additional_info:
To help network admins and security professionals detect the malware used in the attack, Cisco created two new ClamAV detections for the backdoor and a Windows exploit used for privilege elevation.

Win.Exploit.Kolobko-9950675-0  
Win.Backdoor.Kolobko-9950676-0 
While Cisco provided some information on the backdoor and how it was used to remotely execute commands, their writeup does not mention any info on the exploit executable that was discovered.

However, according to detections on VirusTotal, the exploit is for CVE-2022-24521, a Windows Common Log File System Driver Elevation of Privilege vulnerability, reported by the NSA and CrowdStrike to Microsoft and patched in April 2022.
[Bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/cisco-hacked-by-yanluowang-ransomware-gang-28gb-allegedly-stolen/)

name: Kovter
description: Kovter Trojan is downloaded by malware, connects to websites to send/receive data, and deletes its original copy.
additional_info:
Kovter is a Trojan that can be downloaded by other malware/grayware/spyware from remote sites. It connects to certain websites to send and receive information. It deletes the initially executed copy of itself.

Source: [TrendMicro](https://helpcenter.trendmicro.com/en-us/article/TMKA-19789)

name: Kraken Stealer
description: Info stealers are a type of malware that collects data from infected computers and sends it to attackers. They use various methods, such as keylogging and form grabbing, and are often part of botnets.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: Kronos
description: Kronos, a banking malware developed in 2014 for $7,000, was a follow-up to the UPAS Kit released in 2012.
additional_info:
Kronos was a type of banking malware first reported in 2014. It was sold for $7,000.
It was developed as a followup to the UPAS Kit which has been released in 2012.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kronos_(malware))

name: KrustyLoader
description: Volexity team found interesting Rust payloads in Ivanti Connect Secure VPN attacks. KrustyLoader malware, written in Rust, has specific checks and detection tools available for extracting indicators.
additional_info:
Rust payloads detected by Volexity team turn out to be pretty interesting Sliver downloaders as they were executed on Ivanti Connect Secure VPN after the exploitation of CVE-2024-21887 and CVE-2023-46805. KrustyLoader – as I dubbed it – performs specific checks in order to run only if conditions are met. The fact that KrustyLoader was developed in Rust brings additional difficulties to obtain a good overview of its behaviour. A script as well as a Yara rule are publicly available to help detection and extraction of indicators.
[Synacktiv](https://www.synacktiv.com/publications/krustyloader-rust-malware-linked-to-ivanti-connectsecure-compromises)

name: Kryptik
description: Trojans in the Kryptik family employ anti-emulation, anti-debugging, and code obfuscation techniques to hinder analysis and detection. Source: Kaspersky Threats.
additional_info:
Malware of this family consists of Trojans that use anti-emulation, anti-debugging, and code obfuscation to prevent their analysis.
[Kaspersky](https://threats.kaspersky.com/en/threat/Trojan.Win32.Kryptik/)

name: Kumquat
description: Loaders are remote access Trojans used by cybercriminals to control compromised computers. They are evolving with features like user-friendly interfaces and self-healing capabilities, making them valuable tools in cyber attacks.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.

[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: Kutaki
description: Cofense describes Kutaki as a data stealer using old techniques to evade sandboxes and debugging. It effectively bypasses unhardened virtual machines and other analysis tools by backdooring legitimate applications.
additional_info:
Cofense characterizes Kutaki as a data stealer that uses old-school techniques to detect sandboxes and debugging. Kutaki however works quite well against unhardened virtual machines and other analysis devices. By backdooring a legitimate application, it can fool unsophisticated detection methodologies.
[Mapedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.kutaki)

name: LALALA Stealer
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data on infected computers and send it to attackers. They use methods like hooking browsers, web injection scripts, form grabbing, keylogging, and stealing saved passwords.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: LDpinch
description: PinchDuke is malware utilized by APT29 between 2008 and 2010 for cyber espionage activities. Full details available on MITRE's website.
additional_info:
PinchDuke is malware that was used by APT29 from 2008 to 2010.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0048/)

name: LOADOUT
description: LOADOUT is a VBScript-based downloader that collects system information and sends it to a C2 server. The C2 server sends GRIFFON, a JavaScript-based downloader, to infect systems further.
additional_info:
LOADOUT is an obfuscated VBScript-based downloader which harvests extensive information from the infected system. The harvested information is then sent to a command-and-control (C2) server. C2 server responses for LOADOUT infections delivered GRIFFON, a JavaScript-based downloader which retrieves additional JavaScript modules using HTTP or DNS and executes them in memory. 
[Madiant](mandiant.com/resources/evolution-of-fin7)

threat.name: LOADOUT - MITRE - Command and Scripting Interpreter: Visual Basic
LOADOUT - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
LOADOUT - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
LOADOUT - MITRE - System Network Configuration Discovery
LOADOUT - MITRE - System Network Configuration Discovery

name: LaZagne
description: LaZagne is an open-source tool for recovering stored passwords on Windows, Linux, and OSX systems. It is focused on Windows and available on GitHub.
additional_info: LaZagne is a post-exploitation, open-source tool used to recover stored passwords on a system. It has modules for Windows, Linux, and OSX, but is mainly focused on Windows systems. LaZagne is publicly available on GitHub.[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0349/)

name: Lambda Ransomware
description: Lambda ransomware encrypts files on a computer with extensions like .doc and .pdf, asking for Bitcoin ransom in exchange for access to data, displaying instructions for contacting the ransomware authors.
additional_info:
Lambda ransomware is a file-encrypting ransomware infection that restricts access to data (documents, images, videos) by encrypting files with the “.[whirmx@gmail.com][ID].lambda” extension. It then attempts to extort money from victims by asking for “ransom”, in the form of the Bitcoin cryptocurrency, in exchange for access to data.

The Lambda ransomware will scan your computer for images, videos, and important productivity documents and files such as .doc, .docx, .xls, .pdf. When these files are detected, the ransomware will encrypt them and change their extension to “.[whirmx@gmail.com][ID].lambda”, so that you are no longer able to be open them.

Once the Lambda ransomware has encrypted the files on your computer, it will display a file that contains the ransom note and instructions on how to contact the authors of this ransomware.
[Malwaretips](https://malwaretips.com/blogs/remove-lambda-ransomware/)

name: LandUpdate808
description: Fake update variants like SocGholish, Clear Fake, Smart Ape, and ClickFix are being tracked by a collaboration introducing the LandUpdate808 Fake Update Variant.
additional_info:
There are a handful of fake update variants. The most popular is SocGholish. We’ve often observed some of the other fake update variants referred to as SocGholish, but we try to make the distinction. Some of the other variants include Clear Fake, and Smart Ape. There’s also a new variant that is being referenced as ClickFix. This collaboration between Casey Kuwada, April Bucaneg, and Aaron Samala introduces the LandUpdate808 Fake Update Variant that we’ve been tracking.
[Malasada](https://malasada.tech/the-landupdate808-fake-update-variant/)

name: Laplas
description: Laplas Clipper is a new clipboard stealer that replaces cryptocurrency wallet addresses with similar-looking ones, providing hackers with more control and insight into their operations than other malware of its kind.
additional_info:
A new clipboard stealer called Laplas Clipper spotted in the wild is using cryptocurrency wallet addresses that look like the address of the victim's intended recipient.
Laplas is different from other malware of the same kind, which are typically just add-ons of info-stealing malware. The new clipper is a feature-rich tool that gives hackers more granular control and better insight into the efficiency of their operations.
[Bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-clipboard-hijacker-replaces-crypto-wallet-addresses-with-lookalikes/)

name: Lateral - Policy Bypass Execution
description: Lateral movement. Organizational Security policy bypass and malware proliferation. Unauthorized service creation, application installation and file transfer.
additional_info: [[MS-SCMR]: RCreateServiceW (Opnum 12)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/openspecs/windows_protocols/ms-scmr/6a8ca926-9477-4dd4-b766-692fab07227e)

name: Lateral Discovery
description: Scanning activity performed from infrastructure different than the known organization's vulnerability scanners.
additional_info: Lateral discovery scan from production servers.

name: Lateral Scanning
description: Adversaries conduct service and network scanning to identify vulnerable software on remote hosts and network devices for exploitation. This is done through port and vulnerability scans using specific tools.
additional_info:
Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of services running on remote hosts and local network infrastructure devices, including those that may be vulnerable to remote software exploitation. Common methods to acquire this information include port and/or vulnerability scans using tools that are brought onto a system.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1046/)

name: Latrodectus
description: Loaders are evolving to include user-friendly features and self-healing capabilities, serving as lightweight remote access Trojans for cybercriminals to control compromised computers or bots.
additional_info:
Loaders, for the most part, have one job: grab malicious executables or payloads from an attacker-controlled server. But that doesn’t mean there isn’t more happening under the hood of some, such as a user-friendly UI, self-healing capabilities, or the equivalent of a retail shop where a botmaster can sell his bots to potential clients.

Loaders are essentially basic remote access Trojans that give an attacker the ability to remotely interact with and control a compromised computer, or bot. While traditionally lightweight (smaller than 50 KB in size) in order to bypass detection by antivirus and other security monitoring technology, loaders evolve, and their viability to cybercriminals remains.

[Flashpoint](https://www.flashpoint-intel.com/blog/malware-loaders-continue-to-evolve-proliferate/)

name: LazyScripter
description: LazyScripter is a threat group targeting the airlines industry since 2018, using open-source toolsets. For more information, visit MITRE's website.
additional_info:
LazyScripter is threat group that has mainly targeted the airlines industry since at least 2018, primarily using open-source toolsets.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0140/)

name: Legion Loader
description: Legion Loader is a malicious program that infects systems with multiple other malware types, including information stealers, backdoors, cryptocurrency stealers, and miners.
additional_info:
Legion Loader is a malicious program designed to infect systems with 2-3 other programs of this type (or more). Research shows that Legion Loader is used to spread information stealers (such as Vidar, Predator the Thief and Raccoon Stealer), backdoors, cryptocurrency stealers and a cryptocurrency miner.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/16646-legion-loader-malware)

name: Lemon Duck
description: Lemon Duck is a malicious software that mines Monero cryptocurrency by exploiting infected devices' resources, potentially causing severe damage. Originating in Asia, it has now spread globally.
additional_info:
Lemon Duck is malicious software. The primary function of this malware is to exploit the infected machine's resources to mine cryptocurrency, specifically Monero (XMR) cryptocurrency. This malicious program severely compromises infected devices and can even damage them permanently. Lemon Duck was first observed being proliferated in Asia (notably, China), however, its reach has spread exponentially.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/17610-lemon-duck-malware)

name: Leonem
description: A Trojan horse is malware that deceives users, spreading through social engineering. It can provide unauthorized access to a computer, steal personal information, delete files, and carry out ransomware attacks.
additional_info:
In computing, a Trojan horse (or simply trojan) is any malware which misleads users of its true intent. The term is derived from the Ancient Greek story of the deceptive Trojan Horse that led to the fall of the city of Troy.

Trojans are generally spread by some form of social engineering, for example where a user is duped into executing an email attachment disguised to appear not suspicious, (e.g., a routine form to be filled in), or by clicking on some fake advertisement on social media or anywhere else. Although their payload can be anything, many modern forms act as a backdoor, contacting a controller which can then have unauthorized access to the affected computer. Trojans may allow an attacker to access users' personal information such as banking information, passwords, or personal identity. It can also delete a user's files or infect other devices connected to the network. Ransomware attacks are often carried out using a trojan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trojan_horse_(computing))

name: Leviathan
description: Leviathan is a Chinese cyber espionage group linked to the MSS, targeting various sectors globally since 2009.
additional_info:
Leviathan is a Chinese state-sponsored cyber espionage group that has been attributed to the Ministry of State Security's (MSS) Hainan State Security Department and an affiliated front company.[1] Active since at least 2009, Leviathan has targeted the following sectors: academia, aerospace/aviation, biomedical, defense industrial base, government, healthcare, manufacturing, maritime, and transportation across the US, Canada, Europe, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0065/)

name: LgoogLoader
description: LgoogLoader is a malware installer that downloads and executes a batch file, AutoIt interpreter, and AutoIt script. It is used for malicious purposes.
additional_info: LgoogLoader is an installer that drops three files: a batch file, an AutoIt interpreter, and an AutoIt script. After downloading, it executes the batch file.[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.lgoogloader)

name: Liberium RAT

name: Likseput

additional_info:

name: Lilith
description: Lilith ransomware encrypts files and demands payment for decryption. It appends a ".lilith" extension to files and creates a ransom message named "Restore_Your_Files.txt" on the desktop.
additional_info:
Lilith is the name of a malicious program categorized as ransomware. Malware within this category is designed to encrypt data and demand payment for the decryption.

When we executed a sample of Lilith on our testing machine, it encrypted files and appended their filenames with a ".lilith" extension. For example, a file originally titled "1.jpg" appeared as "1.jpg.lilith", "2.png" as "2.png.lilith", etc. Afterwards, a ransom-demanding message named "Restore_Your_Files.txt" - was created on the desktop.
[Pcrisk](https://www.pcrisk.com/removal-guides/24292-lilith-ransomware)

additional_info:

Although it is not really an infection, individuals can discover that their web browsers are infused with multiple ads, along with redirects that lead them to suspicious sites online.

PUA:Win32/LoadMoney customizes specific setups on the system like Windows registry as well as internet browser preferences (collections start and homepage to Yahoo). It additionally creates a folder in the Program Files directory and has the capacity to alter Chrome’s protected choices without authorization, along with preventing detection by the internet browser. This actions is regarded to be harmful by safety and security professionals, as well as the only proper means to deal with these undesirable modifications is to get rid of LoadMoney from your device.
[Howtofix](https://howtofix.guide/loadmoney-removal/)

name: LoggerRust
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data from infected computers, such as credentials for online services, using various methods like keylogging and form grabbing. They are often part of botnets.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: LuminosityRAT
description: Remote Access Trojans provide covert surveillance or unauthorized access to victim PCs, mimicking keyloggers but allowing remote access. Attackers can monitor behavior, change settings, copy files, and more.
additional_info:
Remote Access Trojans are programs that provide the capability to allow covert surveillance or the ability to gain unauthorized access to a victim PC. Remote Access Trojans often mimic similar behaviors of keylogger applications by allowing the automated collection of keystrokes, usernames, passwords, screenshots, browser history, emails, chat lots, etc. Remote Access Trojans differ from keyloggers in that they provide the capability for an attacker to gain unauthorized remote access to the victim machine via specially configured communication protocols which are set up upon initial infection of the victim computer.  This backdoor into the victim machine can allow an attacker unfettered access, including the ability to monitor user behavior, change computer settings, browse and copy files, utilize the bandwidth (Internet connection) for possible criminal activity, access connected systems, and more.
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/remote-access-trojan-rat/)

name: Lyceum
description: CTU researchers identified the COBALT LYCEUM threat group in 2019, linked to Iran, targeting critical infrastructure organizations with unsophisticated malware. Similar tactics to COBALT GYPSY, including the use of RGDoor backdoor.
additional_info:
CTU researchers discovered the COBALT LYCEUM threat group in mid-2019 and determined that it has been active since at least 2018. The group is assessed with moderate confidence to operate on behalf of Iran, with a relatively small scope of operations in comparison to other Iranian groups. Known targets include critical infrastructure organizations, such as telecommunications and oil and gas companies. The threat actors use malicious Excel files with the DanDrop macro to deliver the unsophisticated DanBot first-stage malware, which deploys post-intrusion tools taken from public code repositories. A mid-2018 COBALT LYCEUM campaign focused on South African targets. In February 2019, the threat actors shifted their focus to Kuwait following a period of testing and development. COBALT LYCEUM’s targeting, tactics, and development style are similar to those of COBALT GYPSY along with the use of RGDoor, an IIS backdoor previously only associated with COBALT GYPSY. Document metadata anomalies suggest that the malware developer may work natively in an Arabic or Persian script.
[Secureworks](https://www.secureworks.com/research/threat-profiles/cobalt-lyceum)

name: MACE
description: MACE is a publicly available C2 Framework written in Python and can be found on Github.
additional_info:

[Github](https://github.com/nickvangilder/most-average-c2-ever/)

name: MASSLOGGER
description: MassLogger is a.NET credential stealer with a launcher using simple anti-debugging techniques. The first loader decrypts the second stage assembly, which executes the final payload.
additional_info:
MassLogger is a .NET credential stealer. It starts with a launcher that uses simple anti-debugging techniques which can be easily bypassed when identified. This first stage loader eventually XOR-decrypts the second stage assembly which then decrypts, loads and executes the final MassLogger payload.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.masslogger)

additional_info:
In Jan 2021, Zscaler ThreatLabZ discovered new instances of the MINEBRIDGE remote-access Trojan (RAT) embedded in macro-based Word document files crafted to look like valid job resumes (CVs). Such lures are used as social engineering schemes by threat actors; in this case, the malware was targeted at security researchers.
[MINEBRIDGE](https://www.zscaler.com/blogs/security-research/return-minebridge-rat-new-ttps-and-social-engineering-lures)

name: Maggie
description: Malicious malware disguised as "Extended Stored Procedure" DLL for Microsoft SQL servers allows attackers to control servers through SQL queries, run commands, interact with files, and act as a network bridge head.
additional_info:

additional_info:

Lazarus deployed MagicRAT after the successful exploitation of vulnerabilities in VMWare Horizon platforms.

name: Magnet Goblin APT
description: Magnet Goblin exploits 1-day vulnerabilities in public-facing services like Ivanti Connect Secure VPN, using tools like NerbianRAT and WARPWIRE for financially motivated attacks targeting multiple platforms.
additional_info:
Magnet Goblin is a financially motivated threat actor that quickly adopts and leverages 1-day vulnerabilities in public-facing services as an initial infection vector. At least in one case of Ivanti Connect Secure VPN (CVE-2024-21887), the exploit entered the group’s arsenal as fast as within 1 day after a POC for it was published.
Campaigns that we were able to attribute to this actor targeted Ivanti, Magento, Qlink Sense and possibly Apache ActiveMQ.
Analysis of the actor’s recent Ivanti Connect Secure VPN campaign revealed a novel Linux version of a malware called NerbianRAT, in addition to WARPWIRE, a JavaScript credential stealer.
The actor’s arsenal also includes MiniNerbian, a small Linux backdoor, and remote monitoring and management (RMM) tools for Windows like ScreenConnect and AnyDesk.
[Checkpoint](https://research.checkpoint.com/2024/magnet-goblin-targets-publicly-facing-servers-using-1-day-vulnerabilities/)

description: An exploit kit is a tool that simplifies the use of multiple exploits for users with limited technical knowledge by providing a collection of pre-installed exploits and allowing users to add their own.
additional_info:
An exploit kit is simply a collection of exploits, which is a simple one-in-all tool for managing a variety of exploits altogether. Exploit kits act as a kind of repository, and make it easy for users without much technical knowledge to use exploits. Users can add their own exploits to it and use them simultaneously apart from the pre-installed ones.
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exploit_kit)

name: MalDoc
description: Adversaries trick users into opening malicious files through social engineering, most commonly seen after Spearphishing Attachment. Various file types are used, with masquerading to increase chances of success.
additional_info:
An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution. Users may be subjected to social engineering to get them to open a file that will lead to code execution. This user action will typically be observed as follow-on behavior from Spearphishing Attachment. Adversaries may use several types of files that require a user to execute them, including .doc, .pdf, .xls, .rtf, .scr, .exe, .lnk, .pif, and .cpl.

Adversaries may employ various forms of Masquerading on the file to increase the likelihood that a user will open it.

While Malicious File frequently occurs shortly after Initial Access it may occur at other phases of an intrusion, such as when an adversary places a file in a shared directory or on a user's desktop hoping that a user will click on it. This activity may also be seen shortly after Internal Spearphishing.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204/002/)

threat.name: MalDoc - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
MalDoc - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious Link
MalDoc - MITRE - Exploitation for Client Execution

name: Malicious Chrome Extension
description: Kaspersky found over 1.3 million users were affected by threats in browser extensions in the first half of 2022, mimicking popular apps and collecting user data, making them appealing to cybercriminals.
additional_info:
Kaspersky researchers have analyzed the risks innocent-looking browser extensions pose to users, finding that, in the first half of 2022, more than 1.3 million users were affected by threats hiding in browser extensions, at least once. That number is over 70% of the total number of users affected throughout all of 2021.

Mimicking popular apps, such as Google Translate or extensions with useful functionality like PDF Converter or Video Downloader, threats in browser extensions can insert advertisements, collect data about users’ browsing histories and even search for login credentials, making it one of the most desirable tools for cybercriminals. [Kaspersky](https://usa.kaspersky.com/about/press-releases/2022_13-million-users-encountered-browser-extension-threats-in-the-first-half-of-2022?cjdata=MXxZfDB8WXww&CJEVENT=13bf97fe6e5811ee83c58e580a18b8f8)

name: Malicious DGA Domain
description: Malicious domains are used by threat actors for malware spreading and phishing. C2 servers are established worldwide to evade detection. DGA generates domain names for command and control, challenging security professionals. Stamus Networks offers specialized detection methods.
additional_info:
A malicious domain is a website or domain that has been created or used with the intent of spreading malware, stealing sensitive information, facilitating phishing attacks, or other malicious activities. These domains are typically registered by threat actors and embedded into malware. 

Threat actors often establish multiple C2 servers to maintain redundancy and evade detection by security solutions. These servers can be located anywhere in the world, making it difficult for cybersecurity professionals to trace and disrupt malicious activities. C2 servers can be hosted on legitimate websites, compromised servers, cloud services, or even on peer-to-peer networks to make detection more challenging. Malicious IP addresses can easily be blocked with firewalls, effectively disabling callbacks. By using domain names instead, a malicious actor can simply point the record to a new IP, effectively ignoring the block.

Domain Generator Algorithm (DGA) is a technique used by threat actors to dynamically generate a large number of domain names for command and control communication with malware-infected devices. This helps attackers evade detection and mitigation efforts by security tools and researchers.

One of the challenges in defending against DGA-based malware is that security professionals must be able to detect and block new, unseen domain names in real-time. This requires advanced threat intelligence capabilities and dynamic analysis of network traffic to identify suspicious patterns that may indicate DGA activity.

Stamus Networks has developed specialized detection methods to identify such domains.

name: Malicious Executable
description: Adversaries create malware components like payloads, backdoors, and C2 protocols to support operations, gain control of remote machines, and evade defenses. This allows for executing post-compromise actions.
additional_info: Adversaries may develop malware and malware components that can be used during targeting. Building malicious software can include the development of payloads, droppers, post-compromise tools, backdoors (including backdoored images), packers, C2 protocols, and the creation of infected removable media. Adversaries may develop malware to support their operations, creating a means for maintaining control of remote machines, evading defenses, and executing post-compromise behaviors. [MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1587/001/)

name: Malicious PyPI
description: Beware of malicious packages in PyPI, the Python Package Index. Take caution when downloading to avoid security threats.
additional_info:
Malicious packages present in The Python Package Index (PyPI)
[Malicious PyPI](twitter.com/pypi/status/1562442207079976966)

name: Malicious Pyinstaller
description: PyInstaller creates a standalone package for Python apps, including all dependencies. However, it can also be exploited by malicious actors to distribute malware.
additional_info:
PyInstaller bundles a Python application and all its dependencies into a single package. The user can run the packaged app without installing a Python interpreter or any modules. [PyInstaller](https://pyinstaller.org/en/stable/)

Like any other legitimate software deployment tool, malicious parties can use them to deliver malware.

name: Mallox
description: The ransomware called "Mallox" (also known as "TargetCompany") gained attention in June 2021 for appending the target company's name as a file extension. Cyble reported a rise in Mallox samples.
additional_info:
The ransomware known as “TargetCompany,” which first appeared in June 2021, gained significant attention due to its unique method of appending the name of the targeted company as a file extension to encrypted files. This ransomware variant was also observed appending a “.mallox” extension to encrypted files, leading to its previous identification as “Mallox”. Last year, Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs (CRIL) also reported a significant rise in the Mallox ransomware samples.
[Cyble](https://blog.cyble.com/2023/06/22/mallox-ransomware-implements-new-infection-strategy/)

name: Malvertising Domain
description: MS-ISAC noticed rise in malware spread through malvertising, where malicious ads contain harmful code. Ad networks unwittingly display infected ads on websites, putting users at risk of infection.
additional_info:
The MS-ISAC has recently observed an increase in malware that is most often disseminated through malvertising. Malvertising, or malicious advertising, is the use of online, malicious advertisements to spread malware and compromise systems. Generally this occurs through the injection of unwanted or malicious code into ads. Malicious actors then pay legitimate online advertising networks to display the infected ads on various websites, exposing every user visiting these sites to the potential risk of infection. Generally, the legitimate advertising networks and websites are not aware they are serving malicious content.
[CIS](https://www.cisecurity.org/insights/blog/malvertising)

name: Mandrake
description: Mandrake is a sophisticated Android espionage platform that has been active since 2016, maintaining stealth by posing as legitimate apps until operators issue a specific command for activation.
additional_info:
Mandrake is a sophisticated Android espionage platform that has been active in the wild since at least 2016. Mandrake is very actively maintained, with sophisticated features and attacks that are executed with surgical precision.

Mandrake has gone undetected for several years by providing legitimate, ad-free applications with social media and real reviews to back the apps. The malware is only activated when the operators issue a specific command.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0485/)

name: Manjusaka
description: Cisco Talos analyzed the RAT Manjusaka, which was written in Rust and compared it to Cobalt Strike and Sliver. More information on the analysis can be found on Malpedia.
additional_info:
Cisco Talos compared this RAT to Cobalt Strike and Sliver. Written in Rust.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.manjusaka)

name: Marcher
description: Malware family requests admin rights, hides in app list, intercepts personal data, redirects calls to cybercriminal numbers. Replaces bank app windows with phishing ones to steal banking information.
additional_info:
Malicious programs of this family request administrator rights and then make themselves invisible in the list of installed apps. This malware can intercept the user’s personal data, such as SMS messages, MMS messages, and USSD requests. The program can redirect incoming calls to the phone numbers of cybercriminals. Phone numbers, the texts of the messages to be intercepted, and cybercriminal phone numbers for redirecting calls are downloaded from the command-and-control server.

Programs of this family interfere with bank apps, such as the Commerzbank app or Google Play. When the user tries to open one of these legitimate apps, the malware replaces the genuine app window with a phishing window that asks for banking information. The user’s stolen data is sent to the cybercriminals.
[Kaspersky](https://threats.kaspersky.com/en/threat/Trojan-Banker.AndroidOS.Marcher/)

name: MarkiRAT
description: Kaspersky discovers cyberespionage campaign targeting Persian-speaking individuals in Iran by group known as Ferocious Kitten using custom malware called MarkiRAT. Malware can steal data and control victim's machine.
additional_info:
Kaspersky has uncovered a long-standing cyberespionage campaign against Persian-speaking individuals in Iran. The group behind the malicious activity—dubbed Ferocious Kitten—has been active since at least 2015 and delivers a custom malware called “MarkiRAT” that steals data and can execute commands on the victim’s machine. The malware also has variants that can hijack the infected user’s Chrome browser and their Telegram app.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/about/press-releases/2021_a-6-year-cyberespionage-campaign-uncovered-in-the-middle-east)

name: Masson
description: A Trojan downloader is a form of malware that masquerades as legitimate software to install harmful programs on a device without the user's consent, aiming to disrupt or steal data.
additional_info:
A Trojan downloader is a type of Trojan horse that downloads and installs files, often malicious programs. A Trojan horse is a type of software that looks legitimate but can be malicious in nature. Sometimes these programs can be downloaded onto a device without the user’s knowledge or consent. A Trojan’s purpose is to damage, disrupt, steal, or generally inflict some other harm on your computer and devices.
[Norton](https://us.norton.com/internetsecurity-malware-what-is-a-trojan-downloader.html)

name: MataDoor
description: Metador targets telecom, ISPs, and universities in the Middle East and Africa with sophisticated attacks that bypass security measures and deploy malware directly into memory, including Windows and Linux variants.
additional_info:
Metador primarily targets telecommunications, internet service providers, and universities in several countries in the Middle East and Africa.
The operators are highly aware of operations security, managing carefully segmented infrastructure per victim, and quickly deploying intricate countermeasures in the presence of security solutions.
Metador’s attack chains are designed to bypass native security solutions while deploying malware platforms directly into memory. SentinelLabs researchers discovered variants of two long-standing Windows malware platforms, and indications of an additional Linux implant.
[Sentinelone](https://www.sentinelone.com/labs/the-mystery-of-metador-an-unattributed-threat-hiding-in-telcos-isps-and-universities/)

name: Matanbuchus
description: BelialDemon launched Matanbuchus Loader, a malware-as-a-service for $2,500, to distribute second-stage malware. Malware loaders download malicious software from command and control servers.
additional_info:
In February 2021, BelialDemon advertised a new malware-as-a-service (MaaS) called Matanbuchus Loader and charged an initial rental price of $2,500. Malware loaders are malicious software that typically drop or pull down second-stage malware from command and control (C2) infrastructures.
[Unit42](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/matanbuchus-malware-as-a-service/)

threat.name: Matanbuchus - MITRE - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism

name: Maze
description: Maze ransomware, also known as "ChaCha," was found in May 2019. It not only encrypts files but also steals data from victims and threatens to publish it online to extort companies.
additional_info:
Maze ransomware, previously known as "ChaCha", was discovered in May 2019. In addition to encrypting files on victim machines for impact, Maze operators conduct information stealing campaigns prior to encryption and post the information online to extort affected companies.
[Maze](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0449/)

threat.name: Maze - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Maze - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File
Maze - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact

name: Medusa
description: Medusa is a DDoS bot using .NET 2.0 with an HTTP-based C&C protocol, unlike its predecessor which used IRC. [Malpedia]
additional_info:
HTTP variant
Medusa is a DDoS bot written in .NET 2.0. In its current incarnation its C&C protocol is based on HTTP, while its predecessor made use of IRC.
[Malpedia](https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/details/win.medusa)

name: Medusa Stealer
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data such as online banking credentials, social media logins, and emails to send to attackers. They use various methods like keylogging, form grabbing, and browser hooking.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

name: MegalodonHTTP
description: MegalodonHTTP is a RAT used by attackers to take over machines and steal data. It is not sophisticated and requires the .Net framework on the victim's machine.
additional_info:
MegalodonHTTP is a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) that could be used by attackers to take over victims’s machines and exfiltrate sensitive data. According to the experts, the MegalodonHTTP is not a sophisticated threat, among the major flaws the need for the presence of the .Net framework on the infected machine.
[Securityaffairs](https://securityaffairs.co/wordpress/43625/cyber-crime/megalodonhttp-botnet-takedown.html)

name: Mekotio
description: Mekotio is a Latin American banking trojan active since 2015, using fake pop-up windows to steal sensitive information from victims of Latin American banks and financial institutions.
additional_info:
Mekotio is a typical Latin American banking trojan that has been active since at least 2015. As such, it attacks by displaying fake pop-up windows to its victims, trying to entice them to divulge sensitive information. These windows are carefully designed to target Latin American banks and other financial institutions.
[welivesecurity](https://www.welivesecurity.com/2020/08/13/mekotio-these-arent-the-security-updates-youre-looking-for/)

name: Merlin Command and Control framework
description: Merlin is a post-exploit Command & Control tool that uses HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 protocols, with HTTP/3 being a combination of HTTP/2 over the QUIC protocol. Read more [here](https://merlin-c2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).
additional_info: Merlin is a post-exploit Command & Control (C2) tool, also known as a Remote Access Tool (RAT), that communicates using the HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 protocols. HTTP/3 is the combination of HTTP/2 over the Quick UDP Internet Connections (QUIC) protocol. [Merlin Command and Control framework](https://merlin-c2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)

name: Mespinoza
description: Ransomware gangs are evolving tactics to demand larger ransoms, appearing more professional. Mespinoza ransomware group uses quirky names for tools, showing increased sophistication. [Paloalto]
additional_info:
As cyber extortion flourishes, ransomware gangs are constantly changing tactics and business models to increase the chances that victims will pay increasingly large ransoms. As these criminal organizations become more sophisticated, they are increasingly taking on the appearance of professional enterprises. One good example is Mespinoza ransomware, which is run by a prolific group with a penchant for using whimsical terms to name its hacking tools.
[Paloalto](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/gasket-and-magicsocks-tools-install-mespinoza-ransomware/)

threat.name: Mespinoza - MITRE - Data Encrypted for Impact
Mespinoza - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery
Mespinoza - MITRE - User Execution: Malicious File

name: MetaStealer
description: Info stealers are malware that gather data from infected computers to send to attackers. They use various methods like browser hooking, web injection, form grabbing, keylogging, and stealing saved passwords.
additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).
[Malwarebytes](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threats/info-stealers/)

threat.name: MetaStealer - MITRE - Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
MetaStealer - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: Metador
description: Metador is a sophisticated cyber attack group targeting telecom, ISPs, and universities in the Middle East and Africa. They are adept at evading security measures and deploying countermeasures swiftly.
additional_info:
Metador primarily targets telecommunications, internet service providers, and
universities in several countries in the Middle East and Africa.
The operators are highly aware of operations security, managing carefully segmented
infrastructure per victim, and quickly deploying intricate countermeasures in the
presence of security solutions.
[Sentinelone](https://assets.sentinelone.com/sentinellabs22/metador)

name: Metamorfo
description: Metamorfo is a Latin-American banking trojan operated by a Brazilian cybercrime group targeting banks and cryptocurrency services in Brazil and Mexico since April 2018.
additional_info:
Metamorfo is a Latin-American banking trojan operated by a Brazilian cybercrime group that has been active since at least April 2018. The group focuses on targeting banks and cryptocurrency services in Brazil and Mexico.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0455/)

name: Metasploit
description: The Metasploit Project is a computer security project owned by Rapid7, providing information on security vulnerabilities, penetration testing tools, and the open-source Metasploit Framework for exploit development.
additional_info:
The Metasploit Project is a computer security project that provides information about security vulnerabilities and aids in penetration testing and IDS signature development. It is owned by Boston, Massachusetts-based security company Rapid7.

Its best-known sub-project is the open-source Metasploit Framework, a tool for developing and executing exploit code against a remote target machine. Other important sub-projects include the Opcode Database, shellcode archive and related research.

The Metasploit Project includes anti-forensic and evasion tools, some of which are built into the Metasploit Framework. Metasploit is pre-installed in the Kali Linux operating system.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metasploit)

name: Meterpreter
description: Meterpreter is an advanced payload for Metasploit with features like command history, tab completion, and a Ruby API. Originally written by skape, it is now being overhauled for Metasploit 3.3.
additional_info:
Meterpreter is an advanced, dynamically extensible payload that uses in-memory DLL injection stagers and is extended over the network at runtime. It communicates over the stager socket and provides a comprehensive client-side Ruby API. It features command history, tab completion, channels, and more.

Metepreter was originally written by skape for Metasploit 2.x, common extensions were merged for 3.x and is currently undergoing an overhaul for Metasploit 3.3. The server portion is implemented in plain C and is now compiled with MSVC, making it somewhat portable. The client can be written in any language but Metasploit has a full-featured Ruby client API.
[OffensiveSecurity](https://www.offensive-security.com/metasploit-unleashed/about-meterpreter/)

name: Microcin
description: Trojans are disguised malware used by cyber-thieves to gain access to users' systems. They are activated through social engineering and allow criminals to spy, steal data, and gain backdoor access.
additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.
[Kaspersky](https://www.kaspersky.com/resource-center/threats/trojans)

threat.name: Microcin - MITRE - File and Directory Discovery

name: Micropsia
description: Micropsia is a Delphi-based remote access tool. It has various capabilities for accessing and controlling remote systems. For more information, visit MITRE's page at the provided link.
additional_info:
Micropsia is a remote access tool written in Delphi.
[MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0339/)

name: Mimikatz
description: Mimikatz, originally a hobby project, is a widely-used tool for extracting passwords, hash, PIN code, and more from memory. It can also perform pass-the-hash and pass-the-ticket attacks.
additional_info:
Mimikatz is a tool that started life as hobby project that experimented with Windows security. 

It's now well known and freely available post-exploitation tool for extracting plaintexts passwords, hash, PIN code and kerberos tickets from memory. Mimikatz can also perform pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket or build Golden tickets.
[Github](https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz/)

name: MiniDuke
description: MiniDuke is malware used by APT29 from 2010 to 2015. It includes downloader and backdoor components, and has been used with CosmicDuke and PinchDuke.
additional_info: MiniDuke is malware that was used by APT29 from 2010 to 2015. The MiniDuke toolset consists of multiple downloader and backdoor components. The loader has been used with other MiniDuke components as well as in conjunction with CosmicDuke and PinchDuke. [MITRE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0051/)

name: MiniMoose

additional_info:
A Trojan horse or Trojan is a type of malware that is often disguised as legitimate software. Trojans can be employed by cyber-thieves and hackers trying to gain access to users' systems. Users are typically tricked by some form of social engineering into loading and executing Trojans on their systems. Once activated, Trojans can enable cyber-criminals to spy on you, steal your sensitive data, and gain backdoor access to your system.

additional_info:
The term info stealer is self-explanatory. This type of malware resides in an infected computer and gathers data in order to send it to the attacker. Typical targets are credentials used in online banking services, social media sites, emails, or FTP accounts.

Info stealers may use many methods of data acquisition. The most common are:

hooking browsers (and sometimes other applications) and stealing credentials that are typed by the user
using web injection scripts that are adding extra fields to web forms and submitting information from them to a server owned by the attacker
form grabbing (finding specific opened windows and stealing their content)
keylogging
stealing passwords saved in the system and cookies
Modern info stealers are usually parts of botnets. Sometimes the target of attack and related events are configured remotely by the command sent from the Command and Control server (C&C).

name: Mints Loader
description: MintsLoader is a malware that spreads through phishing emails targeting European organizations. It uses JavaScript and PowerShell in a multi-step infection process involving URLs with .top TLD and domain generation algorithm (DGA).
additional_info:
MintsLoader primarily delivers malicious RAT or infostealing payloads such as AsyncRAT and Vidar through phishing emails, targeting organizations in Europe (Spain, Italy, Poland, etc.). Written in JavaScript and PowerShell, MintsLoader operates through a multi-step infection process involving several URLs and domains, most of which use a domain generation algorithm (DGA) with .top TLD.  
[Orange](https://github.com/cert-orangecyberdefense/mintsloader)

name: Mirai
description: Mirai is malware that infects Linux devices to create a botnet for large-scale attacks. It targets online consumer devices like IP cameras and routers, leading to major DDoS attacks.
additional_info:
Mirai (Japanese: 未来, lit. 'future') is a malware that turns networked devices running Linux into remotely controlled bots that can be used as part of a botnet in large-scale network attacks. It primarily targets online consumer devices such as IP cameras and home routers. The Mirai botnet was first found in August 2016 by MalwareMustDie, a white hat malware research group, and has been used in some of the largest and most disruptive distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks, including an attack on 20 September 2016 on computer security journalist Brian Krebs' web site, an attack on French web host OVH, and the October 2016 Dyn cyberattack. According to a chat log between Anna-senpai and Robert Coelho, Mirai was named after the 2011 TV anime series Mirai Nikki.

Source: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mirai_(malware))

threat.name: Mirai - MITRE - Network Denial of Service

additional_info:
A Trojan downloader is a type of Trojan horse that downloads and installs files, often malicious programs. A Trojan horse is a type of software that looks legitimate but can be malicious in nature. Sometimes these programs can be downloaded onto a device without the user’s knowledge or consent. A Trojan’s purpose is to damage, disrupt, steal, or generally inflict some other harm on your computer and devices.
[Norton](https://us.norton.com/internetsecurity-malware-what-is-a-trojan-downloader.html)
